findings.title: JWTSigningService.create_access_token unconditionally raises — entire signing service is a non-functional stub exported as public API
findings.detail: create_access_token() raises RuntimeError('JWT token creation requires EntityIdentity repository...') at line 112 before ever building a payload. All code after it (lines 118-157: payload construction, header/payload base64 encoding, signing) is unreachable dead code — `payload` is never assigned, so the additional_claims block at 139 would also NameError if reached. create_client_credentials_token() (line 260) calls create_access_token() so it is equally broken. JWTSigningService is exported from security/__init__.py (line 515) and re-exported through 4 package __init__ files as public API, yet grep shows it is never instantiated anywhere in production — a fully dead, never-constructed class advertised as a usable JWT signer.

findings.title: JWTSigningService.verify_access_token selects the verification algorithm from the token's own `alg` header (algorithm confusion)
findings.detail: verify_access_token() reads alg_name = header.get('alg') (line 201) and derives the verification algorithm via _get_algorithm_from_jwt_alg(alg_name) (line 205), then verifies with whatever algorithm the attacker-supplied token header names. This directly violates the catalog invariant 'Algorithm must be an explicit JWTAlgorithm passed by the caller; never inferred from the token header... no path lets the token's own alg select the verification algorithm or key type.' The caller never pins the algorithm, so a token can dictate ES256-vs-PS256 selection against the supplied key. Because _verify_signature dispatches on the key object type rather than the claimed alg the classic RSA/HMAC confusion is partly mitigated, but the algorithm is still attacker-controlled and the method is exported public API contradicting the codec's stated single-codec, caller-dictated-algorithm discipline.

findings.title: HashingService.verify_hash uses non-constant-time `==` for hash comparison
findings.detail: verify_hash() compares computed_hash == hash_value at line 38 with Python's byte `==`, which short-circuits and is not constant-time. For a public hashing service used to verify integrity/MACs this leaks timing about the matching prefix length; hmac.compare_digest / constant-time comparison should be used. Low because plain unkeyed SHA-256 verification of public data is not itself a secret-comparison, but the API is generic and invites use for digest/tag comparison where timing matters.

findings.title: ECIES encrypt substitutes a 0x00 placeholder for empty plaintext, silently corrupting the encrypt/decrypt contract
findings.detail: _ecies_encrypt returns ciphertext=ciphertext if ciphertext else b'\x00' (line 207): when plaintext is empty the real (authenticated-empty) ciphertext is replaced with a literal b'\x00'. _ecies_decrypt then special-cases plaintext == b'\x00' and len(result.ciphertext)==1 to reconstruct b'' (line 251). This out-of-band sentinel breaks AEAD integrity for the empty-message case (the substituted 0x00 is not the GCM output and is not authenticated against the real tag) and is brittle: any legitimate 1-byte 0x00 plaintext round-trips to b''. A reviewer-visible correctness/integrity defect in an exported encryption primitive.

findings.title: JWTSigningService derives service-account subject UUID from MD5
findings.detail: create_client_credentials_token() builds the service-account subject as UUID(bytes=hashlib.md5(f'service:{client_id_str}'.encode()).digest()) at line 280. MD5 is collision-prone; deriving a security-relevant subject identifier from it risks two distinct client_ids mapping to the same sub. Info-only severity because the enclosing method is unreachable (create_access_token raises) and the class is never instantiated, but it is a flagged crypto smell in exported code.

findings.title: SecureRandomService.validate_entropy uses a heuristic unique-char ratio that passes low-entropy strings and is unused
findings.detail: validate_entropy() estimates entropy as math.log2(unique_chars) * total_chars * entropy_ratio (line 290) — a length-dominated heuristic with no relation to real min-entropy. A long repetitive string (e.g. 'abababab...') can exceed minimum_bits, so the function can return True for low-entropy data, and conversely short high-entropy tokens may fail. It is advertised as 'entropy verification and quality assurance' but provides no cryptographic guarantee. No production caller invokes it (only the secrets-based generators are used), so this is a dead/misleading-validation hygiene issue rather than an exploited path.

findings.title: generate_oauth_state_parameter truncates base64url to char-length, weakening entropy for the documented size
findings.detail: generate_oauth_state_parameter(length=32) generates `length` random bytes then base64url-encodes and truncates to `length` characters: base64.urlsafe_b64encode(token_bytes(length)).rstrip('=')[:length] (lines 160-162). For length=32 it produces 32 base64url chars (~192 bits) from 32 bytes — acceptable — but truncating encoded output to a character count is an entropy-accounting hazard: the returned char count is conflated with byte count and the minimum guard (>=16, line 154) is in characters not bytes, so a caller requesting length=16 gets a 16-char (~96-bit) state. The catalog requires state to be 'cryptographically random' CSRF protection; the by-character sizing under-specifies the security parameter. Low: still above trivial brute-force at defaults.

findings.title: JWTSigningService.verify_access_token selects the verification algorithm from the token's own alg header (algorithm confusion)
findings.detail: verify_access_token reads alg_name = header.get("alg") (line 201) and passes it to _get_algorithm_from_jwt_alg(alg_name) (line 205), then _verify_signature uses that attacker-controlled algorithm to pick the hash (e.g. ES256 vs ES384/ES512). The NORMATIVE CATALOG mandates the verifier dictates the algorithm and that 'no path lets the token's own alg select the verification algorithm or key type.' Here the token header chooses the algorithm; the caller passes only a key object, never an expected algorithm. This is the precise algorithm-confusion shape the catalog forbids. The verifier should require an explicit caller-supplied algorithm and reject a header alg that does not match.

findings.title: JWTSigningService.create_access_token is unconditionally broken (raises before payload defined; dead code references undefined name)
findings.detail: create_access_token always raises RuntimeError at line 112 ('JWT token creation requires EntityIdentity repository...') before any payload is built. The code after it (line 139 'reserved_claims = set(payload.keys())' and line 144 'payload.update(...)') references payload which is never assigned, so the method is permanently non-functional yet is a public exported API. create_client_credentials_token (line 260) calls create_access_token and is therefore also dead. This is a stub-by-exception masquerading as an implemented method; per project rules a class should not ship methods that only raise.

findings.title: Hand-rolled parallel JWT codec violates single-codec discipline
findings.detail: This file performs raw JWT construction/parsing — base64url of json.dumps(header/payload) (lines 147-148, 193-194), manual three-part split and signature verify (lines 186-214), and claim dicts — entirely outside security/adapters/jwt/jwt_codec.py. The catalog's single-codec invariant states 'import jwt (PyJWT), base64, and raw claim dicts appear ONLY in jwt_codec.py'. JWTSigningService is a second, divergent JWT verify/mint path (publicly exported via security/__init__.py line 515/1150) that re-implements signature and claim verification with weaker rules than JWTValidator/JWTCodec, defeating the centralization the catalog relies on for fail-closed classification.

findings.title: verify_access_token enforces no leeway/clock-skew discipline and re-implements claim checks divergently from the canonical validator
findings.detail: verify_access_token hand-checks exp (line 221 'now >= exp'), nbf, iss, aud, and scope independently of the canonical JWTValidator/IValidatedTokenClaims path. Audience is compared by strict equality token_aud != audience (line 236), so a token whose aud is a JSON array (RFC 7519 permits aud as a list) would be rejected/mishandled, and there is no issuer/audience typing. Because this duplicates trust-bearing claim validation outside the single codec, its rules can (and here do) drift from the catalog's normative verify semantics.

findings.title: ECIES empty-plaintext sentinel collides with a legitimate single 0x00 byte plaintext
findings.detail: _ecies_encrypt stores ciphertext as 'ciphertext if ciphertext else b"\x00"' (line 207) and _ecies_decrypt reverses it with 'if plaintext == b"\x00" and len(result.ciphertext) == 1: plaintext = b""' (lines 251-252). A caller that legitimately encrypts the one-byte plaintext b"\x00" will, on decrypt, have it silently rewritten to b"" (empty). AEAD authenticates correctly but the value-object layer corrupts a valid payload. The empty-plaintext case should be encoded out-of-band (e.g. a length field), not by overloading a real plaintext value.

findings.title: HashingService.verify_hash uses non-constant-time == comparison
findings.detail: verify_hash computes the digest and compares with 'computed_hash == hash_value' (line 38), a non-constant-time byte comparison. While plain hash equality is usually not secret-dependent, when this primitive is used to verify integrity tags or attacker-supplied expected digests it can leak via timing; the codebase elsewhere mandates constant-time comparison for security-relevant equality (e.g. OAuthState.matches). Use hmac.compare_digest for hash verification to stay consistent with the constant-time invariant.

findings.title: create_client_credentials_token derives a service identity with MD5
findings.detail: hashlib.md5(f"service:{client_id_str}".encode()).digest() (line 280) is used to derive a deterministic service-account UUID. MD5 is a broken hash; even for non-confidential identifier derivation it is forbidden by hygiene standards and collision-prone for adversarially chosen client ids. (Currently unreachable because create_access_token raises first, but the call site remains in exported code.) Use SHA-256 truncated, or a proper namespaced UUIDv5.

findings.title: PKCE code-challenge generator accepts 'plain' method
findings.detail: generate_pkce_code_challenge permits method 'plain' (lines 124, 134-135), returning the verifier unchanged. The catalog marks PLAIN as discouraged and requires production OAuth 2.1 flows to use S256. The ABC default is S256 so this conforms to the interface, but the primitive offers a downgrade path; reviewers must confirm no production caller passes 'plain'. Consider gating 'plain' behind an explicit dev-only flag.

findings.title: KeyManager stores opaque IKeyID as cache key and dict via to_string()/PersistenceBoundary correctly, but KeyPair.to_dict emits the opaque key_id object into a wire dict
findings.detail: KeyPair.to_dict (line 39) places self.key_id (an IKeyID opaque object) directly into the dict returned as the wire/serialized representation, rather than converting it through a PersistenceBoundary/to_string() like KeyManager does (key_manager.py lines 72/114/167). The opaque-ID discipline expects the wire/string form at serialization boundaries; emitting the raw opaque object into a Dict[str,Any] leaks the typed ID into an untyped wire surface and will not round-trip to JSON cleanly.

findings.title: JWT verifier selects verification algorithm from the attacker-controlled token header (algorithm-confusion / fail-open alg selection)
findings.detail: verify_access_token reads alg_name = header.get("alg") (line 201) and derives the verification algorithm via _get_algorithm_from_jwt_alg(alg_name) (line 205), i.e. the algorithm used to verify is taken from the token's OWN header rather than dictated by the caller. This violates the normative invariant 'Algorithm must be an explicit JWTAlgorithm passed by the caller; never inferred from the token header.' The public_key argument constrains family (ECDSA vs RSA isinstance dispatch in _verify_signature), but the attacker still selects the hash variant (e.g. downgrade ES512->ES256) and the design is structurally the alg-confusion antipattern. _get_jwt_alg_name and _get_hash_algorithm additionally fall back to a default (.get(algorithm, 'ES256') line 307 / hashes.SHA256() line 404) instead of raising, compounding the fail-open posture. JWTSigningService is re-exported from the security package (__init__.py line 1150) so this verifier is reachable by any consumer.

findings.title: Client-credentials service-account identity derived via MD5
findings.detail: create_client_credentials_token builds the service-account subject from hashlib.md5(f"service:{client_id_str}".encode()).digest() to seed a UUID (lines 280-282). MD5 is collision-prone; deriving a security-principal identifier (the JWT 'sub' for a service account) from MD5 means two distinct client_ids that collide map to the same service principal. Use a SHA-256-based derivation (or a real EntityIdentity lookup) for any value that participates in an identity/authorization decision.

findings.title: create_access_token is permanently dead (raises RuntimeError before building payload) yet remains a public API
findings.detail: create_access_token unconditionally raises RuntimeError at line 112 (TODO opaque-id-architecture) before any payload is constructed; all code after it (lines 137-157, including the reserved-claim-override guard and signing) is unreachable. create_client_credentials_token (line 284) calls into it and therefore also always raises. The class is exported from the top-level security package (__init__.py line 1150), advertising a token-minting API that cannot function -- a footgun and a sign this signer should be removed in favor of the KMS-backed SigningService rather than shipped half-built.

findings.title: Home-grown JWT verify path duplicates PyJWT-based JWTValidator with weaker guarantees
findings.detail: verify_access_token hand-rolls JWT verification (manual base64url split/decode, manual exp/nbf/iss/aud checks) in parallel to the canonical JWTCodec/JWTValidator path that the catalog designates as the single-codec authority. exp comparison uses now >= exp with no leeway control, audience is Optional and skipped entirely when None (line 234), and aud is compared with raw == against a possibly-list aud claim (line 236) so an RFC 7519 array-valued aud would never match. Prefer routing all verification through JWTValidator; this duplicate path is attack-surface and drift risk.

findings.title: validate_entropy uses a non-cryptographic unique-char heuristic that can pass low-entropy strings
findings.detail: validate_entropy estimates entropy as log2(unique_chars) * total_chars * (unique_chars/total_chars) (line 290) and returns True when >= minimum_bits. This is not a sound entropy measure: a long highly-repetitive but multi-character string can satisfy the threshold while having little real entropy, and it cannot distinguish CSPRNG output from a structured string. If any caller relies on this as a security gate it gives false assurance; entropy must come from the source (secrets), not be post-hoc 'validated'.

findings.title: generate_pkce_code_challenge accepts 'plain' method without restriction
findings.detail: generate_pkce_code_challenge permits method='plain' (lines 124-135), returning the verifier verbatim as the challenge. OAuth 2.1 requires S256 and the catalog states PLAIN is discouraged. This helper makes plain trivially available with no caller-side gate; a production flow that passes method from config/wire could silently downgrade PKCE. Default is S256 so impact is limited, but plain should be rejected here (or the helper made S256-only) to structurally prevent downgrade.

findings.title: generate_oauth_state_parameter truncates encoded output, conflating byte-count and char-count
findings.detail: generate_oauth_state_parameter generates `length` random bytes then base64url-encodes and slices to [:length] characters (lines 160-163). Since base64url yields ~1.33 chars/byte, slicing to `length` chars discards ~a quarter of generated material; the parameter remains adequately unpredictable (>=16 chars CSPRNG) but the byte-vs-char conflation (shared with generate_oauth_authorization_code and generate_client_secret) is a correctness smell. Hygiene only.

findings.title: DigitalIdentityService hardcodes P-256 multicodec prefix and strips it without validating key type
findings.detail: generate_did always prepends multicodec prefix bytes.fromhex('1200') (line 48) and extract_public_key_from_did blindly strips the first 2 bytes (line 175) without verifying the multicodec actually denotes a P-256 SubjectPublicKeyInfo key. validate_did (lines 134-144) only checks base58-decodability, not key type. A did:key string carrying a different multicodec/key type is decoded and load_der_public_key'd as whatever bytes follow. A DID is identity input; round-tripping an unvalidated key type into a PEM downstream code may trust for verification is a binding-integrity risk. Paths fail closed (return None on exception) limiting impact, but key-encoding type confusion is not prevented.

findings.title: resolve_did returns self-asserted did:key documents and silently fails did:web/did:peer
findings.detail: resolve_did constructs a DID document purely from the key embedded in the did:key string itself (lines 88-101) and caches it (line 103). validate_did also admits did:web/did:peer (line 130) for which resolve returns None with no network/registry resolution, so those identities silently fail to resolve rather than being authoritatively resolved. Callers must not treat a self-derived did:key document as externally-verified identity; this resolver provides no external trust anchor.

findings.title: Concrete PublicKeyProvider is dead/unwired and signature-incompatible with its only real consumer
findings.detail: PublicKeyProvider.get_jwk is declared `async def get_jwk(self, *, key_id, verification_context, execution_context)` with execution_context as a MANDATORY keyword arg. The only production consumer of an IPublicKeyProvider (security_client/production_request_context.py:318 and production_gateway.py) calls `get_jwk(key_id=kid, verification_context=vctx)` with NO execution_context, against a divergent locally-defined protocol (security_client/ipublic_key_provider.py:21 whose get_jwk takes only key_id+verification_context). A grep over the whole monorepo shows this concrete `PublicKeyProvider(` is never constructed anywhere except its own docstring example and tests. So this class is exported through three __init__.py layers but is wired to nothing, and if it WERE injected into the production request context it would raise TypeError on every call due to the missing execution_context arg. The shipped, advertised JWKS-caching/serve-stale implementation is dead code that cannot satisfy the interface its consumers actually use.

findings.title: serve_stale_on_error keeps serving a KMS-deleted/revoked signing key as valid
findings.detail: On stale-key refresh the except clause catches `KMSError` (the base class) and, when serve_stale_on_error is True (the DEFAULT, line 49), returns the cached jwk. KMSKeyNotFoundError (kms_key_not_found_error.py, raised on HTTP 404 when the key no longer exists in KMS) inherits KMSError, so a key the KMS has deliberately deleted/retired continues to be served as a trusted signing key for the entire stale_ttl window (default 24h, line 48). A 404 is an authoritative 'this key is gone' signal, not transient unavailability, yet it is treated identically to a network timeout. Refresh failures that fall back to stale are also swallowed with no log/metric/audit, so operators get no signal that a revoked key is still in use. (Defect is latent only because the class is currently unwired — see the dead-wire finding.)

findings.title: PlaintextDEK has no __repr__ override; default dataclass repr leaks raw key bytes into logs/tracebacks
findings.detail: PlaintextDEK is `@dataclass(frozen=True)` carrying `_value: bytes` (the raw data-encryption key) and its docstring states 'SECURITY: Never persist or log this object.' It overrides __eq__ and __hash__ but NOT __repr__, so the dataclass-generated __repr__ renders `PlaintextDEK(_value=b'...raw key...')`. Any f-string, logger.debug('%s', dek), or exception traceback that includes the object therefore leaks the plaintext DEK in cleartext — directly violating the class's own security contract. The custom __eq__/__hash__ show the author intended to control object behavior, making the missing repr guard (e.g. returning a redacted repr) a concrete omission rather than acceptable default.

findings.title: IPublicKeyProvider ABC defined inside the concrete implementation package, not in an *_interfaces module
findings.detail: IPublicKeyProvider (ABC) is declared at ipublic_key_provider.py:13 inside the concrete implementation tree security/.../integrations/kms/, and PublicKeyProvider(IPublicKeyProvider) imports it from there (public_key_provider.py:16). It is the only definition in the whole monorepo (grep confirms no copy in security_interfaces). Every sibling KMS contract — IKMSClient, IKMSClientConfiguration, IEncryptedDEK, IPlaintextDEK, IDEKPair, IEncryptionContext — lives under components/common/security_interfaces/.../common/kms/. This violates the interface-location discipline (interfaces belong in *_interfaces packages) the codebase enforces via the interface_location_validator hook, and means consumers must depend on the concrete package to type-hint against the provider contract.

findings.title: Default fail-open: stale public verification key served when the KMS authority is unreachable
findings.detail: get_jwk catches KMSError during a refresh of an already-stale entry and, when self._serve_stale_on_error is true, returns cached.jwk (public_key_provider.py:119-123). serve_stale_on_error defaults to True (line 49) and stale_ttl defaults to 24h (line 48), so by default a public key that was rotated or revoked at the KMS authority continues to be served for signature verification for up to 24h whenever KMS is unreachable. This is a silent fallback on an unreachable authority, contradicting the cross-cutting fail-closed invariant ('no silent fallback to defaults on ... unreachable authority'). For a key-resolution path feeding signature trust, the safe default is to fail closed and force callers to opt in to stale serving.

findings.title: clear_cache lock-drain loop is a no-op and the cache is cleared without holding any lock
findings.detail: clear_cache iterates the per-key locks acquiring and immediately releasing each one with an empty `async with self._get_lock(key_id): pass` (public_key_provider.py:171-173), then calls self._cache.clear() at line 174 outside every lock. The drain loop provides no mutual exclusion (it releases each lock before touching the cache), and the actual clear() runs unsynchronized, so a concurrent get_jwk holding a key lock can repopulate the cache during/after the clear. The intent (atomic cache flush under contention) is not achieved; the synchronization is dead code.

findings.title: PlaintextDEK exposes a wire-transmission deserializer, contradicting the IPlaintextDEK never-transmit invariant
findings.detail: IPlaintextDEK documents the security invariant 'Never log or transmit this object' (iplaintext_dek.py:6-7,16-19) and the ABC exposes only for_crypto() plus __eq__/__hash__ — deliberately no wire serializer. The concrete PlaintextDEK adds a from_b64_wire(cls, b64_str) classmethod (plaintext_dek.py:44-49) that reconstructs a plaintext key-encryption secret from a base64 *wire* string. EncryptedDEK legitimately has both to_b64_wire and from_b64_wire (encrypted_dek.py:34-52), but PlaintextDEK provides only the inbound half with no outbound counterpart, indicating a copy from the encrypted type rather than a deliberate need. The affordance invites transmitting/round-tripping plaintext DEKs over the wire, the exact thing the contract forbids.

findings.title: Unused import IRelativeReference in KMSClientConfiguration
findings.detail: kms_client_configuration.py:10 imports IRelativeReference from network_interfaces but it is never referenced in the module (only IURL is used, in the endpoint property). The same dead import is copied from the ABC ikms_client_configuration.py:9. Hygiene only.

findings.title: Inconsistent @override usage across KMS concrete classes
findings.detail: DEKPair.plaintext/encrypted (dek_pair.py:23-34), EncryptionContext.tenant_id/purpose/row_identifier (encryption_context.py:24-37), EncryptedDEK and PlaintextDEK methods implement their ABC members without the typing.override decorator, whereas KMSClientConfiguration (kms_client_configuration.py:28-45) and PublicKeyProvider (public_key_provider.py:72,145,161) do decorate every ABC override with @override. The missing decorators remove the static guarantee that these methods still match an ABC member if the contract is renamed; harmless today but an inconsistency a reviewer should normalize.

findings.title: Stale public-key serving defaults ON: revoked/rotated signing key served as trust anchor for up to stale_ttl (24h) when KMS is unreachable
findings.detail: In get_jwk(), when a cached key is past its TTL but within stale_ttl, a KMSError during refresh is caught (line 119-126) and, because serve_stale_on_error defaults to True (constructor line 49), the stale cached JWK is returned (line 123) instead of failing closed. The default stale_ttl is timedelta(hours=24) (line 48). Consequence: if an operator rotates or revokes a signing key in KMS because it was compromised, and the KMS subsequently becomes unreachable (or the refresh hits any KMSError), this provider keeps handing the OLD public key to JWT verifiers for up to 24h, so tokens signed with the revoked key continue to validate. Revocation does not propagate during a KMS outage. The trust anchor for JWS verification is thus served from a fail-OPEN cache by default. This is a documented graceful-degradation choice, but defaulting it ON (rather than requiring an explicit opt-in) makes the insecure-for-revocation posture the implicit behavior for every caller who constructs PublicKeyProvider without overriding serve_stale_on_error.

findings.title: Stale-serving catches only KMSError but invalidate() cannot purge a stale entry mid-outage, so a poisoned/rotated key persists in cache through the whole stale window
findings.detail: The stale-fallback branch (lines 121-123) returns cached.jwk and never refreshes fetched_at, so a key that goes stale stays anchored at its original fetched_at and is served for the full ttl+stale_ttl window. There is an invalidate() method (lines 145-159), but it is the only way to drop a known-bad key, and nothing in get_jwk re-checks freshness against KMS once the stale-serve path is taken. Combined with serve_stale_on_error=True, an operator who learns a key is compromised during a KMS outage has no in-band mechanism here to stop the stale key being served other than calling invalidate() out-of-band. Worth surfacing because the revocation-latency window is unbounded by anything except wall-clock stale_ttl.

findings.title: clear_cache acquires per-key locks as no-ops then clears the dict unguarded, defeating its own stampede-lock contract
findings.detail: clear_cache (lines 162-174) loops over a snapshot of keys and does `async with self._get_lock(key_id): pass` (lines 172-173) — acquiring then immediately releasing each lock with an empty body, accomplishing nothing — and then calls self._cache.clear() (line 174) outside any lock. Because there are no awaits between the snapshot and the clear within a single coroutine step, asyncio prevents a true data race here, so this is not exploitable; but the lock dance is dead code that misleadingly implies synchronization. A concurrent get_jwk that already passed its freshness check and is inside its own lock fetching can re-populate the cache immediately after clear_cache returns, so clear_cache offers no guarantee a freshly-revoked key is gone. Hygiene/correctness, not a vulnerability.

findings.title: SPIFFEProvider can never connect — _ensure_connected hard-codes client to None, so all SPIFFE authentication is dead
findings.detail: _ensure_connected() (lines 613-618) is documented 'SPIFFEClient is not yet implemented - using placeholder' and unconditionally executes `self._spiffe_client = None`. Every code path (authenticate_entity -> _authenticate_x509_svid/_authenticate_jwt_svid, verify_entity, refresh_identity) calls _ensure_connected then immediately tests `if not self._spiffe_client` and bails with CLIENT_ERROR / client_not_connected. A fully-functional SPIFFEClient exists (spiffe_client.py) but is never instantiated here, so the provider is a permanently-failing stub wired into IUniversalIdentityProvider. It fails closed, but the entire provider is non-functional dead wiring — any system relying on SPIFFE auth through this provider silently always denies.

findings.title: TokenExchangeProvider dereferences None _delegation_manager / _token_client stubs — verify/refresh/revoke/authenticate all broken
findings.detail: __init__ sets `self._token_client: Any = None` (line 87) and `self._delegation_manager: Any = None` (line 92) with TODO comments. verify_entity (line 229), refresh_identity (line 308) and revoke_identity (line 372) then iterate `self._delegation_manager._active_tokens.items()` which raises AttributeError on None; _authenticate_delegation/_authenticate_impersonation call `self._token_client.exchange_for_delegation(...)` on None. All are wrapped in broad `except Exception` so they fail closed, but a concrete DelegationTokenManager (delegation_token_manager.py) and TokenExchangeClient (token_exchange_client.py) exist and are never wired. The provider is a non-functional stub presented as a working IUniversalIdentityProvider implementation.

findings.title: TokenExchangeProvider._extract_entity_from_token mints a VERIFIED identity from any non-empty token string without verifying it
findings.detail: _extract_entity_from_token (lines 642-665) returns an EntityIdentity with trust_level=TrustLevel.MEDIUM and verification_status=EntityVerificationStatus.VERIFIED for ANY non-empty `token` string; the docstring admits 'For now, create a mock entity' and the value is `token:{token[:16]}`. No JWT decode, signature check, issuer/audience/expiry validation occurs. _create_mock_entity (line 667) is even worse, producing TrustLevel.VERIFIED for an arbitrary identifier. Were _token_client wired, the subject_entity fed into token exchange would be a forged, unverified identity flagged VERIFIED — a trust-fabrication defect.

findings.title: DelegationTokenManager generates an ephemeral in-process RSA signing key, breaking cross-process token validation and persistence
findings.detail: _generate_signing_key() (line 403-405) calls rsa.generate_private_key(...) at construction (line 81) and keeps it only in memory; the docstring says 'in production, these would be managed securely' (line 80). Tokens are signed with this random key and validate_token (line 234) verifies against the matching in-memory public key, so tokens minted by one process instance cannot be validated by any other instance/replica and do not survive restart — every restart silently invalidates all outstanding delegation tokens. The signing key is NOT a constructor/DI param, violating the credential-injection discipline (keys must be DI params, never self-generated).

findings.title: DID Web Ed25519 multibase key decoding strips only the prefix char and never removes the multicodec header — keys decode to wrong bytes
findings.detail: _verify_ed25519_signature (lines 629-645) handles public_key_multibase by `base64.b64decode(public_key_multibase[1:])` (or base58 fallback) — it removes only the 1-char multibase indicator but never strips the multicodec prefix (e.g. 0xED for Ed25519) that a real multibase key carries, and treats a 'z' (base58btc) multibase value as base64 first. The resulting bytes are not a valid 32-byte Ed25519 key, so Ed25519PublicKey.from_public_bytes / verify raises and the method returns False. This fails closed but means DID Web signature auth for correctly-encoded keys can never succeed — broken verification path. Contrast did_key_provider._parse_did_key (line 461) which correctly strips the multicodec prefix.

findings.title: DID Web authentication never binds the asserted DID to the resolved document id or verification-method controller
findings.detail: authenticate_entity resolves the DID document for the caller-supplied `did` (line 176) then _verify_signature (line 574) iterates ALL authentication methods in that document and accepts if any verifies the challenge. There is no check that did_document.id == did, nor that the matching verification method's `controller` equals the DID. Because _resolve_did_document derives the URL from the DID string itself this is partially mitigated, but the document content (id/controller) is never validated against the asserted identity, so a document whose internal id differs from the fetched DID is still accepted. Combined with the challenge being any caller-chosen string (no freshness/nonce binding enforced here), this weakens the authentication binding.

findings.title: SVIDValidator.validate_jwt_signature uses naive datetime.fromtimestamp(exp) — naive vs aware comparison and local-timezone expiry check
findings.detail: Line 330 computes `exp_time = datetime.fromtimestamp(exp)` (naive, local TZ) then line 331 compares `datetime.now(timezone.utc) > exp_time`. Comparing an aware UTC now against a naive local datetime raises TypeError in Python (caught by the broad `except Exception` at line 366 -> mapped to unexpected error, so technically fail-closed), and even if it didn't, fromtimestamp without tz interprets exp in local time, shifting the expiry boundary by the host UTC offset. The same naive-fromtimestamp pattern appears in jwtsvid.py from_token (lines 68-73) producing naive not_before/not_after used by is_valid_at against aware datetimes.

findings.title: JWTSVID.verify_token lets the token's own header algorithm select the verification algorithm (alg-from-token)
findings.detail: verify_token (line 106) defaults `algorithms = algorithms or [self.algorithm] if self.algorithm else ['RS256']`, and self.algorithm is taken verbatim from the unverified JWT header `header.get('alg','')` in from_token (line 90). The caller SPIFFEClient.validate_jwt_svid (spiffe_client.py line 438) invokes verify_token(public_key_pem) with no explicit algorithms, so the attacker-controlled token header dictates the verification algorithm — the exact algorithm-confusion footgun the catalog says verifiers must preclude. A token presenting e.g. an HMAC alg with a PEM public key, or any algorithm the supplied PEM happens to match, is selected by the token rather than the verifier.

findings.title: EUDIWalletClient credential verification is a hard-coded pass — signature and issuer-trust checks always return True
findings.detail: verify_credential (line 398) gates on three private checks but _verify_credential_signature (line 629) `return True  # Placeholder` (no signature/cert-chain verification) and _verify_issuer_trust (line 645) `return True  # Placeholder` (no eIDAS trust-list check). Only is_valid_at_time and is_revoked are actually evaluated. Any credential that is unexpired and not self-marked revoked is reported authentic — a fail-OPEN credential verifier. EUIDProvider currently never constructs this client (it is None), but the class is a complete, importable ISO/eIDAS verifier whose core cryptographic checks are stubbed to accept everything.

findings.title: TokenRefreshManager._should_refresh lifetime computation is nonsensical and mis-fires refresh decisions
findings.detail: _should_refresh (lines 212-221) computes lifetime as `expiration - expiration.replace(year=expiration.year - (expiration.year - now.year))`, i.e. expiration minus expiration-with-now's-year. This is not the SVID lifetime (issuance is never used); it collapses to roughly the year-difference in time and is 0 when now and expiration share a year (then total_seconds()<=0 -> returns True = always refresh). The threshold-based refresh logic is therefore broken: for same-year tokens it forces continuous refresh, for cross-year tokens it uses an arbitrary multi-month 'lifetime'. The refresh_threshold_percentage knob is effectively dead.

findings.title: verify_entity (SPIFFE) computes a verification status expression and discards it
findings.detail: Lines 314-318 evaluate `(EntityVerificationStatus.VERIFIED if meets_requirements and len(failure_reasons)==0 else EntityVerificationStatus.UNVERIFIED)` as a bare expression statement whose result is never assigned or used. EntityVerificationResult is built without an explicit verification status from this computed value. Dead code indicating an intended status field was dropped during a refactor; harmless to trust (the boolean is_verified is set correctly) but a code-health defect in a security decision path.

findings.title: TokenExchangeProvider.health_check returns True without performing any endpoint check
findings.detail: health_check (lines 429-438): once _token_client is non-None it enters `async with self._token_client:` and the comment states 'This would make a test request ... For now, just return True'. No connectivity or token-endpoint reachability is actually verified, so a healthy result is reported whenever a client object merely exists. Misleading health signal for a delegation provider.

findings.title: X509SVID.verify_certificate_chain does no cryptographic chain validation — only checks the chain list is non-empty
findings.detail: verify_certificate_chain (lines 216-227) comment: 'For now, just check that the chain is not empty / Full chain verification would require implementing X.509 path verification'. It parses the leaf and returns `len(self.certificate_chain) > 0` — no issuer/subject linkage, signature, validity-window, or trust-anchor checks. Any X509SVID carrying at least one (arbitrary, possibly attacker-supplied) chain cert reports a valid chain. A method named verify_* that performs no verification is a fail-open trap for any consumer that trusts its boolean.

findings.title: JWTSVID.verify_token selects verification algorithm from the token's own header (algorithm confusion)
findings.detail: `verify_token` computes `algorithms = algorithms or [self.algorithm] if self.algorithm else ["RS256"]`, where `self.algorithm` is `header.get("alg", "")` taken verbatim from the unverified JWT header in `from_token` (line 90). This violates the catalog invariant that the verifier dictates the algorithm and the token's own `alg` must never select the verification algorithm/key type. An attacker controls `alg`; combined with an RSA public key bundle and HMAC, this is the classic alg-confusion vector. The verifier here is `SPIFFEClient.validate_jwt_svid` (spiffe_client.py:437-438) which calls `verify_token(public_key_pem)` with no explicit algorithm, so the header-supplied alg is used.

findings.title: SPIFFEProvider._ensure_connected is a no-op stub that force-nulls the client
findings.detail: `_ensure_connected` body is `if not self._spiffe_client: self._spiffe_client = None` (lines 615-618) with comment "SPIFFEClient is not yet implemented - using placeholder". A real `SPIFFEClient` exists in this very package (spiffe_client.py) but is never instantiated. Consequently every authenticate/verify path that depends on the client (e.g. `_authenticate_x509_svid` line 493, `verify_entity` line 265) takes the `client_not_connected`/`CLIENT_ERROR` branch. The provider's entire authentication surface is dead-wired; it can never return a successful SPIFFE authentication.

findings.title: SPIFFEProvider._ensure_jwt_bundles_updated never fetches bundles; JWT SVID signature is verified against an empty bundle set
findings.detail: `_ensure_jwt_bundles_updated` sets `self._jwt_bundles = {}` (line 632, the real fetch is commented out) then marks bundles fresh. `_authenticate_jwt_svid` then calls `validate_jwt_svid(jwt_svid, self._jwt_bundles, ...)` (line 576). In `SPIFFEClient.validate_jwt_svid` an empty bundle for the trust domain returns False (spiffe_client.py:434) — so it fails closed today, but the bundle-refresh path is a permanent no-op masquerading as a 5-minute cache update, meaning JWT-SVID authentication can never succeed and any future flip of the empty-bundle guard would verify signatures against no authorities.

findings.title: DID Web challenge-response has no nonce/freshness binding — signature replay
findings.detail: `authenticate_entity` accepts `challenge` and `signature` straight from `authentication_request.authentication_data` (lines 164-166) and `_verify_signature` (line 574) only checks that `signature` is a valid Ed25519 signature over `challenge.encode()` (line 664). Nothing binds `challenge` to a server-issued, single-use, time-limited nonce, and nothing records used challenges. Any previously observed (challenge, signature) pair re-authenticates the DID indefinitely. The catalog's non-body/replay invariant requires freshness; here the entire proof comes from the forgeable request body.

findings.title: DID Key challenge-response has no nonce/freshness binding — signature replay
findings.detail: `authenticate_entity` reads `did_key`, `challenge`, `signature` from the request body (lines 121-123) and `_verify_signature` (line 510) verifies the signature over `challenge.encode()` with no server-issued nonce, expiry, or used-challenge tracking. A captured (did_key, challenge, signature) triple is replayable forever to authenticate as that key holder. Same forgeable-body / missing-freshness defect as DID Web.

findings.title: JWTSVID builds not_before/not_after as naive local-time datetimes, breaking validity comparisons against aware UTC now
findings.detail: `from_token` uses `datetime.fromtimestamp(...)` (lines 68,69,73) with no tz, producing naive local-time datetimes for `issued_at`/`not_before`/`not_after`. The base `SVID.is_valid_at` and `is_expired` compare these against `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` (svid.py:53,57), which raises `TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes`. In `SPIFFEProvider._authenticate_jwt_svid` that call is at line 567 inside a try that maps any exception to a generic failure (line 608) — fail-closed, but the validity window is effectively never correctly evaluated, and on a host whose local tz is behind UTC the naive value is also numerically wrong, silently widening/narrowing the window wherever the comparison does not raise.

findings.title: SVIDValidator.validate_jwt_signature compares naive exp against aware now and re-checks exp PyJWT already enforced
findings.detail: After `jwt.decode(... verify_signature=True)` the code does `exp_time = datetime.fromtimestamp(exp)` (line 330, naive local) and `datetime.now(timezone.utc) > exp_time` (line 331), comparing aware vs naive which raises `TypeError`. That TypeError is caught by the generic `except Exception` (line 366) and mapped to an unexpected error — so a token that actually passed signature verification is reported as a verification failure rather than expiry, and the bespoke audience check (lines 339-348) after it is unreachable for any token carrying `exp`. The expiry intent is unreliable; rely on PyJWT's own `verify_exp` instead.

findings.title: TokenExchangeClient takes client_secret as a bare str instead of typed IClientSecret
findings.detail: Constructor param `client_secret: Optional[str] = None` (line 61) and it is interpolated raw into the HTTP Basic header at line 140 (`f"{client_id_str}:{self._client_secret}"`). The opaque-ID & credential-injection invariant requires credentials to be typed primitives (`IClientSecret`) with wire-`str` only at `to_wire`; here the secret is an unstructured string with no type-confusion protection, defeating the structural guarantee the catalog calls for. The client_id is correctly opaque (`IOAuthClientIdentifier`, line 59) — the secret is the outlier.

findings.title: EUIDProvider accepts verifier credentials as a plain Dict and is a non-functional stub
findings.detail: `__init__` takes `verifier_key_pair: Optional[Dict[str, str]]` (line 64) — a raw string dict of cryptographic key material rather than a typed credential primitive, violating the constructor-injected-typed-credential invariant. Compounding this, `authenticate_entity` (line 175) and `verify_entity` (line 209) unconditionally return failure (`EUID_NOT_IMPLEMENTED`), and `_wallet_client` is hard-coded to None (line 116). The class declares conformance to IUniversalIdentityProvider but its core methods are placeholders; the unused `_create_entity_identity_from_credential`/`_assess_credential_base_trust_level` helpers are dead code.

findings.title: X509SVID.verify_certificate_chain does no actual chain verification — only a non-empty check
findings.detail: `verify_certificate_chain` (line 216) returns `len(self.certificate_chain) > 0` after merely confirming the leaf parses; the comment admits "Full chain verification would require implementing X.509 path verification". A method named verify_* that returns True for any parseable leaf with at least one (unvalidated) chain cert is a misleading no-op: it performs no signature-linkage, trust-anchor, or path validation, so a caller relying on it to gate trust gets a false assurance.

findings.title: DIDWebProvider caches resolved DID documents with no signature/integrity check, trusting plain TLS-fetched JSON
findings.detail: `_resolve_did_document` fetches the did.json over `UnauthenticatedHttpClient` and constructs `DIDDocument(document_data)` (line 562) with no proof-of-control / controller binding beyond transport TLS, then derives the authentication public key from `authentication[0]` (`_extract_primary_public_key`, line 817). Trust level can reach VERIFIED purely from self-asserted document fields (`trust_anchors`/`model_provenance`, did_web_provider.py:779-784) that the document author controls. This mirrors the OIDC discovery 'integrity rests on TLS' caveat but here self-asserted trust_anchors elevate trust_level with no external anchor verification (the anchor-check branch at lines 753-757 is an explicit no-op `pass`).

findings.title: SVIDValidator.validate_jwt_signature accepts a fixed multi-algorithm allow-list embedding the header alg indirectly
findings.detail: `jwt.decode` is called with a hardcoded `algorithms=["RS256","RS384","RS512","ES256"]` (line 323). This is safer than trusting the header, but it still lets the token's header choose among four algorithms against a single caller-supplied key; the catalog invariant is that the verifier dictates a single explicit algorithm. Mixing RSA and ECDSA acceptors against one key object is benign with `load_pem_public_key` typing but conflicts with the 'explicit JWTAlgorithm passed by caller' rule and should be a single caller-selected algorithm.

findings.title: JWTSVID.verify_token lets the token's own header pick the verification algorithm (algorithm confusion)
findings.detail: `algorithms = algorithms or [self.algorithm] if self.algorithm else ["RS256"]` — when the caller omits `algorithms`, the allowed-algorithm list is built from `self.algorithm`, which `from_token` (line 90) copied verbatim from `jwt.get_unverified_header(token)['alg']`. The attacker controls the JWT header, so the attacker selects which algorithm PyJWT uses to verify against the supplied RSA `public_key_pem`. Classic RS256→HS256 confusion is enabled: a token forged with alg=HS256 would cause PyJWT to treat the PEM bytes as an HMAC secret. The catalog explicitly requires the verifier to dictate the algorithm and forbids inferring it from the token header ("never inferred from the token header"). Worse, `spiffe_client.validate_jwt_svid` (spiffe_client.py:438) calls `jwt_svid.verify_token(public_key_pem)` with NO `algorithms` argument, so production SPIFFE JWT-SVID validation hits exactly this attacker-controlled-alg path.

findings.title: JWT-SVID validation is structurally dead: provider always supplies an empty trust bundle
findings.detail: `_ensure_jwt_bundles_updated` never fetches real bundles: the only assignment is `self._jwt_bundles = {}` (line 632, with the actual fetch commented out), and it is gated on `if self._spiffe_client` which is itself always None (see _ensure_connected finding). `_authenticate_jwt_svid` (line 576) then calls `validate_jwt_svid(jwt_svid, self._jwt_bundles, ...)`; in spiffe_client.validate_jwt_svid the empty bundle makes `bundle = jwt_bundles.get(trust_domain)` None → returns False. This is fail-closed (JWT auth always rejected), so not an auth bypass, but it is a non-functional verification path masquerading as implemented — a reviewer must not treat SPIFFE JWT-SVID auth as working, and the dead path hides the alg-confusion bug above from ever being exercised in this code path while still leaving JWTSVID.verify_token exposed to any other caller.

findings.title: SPIFFEProvider._ensure_connected is a noop stub that hard-codes the client to None
findings.detail: `_ensure_connected` body is `if not self._spiffe_client: self._spiffe_client = None` — it never instantiates SPIFFEClient(self._socket_path). Consequently `_authenticate_x509_svid` (line 493) and `_authenticate_jwt_svid` (line 556) always take the `if not self._spiffe_client:` branch and return CLIENT_ERROR, and verify_entity returns {client_not_connected}. The whole SPIFFEProvider authentication surface is inert. This fails closed, so it is not exploitable, but it is a stub that silently disables a security provider; combined with health_check (line 431) which DOES build a real client, callers can be misled into believing the provider is operational when authenticate/verify can never succeed.

findings.title: DID Web / DID Key challenge-response has no nonce/replay binding; challenge is fully attacker-supplied from the request body
findings.detail: authenticate_entity parses `did`, `challenge`, `signature` directly from `authentication_request.authentication_data` (lines 162-165) and `_verify_signature`/`_verify_ed25519_signature` (line 664) verify `signature` over `challenge.encode()`. Nothing requires the challenge to have been server-issued, bound to a nonce, time-boxed, or tied to this verifier/session. A captured (did, challenge, signature) triple replays indefinitely and authenticates as that DID. The same pattern exists in did_key_provider.py authenticate_entity (lines 120-139). The catalog's binding-proof / replay requirements (PEP assertions are 'short-lived, single-use, request-bound'; SAML callers must enforce one-time use) are violated: this is a primary authentication path with zero replay protection.

findings.title: DID Web Ed25519 verification accepts arbitrary attacker-chosen public key from the resolved document with no key/DID binding or trust anchoring
findings.detail: _verify_signature iterates EVERY authentication method in the DID document and returns True if ANY one verifies (lines 600-611). The DID document is fetched over plain HTTPS from a host derived from the attacker-supplied `did` string (_resolve_did_document lines 525-539: `did:web:<host>...` → `https://<host>/...`). There is no trust-anchor / allow-list check on which DIDs/hosts are acceptable (the trust-anchor branch in _assess_trust_level is a `pass`, lines 754-757), and no pinning. An attacker who controls any web host can mint a did:web pointing at it, publish a document containing their own Ed25519 key, sign the challenge, and authenticate as that DID with trust_level up to VERIFIED (via _assess_base_trust_level, lines 779-785, which elevates to VERIFIED merely if the document declares trust_anchors or a model_provenance field — both attacker-controlled document contents). The provider treats document self-assertions as trust signals.

findings.title: SVIDValidator.validate_jwt_signature expiry check is broken (naive vs aware datetime) and silently routed to fail-closed
findings.detail: `exp_time = datetime.fromtimestamp(exp)` builds a naive (local-time) datetime, then `datetime.now(timezone.utc) > exp_time` compares aware vs naive, which raises TypeError. That TypeError is swallowed by the broad `except Exception` (line 366) and mapped to handle_unexpected_error (fail-closed). Net effect: the explicit per-claim expiry branch is dead code, and the call also passes `options={"verify_signature": True}` to jwt.decode WITHOUT enabling verify_exp, so PyJWT does not enforce exp either. Same naive-fromtimestamp pattern appears in jwtsvid.py from_token (lines 68-73). Fails closed by accident rather than design; an expired-but-otherwise-valid token would error out, but the intended graceful expiry classification never runs and the dependence on an accidental TypeError is fragile.

findings.title: SVIDValidator.validate_certificate_chain performs name-chaining only, never verifies signatures (no real path validation)
findings.detail: validate_certificate_chain only checks that `current_cert.issuer != next_cert.subject` (DN string equality, lines 181-189) and expiry. It never calls the parent's public key to verify the child certificate's signature, never checks BasicConstraints/CA flags, key usage, or anchoring to a trusted root/trust bundle. An attacker can present a leaf they generated plus an intermediate whose Subject DN merely string-matches the leaf's Issuer DN — DNs are not authenticated, so a forged chain with matching DN text passes. X509SVID.verify_certificate_chain (x509_svid.py:216-227) is even weaker: it returns True iff the chain list is merely non-empty. The catalog states X.509 SVID validation is 'only as sound as the bundle'; here there is no cryptographic chain verification and no trust-bundle anchoring at all, so a chain this validator blesses provides no real assurance.

findings.title: EUIDProvider._create_entity_identity_from_credential trusts unverified credential and assigns TrustLevel.VERIFIED with no signature check
findings.detail: _assess_credential_base_trust_level returns TrustLevel.VERIFIED unconditionally for any DigitalCredential (line 394, and both eIDAS branches also return VERIFIED), and _create_entity_identity_from_credential stamps verification_status=EntityVerificationStatus.VERIFIED (line 373) and copies the credential's proof_of_issuance into a signature object without ever verifying that proof against an issuer/member-state trust anchor. Today this is unreachable because authenticate_entity/verify_entity are hard stubs returning EUID_NOT_IMPLEMENTED (lines 175-188, 209-221), so the live provider fails closed. But the helper is wired to mint a government-grade VERIFIED identity from an unauthenticated credential blob, which is a latent fail-open the moment the wallet client is connected; flagging so it is fixed before the stub is completed.

findings.title: DID Web document resolution and cache are not validated against the requested DID's controller (cache poisoning / SSRF-shaped resolution)
findings.detail: _resolve_did_document keys its in-memory cache purely on the raw `did` string (lines 519, 565) and accepts whatever JSON the derived URL returns as the authoritative DIDDocument (line 562) without checking that the document's own `id` field equals the requested DID. A document served at host A can claim to be the DID document for an unrelated DID id; nothing rejects the mismatch, so downstream _create_entity_identity binds provider_identifier to the requested `did` while the keys came from an unrelated document. Combined with the lack of host allow-listing, the host portion of did:web is fully attacker-controlled, letting the resolver be pointed at arbitrary internal/external hosts over the shared UnauthenticatedHttpClient. A reviewer must confirm consumers never treat a did:web identity as trusted without an out-of-band controller/host allow-list.

findings.title: TrustDecisionCombinator ignores the caller-supplied trust_threshold floor — high-bar thresholds silently downgraded to 60.0
findings.detail: combine() receives trust_threshold: ITrustThreshold (which carries required_trust_level: TrustLevel and required_factors per security_interfaces/.../itrust_threshold.py) but never reads it. Line 78-79 set `trust_floor = self._minimum_trust_score` (the fixed _DEFAULT_MINIMUM_TRUST_SCORE = 60.0) and compare `trust_score.overall_score < trust_floor`. The class docstring (lines 38-41) explicitly claims it uses 'trust_threshold.required_trust_level floor (falling back to minimum_trust_score when the threshold carries no numeric mapping)'. No such mapping or fallback exists — the threshold parameter is dead. An operation that requests a VERIFIED/admin/financial bar (e.g. required_trust_level=VERIFIED, ~81+) is approved at any score >= 60.0, weakening the PERMIT decision below the requested policy. required_factors is likewise never enforced here.

findings.title: TrustManager.validate_trust_chain is a fail-open placeholder that always returns True
findings.detail: validate_trust_chain (lines 118-150) ignores source_entity, target_entity, and required_trust_level and unconditionally `return True` with a comment 'Placeholder implementation: always valid'. If this method is ever consumed as a trust gate it grants every chain regardless of trust level or expiry. assess_entity_trust (lines 34-63) likewise returns a hardcoded Trust.create(score=65.0, factors=set()), and calculate_transitive_trust (lines 152-189) returns an empty factor set with a TODO. TrustManager is exported from security/__init__.py:683 as public API; nothing constructs it today, but it is a live re-export presenting a real trust API whose security-relevant methods are stubs.

findings.title: TrustManager exported as public security API but never constructed — dead-wire stub family
findings.detail: TrustManager is re-exported through four __init__ chains (security/__init__.py:683, layer_0_primitives/__init__.py:342, identity_framework/__init__.py:120, trust/__init__.py:45) but no production code constructs `TrustManager()` anywhere in components/ or applications/ (grep for `TrustManager()` excluding CrossDomainTrustManager returns nothing). It implements no ABC (line 244 comment: 'Protocol verification removed - no ITrustManager protocol exists'), so its placeholder methods are not even checked against a contract. The whole class is dead-but-public: callers importing `from security import TrustManager` would get an always-true trust evaluator.

findings.title: TrustValidator orchestrator and per-concern checker chain are exported but never instantiated by any consumer
findings.detail: TrustValidator (and its collaborators IdentityVerificationChecker, PolicyComplianceChecker, RiskAssessor, TemporalConstraintChecker, CrossDomainResultAnalyzer, TrustDecisionCombinator) are only re-exported via __init__ chains and referenced in a docstring of layer_2_gateways/implementations/verify_identity_impl.py (line 36, prose only). No production site constructs TrustValidator or calls validate_entity_trust. verify_identity_impl itself ignores the trust machinery and, when no verifier is injected, returns {'verified': True, 'trust_level': 'high'} (a separate fail-open, outside this unit). The entire identity-trust evaluation pipeline is currently unwired.

findings.title: required_factors from the operation threshold is discarded by the identity checker
findings.detail: check() accepts required_factors: frozenset[TrustFactorType] (line 55) and the docstring at lines 100-104 admits 'passing it through the engine is Phase 3 work — today the engine applies its own global minimum set.' The parameter is never used: line 105 calls validate_minimum_trust_factors(entity, verification_context, operation_id) with no required_factors. An operation that demands specific factors (e.g. GOVERNMENT_ISSUED, HARDWARE_BACKED) gets only the engine's fixed minimum set {CRYPTOGRAPHIC_PROOF, TIME_CONSISTENCY} checked, so a per-operation factor requirement is silently not enforced.

findings.title: TrustAssessmentEngine.validate_minimum_trust_factors swallows all exceptions and returns False without surfacing the error
findings.detail: Lines 299-319 wrap the entire factor collection in `try: ... except Exception: return False`. The fail-closed direction (return False on error) is correct for a gate, but the blanket catch erases the distinction between 'entity genuinely lacks factors' and 'attribute projection / repository raised', so misconfiguration or a bug in entity_identity_to_attribute_map degrades every entity to 'insufficient factors' with no log, error, or VerificationContext signal. A real fault becomes an invisible blanket denial that is hard to diagnose.

findings.title: CrossDomainTrustManager._determine_entity_domain binds an entity to the first registry domain that merely supports its type/provider
findings.detail: _determine_entity_domain (lines 352-361) iterates self._domains and returns the first domain where supports_entity_type AND supports_provider are true — a structural-capability match, not an authority-resolved home domain. The comment on line 359 ('Additional domain-specific checks could be added here') confirms it is provisional. The chosen source domain then drives _find_trust_path and the entire cross-domain PERMIT/CONDITIONAL outcome (lines 217-252). An entity can be attributed to a source domain it does not actually belong to whenever two domains share entity-type/provider support, producing a trust path that was never authority-verified.

findings.title: CrossDomainTrustManager._find_trust_path has no transitive path-finding; comment claims BFS that is not implemented
findings.detail: Lines 394-398 carry the comment 'BFS for transitive trust path (simplified implementation) ... For now, return empty path if no direct relationship' and then cache+return []. Only direct (and bidirectional-direct) relationships are honored. This is fail-closed (no path -> INVALID via line 218-225), so it is not a bypass, but it is a stubbed algorithm masquerading as BFS in the comment, and it silently denies any legitimately transitive trust topology — a correctness gap a reviewer must know is not real path-finding.

findings.title: TrustStore is an in-memory placeholder behind a PersistenceBoundary with no actual persistence and no ABC
findings.detail: TrustStore stores trusted entities in a plain dict (line 14, self._trusted_entities) yet wraps each id conversion in PersistenceBoundary() (lines 22, 32, 44, 53) implying a persistence layer that does not exist. is_trusted() (lines 39-46) returns membership in a process-local dict that is empty on every restart, so 'trusted' state is lost across processes/instances. The class implements no interface and is exported but unconstructed; as a trust source it would answer 'not trusted' for everything after restart and cannot be shared across instances.

findings.title: TrustValidator drops risk_factors on the floor due to an unresolved type mismatch
findings.detail: RiskAssessor.assess returns a populated risk_factors Dict[str,Any] (risk_assessor.py lines 52-130), but TrustValidator assembles the result with risk_factors=None (line 269) because TrustVerificationResult.risk_factors is typed ISessionRecord and the bridge is deferred (comment lines 265-268). The computed per-factor risk breakdown never reaches the result object, so any downstream consumer reading result.risk_factors sees None regardless of detected risk. risk_level is still propagated, so the final decision is unaffected, but the detailed risk evidence is silently discarded.

findings.title: TrustDecisionCombinator.combine ignores the caller-supplied trust_threshold (dead parameter) — fail-open against a stricter operation bar
findings.detail: The class docstring (lines 36-44) and the ITrustDecisionCombinator contract (itrust_decision_combinator.py line 53, 'trust_threshold: The bar the entity must clear') promise that the decision uses the threshold's required trust level, 'falling back to minimum_trust_score when the threshold carries no numeric trust-level mapping.' But line 79 unconditionally sets `trust_floor = self._minimum_trust_score` and the `trust_threshold` argument is never read anywhere in combine(). A caller passing an ITrustThreshold demanding e.g. VERIFIED/90.0 gets the static 60.0 floor instead. Any per-operation tightening of the trust bar is silently dropped, so an entity scoring 60-89 is granted trust for operations that required a higher bar. This is a weakened/dead-wired authorization control.

findings.title: IdentityVerificationChecker.check ignores required_factors — operation-specific trust-factor requirements are never enforced
findings.detail: IIdentityVerificationChecker.check (iidentity_verification_checker.py lines 33-51) declares `required_factors: frozenset[TrustFactorType]` as 'Trust-factor kinds that must be present, drawn from the operation's ITrustThreshold.' The concrete check() at lines 52-147 accepts the parameter but never reads it; line 105 calls validate_minimum_trust_factors(entity, verification_context, operation_id) which applies only the engine's GLOBAL minimum factor set (TrustAssessmentEngine._create_default_trust_factors minimum_factors = {CRYPTOGRAPHIC_PROOF, TIME_CONSISTENCY}). The inline comment (lines 100-104) concedes 'passing it through the engine is Phase 3 work.' Result: an operation whose threshold demands additional factors (e.g. GOVERNMENT_ISSUED, HARDWARE_BACKED) is admitted as long as the global two are present — the per-operation requirement is a no-op.

findings.title: TrustManager.validate_trust_chain unconditionally returns True — trust-chain check is a fail-open placeholder
findings.detail: validate_trust_chain (lines 118-150) is documented as determining 'if there is a valid trust relationship chain from source to target entity that meets the required trust level,' but the body is a placeholder that ends with `return True` (line 150) regardless of source_entity, target_entity, or required_trust_level. Any caller relying on this to gate cross-entity trust gets an unconditional ALLOW. Combined with assess_entity_trust returning a hardcoded score=65.0 (line 60-63) and get_trust_relationships_for_entity returning [] (line 196), TrustManager is a trust-granting stub. This violates the project rule against stub implementations that fabricate trust answers.

findings.title: TrustManager.assess_entity_trust returns a hardcoded medium trust score of 65.0 independent of the entity
findings.detail: assess_entity_trust (lines 34-63) ignores entity_identity and verification_request and returns Trust.create(score=65.0, factors=set()). 65.0 is above the combinator/policy default trust floor of 60.0, so any consumer of TrustManager (as opposed to TrustValidator/TrustAssessmentEngine) treats every entity — including unverified or expired ones — as medium-trust by default. A trust assessor that fabricates a passing score for all inputs is a fail-open default.

findings.title: CrossDomainTrustManager._determine_entity_domain binds entity to the first domain that merely supports its type/provider, not its actual source domain
findings.detail: _determine_entity_domain (lines 352-361) returns the first registered domain whose supports_entity_type AND supports_provider match the entity, with the comment 'Additional domain-specific checks could be added here.' The entity's real originating domain is never consulted — there is no field on EntityIdentity tying it to a source domain in this path. This means cross-domain validation computes a trust path FROM an arbitrarily-chosen domain that happens to accept the entity's type, which can shortcut or fabricate a trust path the entity is not actually entitled to. The downstream decision (validate_cross_domain_trust lines 216-247) then trusts this guessed source. Weakened domain-binding in an authorization path.

findings.title: CrossDomainTrustManager broad except maps any internal error to an INVALID result but also catches programmer errors, masking failures as data
findings.detail: validate_cross_domain_trust wraps the whole body in `try/except Exception as e` (lines 154, 285-292) and converts every exception into _create_failed_verification with failure_reason=f'verification_error: {e}'. While this fails closed for the cross-domain status (INVALID), it embeds the raw exception string into verification_factors (line 609) and swallows all error typing, so a genuine bug (e.g. AttributeError) is indistinguishable from a legitimate trust-path denial. Catalog requires failure classification by type, not by string-formatting arbitrary exceptions into the result. Low because the immediate decision still denies.

findings.title: TrustValidator drops IRiskAssessor risk_factors on the floor (risk_factors=None) — assembled result loses the risk breakdown it computed
findings.detail: Step 5 (lines 217-220) computes risk_level, risk_factors, risk_issues from the RiskAssessor, but the assembled TrustVerificationResult is constructed with risk_factors=None (line 269) because TrustVerificationResult.risk_factors is typed as Optional[ISessionRecord] while the assessor returns Dict[str,Any] (acknowledged in the comment lines 265-268). The risk issues still flow into verification_issues, so the decision is not weakened, but TrustVerificationResult.get_overall_risk_score() (trust_verification_result.py lines 99-122) will always return 0.0 for results produced by this validator. A type mismatch between the assessor contract and the result contract that silently discards computed risk detail.

findings.title: TrustRelationship.is_valid_at passes a relationship that requires reverification but was never validated (last_validated_at is None)
findings.detail: is_valid_at (lines 77-94) only fails the reverification check when last_validated_at AND reverification_interval are both set (lines 85-92). When requires_reverification=True but last_validated_at is None — the state of a freshly established, never-revalidated relationship (establish_trust_relationship never sets last_validated_at) — the guard is skipped and the relationship is treated as valid. needs_reverification (line 101-102) correctly returns True for the same state, so the two methods disagree: a relationship that needs reverification is still accepted by is_valid_at, which is the method the trust-path validation actually gates on (cross_domain_trust_manager.py lines 377, 388, 434). Missing-state defaults to valid rather than fail-closed.

findings.title: TrustValidator.get_verification_statistics reports zeroed audit metrics it cannot populate — statistics are partly fabricated/empty
findings.detail: get_verification_statistics (lines 281-311) returns an EnforcementStatistics whose policies_enforced, violations_detected, average_processing_time_ms, compliance_checks_performed, compliance_failures, peak_processing_time_ms are all hardcoded to 0/0.0 (lines 304-310), as the docstring concedes ('left at their zero defaults until the validator is wired to a real audit source'). Only cache-derived granted/denied counts are real. Consumers reading these fields as enforcement evidence would be misled. Hygiene/observability only — not an authorization path.

findings.title: TrustDecisionCombinator ignores the operation's required trust level — every operation gated at a fixed 60.0 floor
findings.detail: combine() receives trust_threshold: ITrustThreshold (which carries required_trust_level and required_factors), and the docstring (lines 38-44) claims the trust floor uses "the threshold's numeric cutoff when available, otherwise fall back to minimum_trust_score". But the body sets `trust_floor = self._minimum_trust_score` (60.0) at line 79 and never reads trust_threshold at all — required_trust_level and required_factors are completely unused. An operation that declares a HIGH/VERIFIED bar (e.g. financial/admin) is PERMITTED at the same 60.0 score as a read-only operation. The per-operation trust bar is silently dropped, weakening every authorization that relies on a stricter threshold.

findings.title: TrustManager.validate_trust_chain is a fail-OPEN stub that always returns True
findings.detail: validate_trust_chain(source_entity, target_entity, required_trust_level, operation_id) ignores all four arguments and unconditionally `return True` (line 150). TrustManager is a publicly exported symbol (security/__init__.py:1527). Any caller asking "is there a valid trust chain from A to B meeting required_trust_level?" receives an unconditional yes regardless of whether any relationship exists, is expired, or meets the level — a trust-chain authorization bypass for any consumer wired to this class.

findings.title: TrustManager.assess_entity_trust returns hardcoded medium trust (65.0) for every entity
findings.detail: assess_entity_trust ignores entity_identity and verification_request and returns Trust.create(score=65.0, factors=set()) (lines 60-63). A score of 65.0 lands above common MEDIUM/HIGH thresholds (e.g. TrustScoreLevel.HIGH=61.0 in trust_assessment_engine.py:686), so an untrusted or unverified entity is reported as moderately trusted. Exported via security/__init__.py:1527; any consumer using TrustManager (rather than TrustValidator/TrustAssessmentEngine) gets a constant trust verdict decoupled from the actual identity.

findings.title: TrustRelationship treated as valid forever when reverification required but never validated
findings.detail: is_valid_at() only enforces the reverification deadline when last_validated_at is truthy (lines 85-92: the branch requires `self.last_validated_at` to be set). A relationship constructed with requires_reverification=True but last_validated_at=None (the default, e.g. via TrustManager.establish_trust_relationship which never sets last_validated_at) skips the staleness check and returns True indefinitely. Inconsistent with needs_reverification() (line 101-102) which correctly returns True for the same never-validated state. CrossDomainTrustManager._find_trust_path / _validate_trust_path gate on is_valid_at, so a never-revalidated relationship is accepted as a live trust hop forever.

findings.title: Entity home-domain inferred from first capability-matching domain (no verified binding)
findings.detail: _determine_entity_domain iterates the domain registry and returns the FIRST domain that merely supports the entity's entity_type and provider_type (lines 355-361), with a comment conceding it is a "Simple heuristic". There is no check that the entity actually belongs to that domain (no signed assertion, no SAN/issuer binding). The chosen source_domain_id then drives _find_trust_path; an entity can be silently attributed to whichever capability-compatible domain happens to be registered first, so cross-domain trust-path validation is computed against an unverified origin. Entity-to-domain binding is forgeable here.

findings.title: required_factors from the operation threshold is never enforced in the identity check
findings.detail: check() accepts required_factors: frozenset[TrustFactorType] (the operation's demanded factors, sourced from request.trust_threshold.required_factors in trust_validator.py:183) but never uses it. The comment at lines 100-104 admits the parameter is passed through but ignored — validate_minimum_trust_factors applies the engine's own global minimum set (CRYPTOGRAPHIC_PROOF + TIME_CONSISTENCY per trust_assessment_engine.py:678-681) instead. An operation requiring e.g. GOVERNMENT_ISSUED or HARDWARE_BACKED will not have that factor enforced; the per-operation factor requirement is a no-op.

findings.title: validate_minimum_trust_factors swallows all exceptions
findings.detail: validate_minimum_trust_factors wraps the factor collection in `try: ... except Exception: return False` (lines 299-319). The fail-closed direction (return False on error) is correct for this boolean, but the bare `except Exception` masks the actual failure cause from the caller and from audit — an attribute-map or repository defect that consistently throws becomes indistinguishable from a legitimately-unmet requirement. Hygiene: classify and surface the error rather than blanket-swallow.

findings.title: Entire revocation subsystem is unwired — never constructed by any application or service
findings.detail: RevocationManager, CascadeRevocationEngine, RevocationRegistry and EntityRelationshipManager are only ever referenced through __init__.py re-exports (security/__init__.py:645, layer_0_primitives/__init__.py:304, etc.). A repo-wide grep for constructions outside this directory finds none — the only `RevocationManager(...)` construction in the codebase is the unrelated `delegation_authority/service/revocation_manager.py`. This whole identity-framework revocation/relationships family is dead code: no PEP/PDP/SSO path checks `RevocationRegistry.is_entity_revoked` before trusting an identity, so revocations performed here would never be consulted.

findings.title: revoke_identity can never revoke a real entity — _get_entity_details always returns None
findings.detail: `_get_entity_details` is a stub that unconditionally `return None` (line 452, comment 'return None as placeholder'). In `revoke_identity` (line 134-138) this makes `entity` always None, so it always raises `RuntimeError('Entity ... not found')`, is caught at line 279, and returns a `RevocationResult(is_revoked=False)`. The public revoke_identity API therefore cannot ever record a revocation — it is a permanent no-op masquerading as a working revocation manager.

findings.title: Cascade impact analysis is a hardcoded no-op (relationships = [])
findings.detail: `_analyze_direct_impacts` sets `relationships = []  # Placeholder until async refactor` (line 337) and `_analyze_indirect_impacts` sets `cascade_relationships = []` (line 400), so both impact loops never execute. analyze_revocation_impact therefore always returns an analysis with empty direct/indirect impacts, zero blocking_impacts and ImpactSeverity.LOW. Consumers gating on `has_blocking_impacts()` (revocation_manager.py:178, _perform_safety_checks:477) will always see 'no blocking impacts', so the impact-based safety gate is structurally bypassed.

findings.title: Cascade execution simulates success without revoking anything
findings.detail: `_execute_immediate_cascade` (lines 554-560) and `_execute_gradual_cascade` (lines 578-602) only append `impact.affected_entity.entity_id` to `revoked_entities` inside a try/except that calls no provider ('For now, simulate successful revocation'). No revocation actually occurs, the except branch is unreachable, and execute_cascade_revocation returns total_revoked counts that are pure fiction. A caller relying on this to actually revoke dependent entities is silently no-op'd.

findings.title: _validate_prerequisite always returns True (fail-open prerequisite gate)
findings.detail: `_validate_prerequisite` unconditionally `return True  # For now, return True as placeholder` (line 620). execute_cascade_revocation (lines 205-213) iterates impact_analysis.prerequisites and only raises if a prerequisite is NOT met — but since validation always passes, any documented prerequisite (e.g. 'Establish alternative for X', 'Implement mitigation for Y' from _generate_prerequisites) is treated as satisfied and the cascade proceeds regardless.
