findings.title: _get_entity_by_id always returns None, defeating all impact assembly
findings.detail: `_get_entity_by_id` is a stub returning None (line 626, 'For now, return None'). Combined with the empty-relationships placeholders, even if relationships were populated, every affected-entity lookup would be dropped (lines 351-352, 413-414 `if not affected_entity: continue`). Several other helpers (_get_affected_operations, _get_affected_services, _estimate_relationship_downtime, _get_mitigation_strategies, _find_alternative_entities, _assess_business_impact) are likewise hardcoded placeholders returning fixed dummy values.

findings.title: RevocationRegistry persistence is a no-op despite enable_persistence defaulting True
findings.detail: Constructor defaults `enable_persistence=True` (line 32) and add_revocation_record/update_revocation_status call `self._persist_record(...)` when enabled (lines 107-108, 175-176). But `_persist_record` is `pass` ('For now, this is a no-op placeholder', line 487). All revocation records, status updates and indexes live only in process memory and are lost on restart — a durability gap that, if this subsystem were ever wired, would silently drop revocations across process boundaries while reporting persistence as on.

findings.title: RevocationManager.get_revocation_statistics discards registry stats via impossible isinstance check
findings.detail: `registry_stats = self._registry.get_revocation_statistics()` returns an `EnforcementStatistics` object (revocation_registry.py:385-444 returns `EnforcementStatistics(...)`), but the next line `if not isinstance(registry_stats, dict): registry_stats = {}` (lines 391-392) always evaluates true and replaces the real statistics with an empty dict. The `**registry_stats` spread at line 413 then contributes nothing — the registry's verified/denied/cascade/compliance counts are silently dropped from the reported statistics.

findings.title: Recent-revocations counter breaks immediately due to ascending sort order
findings.detail: `get_revocation_statistics` iterates `self._revocation_time_index` and counts entries within 7 days, with `else: break  # List is sorted, so we can stop here` (lines 405-409). But the index is sorted ascending by timestamp (oldest first — line 98 `.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])`), so the first element is the OLDEST. As soon as any record older than 7 days exists at the front of the list, the loop breaks before reaching recent records, undercounting recent_revocations (typically to 0). The early-break optimization assumes descending order.

findings.title: EntityRelationshipManager fabricates ownership-transfer trust attributes and never persists
findings.detail: transfer_ownership hardcodes `ownership_proof='cryptographic_signature'  # Placeholder`, `consent_received=True  # Assume consent obtained`, `legal_requirements_met=True  # Assume requirements met`, `transfer_authorized_by=current_owner  # Assume current owner authorizes` (lines 586-590) with no actual proof, consent, or authorization verification. Separately, all repository persistence is permanently commented out as TODO (lines 151-157, 183-189), so create/remove relationships and ownership transfers are memory-only. If this manager were ever trusted by a security decision, the fabricated consent/proof flags would be forgeable-by-default.

findings.title: Entity lookup is a hardcoded None placeholder — revocation never executes, fails silently as not-revoked
findings.detail: _get_entity_details() always returns None (line 452: 'return None as placeholder'). In revoke_identity() the entity lookup at line 134-138 therefore always raises RuntimeError('Entity ... not found'), which the broad except at line 279 swallows and returns RevocationResult(is_revoked=False). The result: the revocation manager can NEVER revoke any identity — every call fails closed-to-no-op. A compromised credential/identity submitted for revocation is silently never revoked, and the caller gets is_revoked=False which (if treated as 'already not revoked') is fail-open against the security goal.

findings.title: check_revocation_status defaults to ACTIVE (not-revoked) on lookup miss — fail-open enforcement
findings.detail: check_revocation_status() returns RevocationStatus.ACTIVE when no record id maps to the entity (line 190-192) and again when the record object is missing (line 194-196). is_entity_revoked() (line 204-215) builds on this and returns False for any entity not present in the in-memory dict. Because there is no real backing store (see _persist_record no-op), any entity the in-memory registry has not seen — including after process restart — is reported as ACTIVE/not-revoked. A revocation check is a security gate; the default on absence/uncertainty must be deny/revoked, not active.

findings.title: _persist_record is a no-op while enable_persistence defaults True — revocations are memory-only and lost on restart
findings.detail: RevocationRegistry.__init__ defaults enable_persistence=True (line 32) and add_revocation_record/update_revocation_status call _persist_record on every change (lines 107-108, 175-176), but _persist_record() is an empty placeholder ('this is a no-op placeholder', line 481-487). All revocation records live only in process memory. After a restart or in any second process, every previously revoked identity reverts to ACTIVE (per check_revocation_status fail-open), silently un-revoking compromised identities. The persistence flag advertises durability the code does not provide.

findings.title: Cascade revocation is entirely simulated — dependent tokens reported revoked but never revoked
findings.detail: The cascade engine performs no real work: _analyze_direct_impacts hardcodes relationships=[] (line 337) and _analyze_indirect_impacts hardcodes cascade_relationships=[] (line 400); _get_entity_by_id returns None (line 626); _execute_immediate_cascade and _execute_gradual_cascade merely append entity IDs to revoked_entities inside a try/except that can never throw (lines 554-562, 578-602) with the comment 'simulate successful revocation' — no provider revocation call is made. RevocationManager then reports these as cascaded_revocations on a successful RevocationResult (revocation_manager.py:250). When an upstream identity is revoked, its delegated/dependent identities and tokens are claimed revoked but remain fully valid — a delegation-revocation bypass.

findings.title: _validate_prerequisite always returns True — blocking-impact prerequisite gate is a no-op
findings.detail: execute_cascade_revocation iterates impact_analysis.prerequisites and raises a security error if _validate_prerequisite returns False (lines 205-213), but _validate_prerequisite() unconditionally 'return True' ('placeholder', line 614-620). The prerequisite safety gate that is supposed to block a cascade until alternatives/mitigations are established (prerequisites generated for blocking impacts at line 528-544) can never actually block. Combined with the simulated execution this is presently moot, but as a security control it is structurally defeated.

findings.title: RevocationEvidence is not a dataclass — score-range validation and per-instance fields are dead/broken
findings.detail: RevocationEvidence (line 15) declares class-level annotated attributes with defaults and a __post_init__ that validates reliability_score/completeness_score are in [0.0,1.0] (lines 42-50), but the class has NO @dataclass decorator (confirmed: only 'class RevocationEvidence:' at line 15, no dataclass import used on it). Therefore no __init__ is generated and __post_init__ is never invoked: the score-range validation never runs, and evidence_id/collected_at/evidence_source become mutable class attributes shared across all instances rather than per-instance fields. Evidence backing a revocation decision is unvalidated and structurally unsound (instances cannot even be constructed with the documented field semantics).

findings.title: force_revocation / bypass_prerequisites skip all safety checks with no authorization on the flag
findings.detail: revoke_identity skips the entire safety-check block when request.force_revocation or request.bypass_prerequisites is set (lines 187-191), and skips the blocking-impact gate when force_revocation is set (lines 178-181). These are plain booleans on the request dataclass (revocation_request.py:58-59) with no verification that the requester is privileged to bypass — _validate_revocation_request (line 420-444) only checks concurrency and a 30-day future-timestamp bound, never the bypass flags. Any caller able to construct a RevocationRequest can set force_revocation=True to bypass blocking-impact and safety controls (this matters once the engine is wired to do real work / for DoS via mass forced revocation).

findings.title: restore_identity un-revokes any entity with no authorization check
findings.detail: restore_identity(entity_id, restored_by: str, ...) (lines 313-358) flips a revoked record back to RevocationStatus.ACTIVE and records restored_by as a free-text string. There is no check that the caller/restored_by is authorized to reactivate a revoked (potentially compromised) identity, and no evidence requirement. A revocation is a security action; its reversal needs at least equal authority. As written, restoration of a revoked identity is an unauthenticated, unauthorized state transition.

findings.title: Non-emergency revocations blocked outside 09:00-17:00 UTC — security revocations delayed up to 16h/day
findings.detail: _perform_safety_checks blocks any non-emergency revocation when datetime.now(UTC).hour < 9 or > 17, returning passed=False reason='outside_business_hours' (lines 471-474). This causes revoke_identity to return a blocked result for routine (non-EMERGENCY) revocations during ~16 hours of every day, and ties the 'business hours' window to UTC regardless of operator locale. Routine credential/identity revocation (e.g. offboarding, suspected-but-not-confirmed compromise) is a security operation that should not be time-gated; this weakens timeliness of revocation.

findings.title: Critical-component safety check decided by free-text substring match on description
findings.detail: _perform_safety_checks gates the 'critical_system_component' protection on `entity.entity_type == SERVICE and entity.description and 'critical' in entity.description.lower()` (lines 463-468). Classifying a security-relevant property by case-insensitive substring of a free-text description is spoofable (omit the word to evade the safety gate, or trigger it accidentally) and is not a typed/authoritative criticality attribute. Criticality should be a structured, authority-resolved field, not string-sniffed.

findings.title: AgentIdentityProvider.verify_credential is a fail-open stub that always returns is_valid=True
findings.detail: verify_credential (lines 85-106) ignores the `credential` argument entirely and unconditionally builds a VerificationResult with is_valid=True / VerificationOutcome(is_valid=True). No signature, expiry, issuer-trust, or revocation check is performed. The whole class is a placeholder (create_agent_identity returns private_key='placeholder', resolve_did returns an empty doc, issue_verifiable_credential returns proof={}). It implements the IAgentIdentityProvider ABC and is publicly re-exported from `security/__init__.py`; if any caller wires it as the IAgentIdentityProvider, every credential is accepted as valid. Violates the catalog's 'NEVER trust unverified credentials' and fail-closed defaults.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier.verify_credential_signature passes valid when no DIDService is injected
findings.detail: verify_credential_signature (lines 51-103) only performs signature verification inside `if self._did_service is not None:`; when did_service is None it falls through to builder.set_validity(True) at line 102 and returns a VALID result without ever checking the signature. The constructor (line 26) defaults did_service=None, and the docstring openly states 'When no DIDService is configured, returns valid without verifying.' A credential with any/forged proof passes. Fail-open default for a security verifier.

findings.title: CredentialService.verify_credential returns success without verifying when did_service is None
findings.detail: verify_credential (lines 165-247) gates the actual signature check (get_public_key + verifying_key.verify) behind `if self._did_service is not None:` at line 237. With the constructor default did_service=None (line 43), the method skips verification and returns (True, 'Credential verification successful') at line 244 for any credential that merely has a non-empty proof.jws and is unexpired/active/non-revoked. The signature is never validated. Fail-open.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier.check_credential_revocation is a noop that always returns not-revoked
findings.detail: check_credential_revocation (lines 135-155) unconditionally sets validity True and returns; the docstring admits 'Currently always returns valid (not revoked)'. It never reads credentialStatus / StatusList2021 / a revocation registry. A revoked credential is reported as not revoked. Also note verify_credential (lines 30-49) only calls expiration+signature and never invokes revocation at all, so revocation is doubly absent from the verify path.

findings.title: CredentialService revocation registry is process-local in-memory dict (lost on restart, not authority)
findings.detail: _revoked_credentials is an in-memory dict (line 54); revoke_credential writes to it (line 278) and check_revocation_status reads it (line 308). Revocations do not persist across process restarts and are not visible to other instances/processes, so check_revocation_status returns (False, None) for a credential revoked elsewhere or before a restart — effectively fail-open for revocation in any multi-process / restarted deployment. The code comment acknowledges it 'would be replaced with persistent storage'.

findings.title: DIDAgentRegistry.register_agent is a stub that always returns success without storing or validating
findings.detail: The public ABC method register_agent (lines 68-94) ignores `did` and `agent_identity`, never persists anything, never checks for DID conflicts, and returns _create_verification_result(is_valid=True, outcome='success'). Comment at line 90-91 says 'This is a simplified stub implementation'. The real registration logic lives in the separate, differently-shaped _register_agent_internal (takes a typed SecurityAgentIdentity), which register_agent never calls. So the interface entry point is a no-op success that silently registers nothing.

findings.title: DIDService uses a process-local in-memory DID registry with no persistence
findings.detail: DIDService._did_registry (line 40) is an in-memory dict; create_did stores into it (line 104) and resolve_did/get_public_key/verify_signature all read from it (lines 125, 202). resolve_did raises 'not found' for any DID this process did not itself create, and the store is lost on restart. Comment 'will be replaced with database in Phase 3.10'. As a DID resolver/verifier this cannot resolve externally-issued DIDs and is unsuitable for production trust decisions.

findings.title: IdentityVerifier.check_credential_trust trusts solely a constructor-supplied issuer DID list with no resolution
findings.detail: check_credential_trust (lines 134-148) returns 'TRUSTED' iff issuer_did is in the in-memory self._trusted_issuers list (populated from the constructor arg, default empty, line 34) and ignores credential_type entirely. get_trusted_issuers (lines 114-132) likewise just echoes that list with hardcoded trust_level='TRUSTED' and empty credential_types; docstring says 'In production this would query an external trust registry'. No trust registry / revocation / expiry of issuer trust is consulted.

findings.title: IdentityAssertion declares attributes but is not a dataclass and has no __init__ — instances are broken
findings.detail: IdentityAssertion (lines 19-56) has only class-level annotations (assertion_id, verified_claims, cryptographic_proof, trust_level, ...) with no @dataclass decorator and no __init__. The annotations create no instance attributes, so is_cryptographically_valid() (line 40, reads self.cryptographic_proof) and is_assertion_valid() (line 50) raise AttributeError on any plain instance. The class is never constructed anywhere in production. Dead/broken security-assertion type.

findings.title: EUIDVerificationResult defaults every verification flag to True and is not a dataclass (validation never runs)
findings.detail: EUIDVerificationResult (lines 16-101) sets class-level defaults signature_valid=True, issuer_trusted=True, not_expired=True, not_revoked=True, credential_valid=True, revocation_checked=True (lines 28-33). It has a __post_init__ (line 51) but no @dataclass decorator and no __init__, so __post_init__ is never auto-invoked and the defaulted booleans are the values is_fully_verified() (line 63) reads. A default/partially-populated instance reports SUCCESS / full assurance. Fail-open-by-default verification result; never constructed in production (dead but dangerous if adopted).

findings.title: CredentialRevocationInfo has __post_init__ but no @dataclass — required fields never initialized
findings.detail: CredentialRevocationInfo (lines 16-53) carries class-level annotations (revoked_at, revoked_by, revocation_reason, ...) and a __post_init__ (line 40) but lacks @dataclass and __init__, so __post_init__ never runs and instance fields are never set; is_cascade_revocation()/has_revocation_proof() rely on attributes that don't exist on a bare instance. Same broken pattern shared by credential_claim.py, credential_presentation.py, credential_schema.py, credential_subject.py, credential_verification_request.py, digital_driving_license.py, euid_verification_request.py, euid_wallet_configuration.py, professional_qualification.py (all have __post_init__ with no dataclass decorator). None are constructed in production — dead code.

findings.title: DIDService.verify_signature / get_public_key only ever use verification_method[0] (key-selection TODO)
findings.detail: verify_signature (line 171), get_public_key (line 214) and verify_did_ownership (line 279) hardcode verification_method[0] with an explicit 'TODO: support key selection by ID'. The signature's/proof's verificationMethod (which key id signed) is ignored, so a DID document with multiple keys cannot have the correct key selected; verification silently uses the first listed key. Weakens correctness of multi-key DID verification.

findings.title: JsonWebSignatureProofVerifier never validates the protected header (alg/b64/crit) it accepts from the JWS
findings.detail: verify() takes protected_b64 straight from the wire JWS (split_detached_jws, line 47) and feeds it into the signing input (line 53) without decoding/validating that the header declares alg=ES256, b64:false, or crit=['b64']. Algorithm confusion is precluded because the verifier hardcodes the _ES256 = ECAlgorithm(SHA256) object (line 25), so this is defense-in-depth only — but a proof whose header claims a different alg/b64 would still be accepted as long as the ES256 signature over the wire header bytes matches, rather than being rejected as non-conformant to the JsonWebSignature2020 detached profile the signer emits (jws_credential_proof._PROTECTED_HEADER).

findings.title: AgentIdentityProvider.verify_credential is a stub that always returns is_valid=True (auth bypass)
findings.detail: AgentIdentityProvider implements IAgentIdentityProvider, but verify_credential() (lines 85-106) ignores the `credential` argument entirely and unconditionally constructs VerificationOutcome(is_valid=True) / VerificationResult(_is_valid=True). The body is marked `# Implementation placeholder`. Any consumer that wires this concrete in via the IAgentIdentityProvider ABC gets a verifier that accepts every credential — including unsigned, expired, or forged ones. This violates the catalog rule 'NEVER trust unverified credentials' and the no-stub rule; it is a complete credential-verification bypass behind a conformant ABC signature.

findings.title: EUIDVerificationResult / EUIDPersonalData / CredentialSubject / CredentialPresentation etc. are non-dataclass classes whose __post_init__ never runs and which cannot be constructed with field values
findings.detail: EUIDVerificationResult (line 16) declares class-level annotated fields with defaults (credential_valid: bool = True, signature_valid: bool = True, issuer_trusted: bool = True, not_revoked: bool = True, ...) and defines __post_init__ (line 51), but the class has NO @dataclass decorator and NO __init__. __post_init__ is a dataclass-only hook, so it never executes; there is no constructor to populate request_id/verified_at/verification_successful. The class is effectively a bag of class-level defaults where every verification flag is hardcoded True — is_fully_verified() (line 63) returns True for a default instance. The identical broken pattern (annotations + __post_init__, no @dataclass) is in euid_personal_data.py:15 (EUIDPersonalData, also implements IEUIDPersonalData with non-default fields after defaulted ones), credential_subject.py:16 (CredentialSubject), credential_presentation.py:15, euid_verification_request.py:16, digital_driving_license.py:17, professional_qualification.py:17. These are dead/stub value objects: they satisfy their ABCs nominally but cannot carry the data the methods read, and any validation intended in __post_init__ silently does not happen.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier.verify_credential_signature fails open when no DIDService is injected
findings.detail: verify_credential_signature() (lines 51-103) only performs cryptographic verification inside `if self._did_service is not None:` (line 88). When did_service is None it skips straight to builder.set_validity(True) / return (lines 102-103). The constructor (line 26) makes did_service optional and defaults to None, so the default-constructed verifier reports every credential's signature as VALID without checking anything. The docstring even states 'When no DIDService is configured, returns valid without verifying.' This is a fail-open trust decision violating the catalog's 'Default everywhere is DENY/raise/fail-closed; NEVER trust unverified credentials.' The same fail-open shape exists in CredentialService.verify_credential (credential_service.py:237) which returns (True, 'success') when self._did_service is None after skipping verifying_key.verify.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier/CredentialService verify against a raw base64 signature over non-canonical JSON, incompatible with the detached-JWS the signers actually produce
findings.detail: verify_credential_signature reconstructs signed bytes as json.dumps(unsigned, sort_keys=True) WITHOUT compact separators (line 80) and treats proof['jws'] as a plain base64 blob via base64.b64decode (line 83), then calls verifying_key.verify(signature_bytes, credential_bytes). The canonical signer path (jws_credential_proof.canonical_credential_bytes, line 31) uses separators=(',',':') and a *detached compact JWS* (header '..' base64url(sig)) whose signing input is `protected_b64 . canonical` (detached_signing_input, line 44), verified by JsonWebSignatureProofVerifier. The two schemes disagree on (a) JSON separators, (b) JWS framing (raw b64 vs detached three-segment), and (c) signing input (payload-only vs header+payload). A credential signed by LocalEs256CredentialSigner/KmsCredentialSigner will NOT verify here, and base64.b64decode of a real detached JWS 'h..s' would either error or decode garbage — so this verifier can only ever pass the fail-open branch or a credential signed by the legacy CredentialService.issue_credential path. CredentialService.verify_credential (credential_service.py:233) has the same non-canonical/raw-b64 mismatch. These verifiers are non-conformant with the project's single detached-JWS proof scheme.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier.check_credential_revocation always returns valid (revocation never checked)
findings.detail: check_credential_revocation() (lines 135-155) unconditionally sets validity True and returns; the docstring admits 'Currently always returns valid (not revoked)'. The catalog requires 'ALWAYS check credential expiration and revocation status' and the StatusList2021 codec (status_list_2021_codec.py) exists precisely to read revocation bits fail-closed. Worse, verify_credential() (line 30) chains expiration -> signature but never calls revocation at all, so a revoked credential passes full verification. This is a security no-op stub on the revocation path.

findings.title: DIDAgentRegistry.register_agent is a stub returning success without persisting or validating
findings.detail: register_agent() (lines 68-94) is the public IDIDAgentRegistry entry point but its body is `# This is a simplified stub implementation` and just returns _create_verification_result(is_valid=True). It never converts the agent_identity dict, never validates it, never calls the repository or in-memory store (_register_agent_internal exists at line 96 but is unreachable from this public method). A caller registering an agent through the ABC gets a success result while nothing is recorded — DID-uniqueness/conflict checks and persistence are silently skipped. Note also _create_verification_result (line 467) returns a cast SimpleVerificationResult described as 'Mock implementation' that only structurally satisfies IVerificationResult.

findings.title: DIDService uses ephemeral in-memory DID registry; resolution/verification depend on process-local state
findings.detail: DIDService stores DIDs in self._did_registry: dict (line 40, '# In-memory DID registry (will be replaced with database in Phase 3.10)'). verify_signature/get_public_key/verify_did_ownership resolve the public key only from this local dict (resolve_did, line 125), so any DID not created in the same process instance resolves to 'not found' and verification fails closed — but it also means cross-process / restart identity is lost and the registry is unauthenticated mutable state. This is acceptable-fail-closed for verification but is a non-production stub backing a security primitive; combined with get_public_key returning the first verification_method only (line 214, 'TODO: support key selection by ID'), a kid/verification-method mismatch is silently ignored rather than enforced.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier and CredentialService are credential-verification services not bound to any security ABC
findings.detail: CredentialVerifier (class declared line 14: `class CredentialVerifier:`) and CredentialService (credential_service.py:30: `class CredentialService:`) perform trust-bearing credential verification yet inherit from no ABC and use no @override, so there is no interface contract pinning their fail-closed behaviour or signature scheme. The catalog's binding table expects credential verification to implement a security_*_interfaces ABC; these free-floating services duplicate (incompatibly — see the detached-JWS finding) the JsonWebSignatureProofVerifier/IJsonWebSignatureProofVerifier contract. Untyped against an interface, callers cannot rely on a conformant verify result and the two divergent verification implementations cannot be substituted safely.

findings.title: AgentIdentityProvider.verify_credential always returns valid (no verification at all)
findings.detail: verify_credential() is an 'Implementation placeholder' (line 91 comment) that ignores the `credential` argument entirely and unconditionally returns VerificationResult(_is_valid=True) with is_valid=True / total_rules=0 / passed_rules=0 (lines 92-106). No signature check, no expiry check, no issuer/trust check. This concrete class implements IAgentIdentityProvider and is exported from `security`; any consumer routing VC verification through this provider accepts ANY credential — including forged, expired, or attacker-minted ones — as valid. Same file also returns placeholder DID documents (resolve_did, line 60) and a literal 'placeholder' private key (issue_verifiable_credential / create_agent_identity, line 49), so the whole provider is a non-functional stub masquerading as a verifier. This violates the catalog's fail-CLOSED invariant and 'NEVER trust unverified credentials'.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier silently returns VALID when no DIDService is configured (signature verification skipped)
findings.detail: verify_credential_signature() only performs cryptographic verification inside `if self._did_service is not None:` (lines 88-100); when did_service is None it falls through to builder.set_validity(True) / return (lines 102-103). The constructor makes did_service Optional and defaults to None (line 26), and the docstring states 'Without it, signature verification is skipped' (line 24). A CredentialVerifier() constructed without a DID service therefore returns is_valid=True for any credential that merely carries a base64-decodable `proof.jws` string (the only structural gate, lines 74-86) — a complete signature-verification bypass. Fail-open default contradicts the catalog 'Default everywhere is DENY/raise; no silent fallback' invariant. Should fail closed (return invalid / raise) when no key-resolution path is available.

findings.title: CredentialVerifier signature reconstruction is incompatible with the issuer's detached-JWS signing input
findings.detail: This verifier (a) reconstructs signed bytes as json.dumps(unsigned, sort_keys=True) with DEFAULT separators ', '/': ' (line 80), and (b) treats proof.jws as a bare base64 blob via base64.b64decode(jws) (line 83), then calls verifying_key.verify(signature, credential_bytes) (line 97). But the canonical signer path (jws_credential_proof.py: canonical_credential_bytes uses separators=(',',':') line 32; detached_signing_input prepends protected_b64 + b'.' line 46; assemble_detached_jws produces 'header..base64url(sig)' line 51) signs base64url-encoded compact JSON WITH the protected header, and the JWS is a 3-segment detached string, not raw base64. The two are mutually inconsistent: any credential produced by LocalEs256CredentialSigner/KmsCredentialSigner/VerifiableCredentialIssuer will FAIL here, and conversely this code only validates credentials signed by an entirely different (non-detached, wrong-separator) scheme. Verification that cannot agree with the issuer is broken crypto — it either rejects all real credentials or, combined with the None-did_service path, rubber-stamps everything.

findings.title: check_credential_revocation is a no-op that always reports 'not revoked'
findings.detail: check_credential_revocation() ignores the credential's credentialStatus / revocation registry entirely and unconditionally builder.set_validity(True) (lines 153-155); the docstring admits 'Currently always returns valid (not revoked)' (line 142). The full verify_credential() pipeline (lines 30-49) only calls expiration then signature and never calls revocation, so a revoked credential is accepted. The catalog requires the calling service to check replay/revocation and the status codec is explicitly fail-closed; here the revocation gate is a stub that defeats StatusList2021 entirely. Violates 'ALWAYS check credential expiration and revocation status'.

findings.title: DIDService resolves DIDs only from an in-memory registry it populates itself — no did:web/did:key resolution, trust anchored on local self-issued keys
findings.detail: resolve_did() (lines 109-137) and thus get_public_key()/verify_signature()/verify_did_ownership() resolve exclusively against self._did_registry (line 40), an in-memory dict populated only by this process's own create_did() (line 104). There is no resolution of an external did:web document or derivation from a did:key — so signature verification trusts whatever public key the SAME process generated, never the actual issuer's published key. An attacker who can get any DID document registered (or who controls the credential's `issuer` value pointing at a self-registered DID) controls the verifying key. The module docstring of the proper verifier (json_web_signature_proof_verifier.py lines 5-7) explicitly says issuer-key resolution from did:web/did:key is 'the caller's concern', but DIDService.get_public_key provides no such resolution — making any CredentialService/CredentialVerifier wired to it verify against forgeable local keys.

findings.title: DIDAgentRegistry.register_agent is a stub that returns success without persisting or validating
findings.detail: The public register_agent() (lines 68-94) is 'a simplified stub implementation' (comment line 90) that ignores the `did` and `agent_identity` arguments and returns _create_verification_result(is_valid=True, 'success') without converting/validating the identity, checking DID collision, or writing to the repository/in-memory store. Callers receive a successful IVerificationResult while no registration occurred and no validation (e.g. DIDAlreadyExistsError dedupe that the internal path enforces at line 130) ran. A registry whose public registration call is a no-op success can cause downstream lookups to silently miss or allow duplicate/forged DIDs depending on which path is invoked.

findings.title: VerifiableCredentialIssuer.revoke_credential returns success without revoking when no status allocator is configured
findings.detail: revoke_credential() returns True immediately when self._status_allocator is None (lines 149-150), described as 'local-intent stub behaviour ... without touching any bitstring'. A caller revoking a credential gets a True (success) result while the credential's StatusList2021 bit is never set, so subsequent verifications still see it as not-revoked. A revocation API that reports success but performs no revocation is a fail-open of the revocation control; it should fail closed (return False / raise) when it cannot actually effect revocation.

findings.title: CredentialVerificationRequest declares security-defaulting fields but is not a dataclass — __post_init__ never runs and class attributes are shared mutable defaults
findings.detail: The class (lines 17-67) uses class-level annotations with defaults (check_revocation=True, verify_signature=True, verify_issuer=True, etc., lines 39-42) and a __post_init__ (lines 43-48) plus object.__setattr__ as if it were a frozen @dataclass, but it carries NO @dataclass decorator. Consequences: __post_init__ is never invoked by Python (no dataclass-generated __init__ calls it), so required_claims/verification_context are never initialized per-instance; and the mutable/boolean defaults live as CLASS attributes shared across all instances. required_claims default of None means has_specific_claim_requirements()/requires_comprehensive_verification() (lines 50-66) read class state, and the security toggles cannot be reliably set per request. A verification-policy object whose 'verify_signature/verify_issuer/check_revocation' flags are unreliable class globals can silently weaken the verification a consumer believes it requested.

findings.title: JsonWebSignatureProofVerifier does not validate the proof's protected header (alg / b64 / crit) before verifying
findings.detail: verify() splits the detached JWS and uses protected_b64 only as opaque signing-input bytes (lines 47-53) but never base64url-decodes the protected header to confirm alg=='ES256', b64==false, or that 'b64' is in crit. Algorithm confusion is structurally precluded because the verifier hardcodes ECAlgorithm(SHA256) (line 25) and the header is bound into the signed bytes (so tampering breaks the signature), so this is not directly exploitable. However, per RFC 7797 a verifier MUST reject a JWS whose crit contains an unrecognized member or whose header it does not understand; silently accepting any header that happens to round-trip the signature is weaker than the spec requires and should assert the expected header shape. Low because not exploitable given the fixed-alg/bound-header design.

findings.title: OIDC ID-token validation uses unverified peek_claims — no signature check (auth bypass)
findings.detail: validate_id_token() is the trust-deciding entry point but decodes the ID token via self._codec.peek_claims(id_token) at line 319. peek_claims is the UNVERIFIED codec path (security_interfaces ijwt_codec.py:122 'diagnostic reads only'; jwt_codec.py:112-115 passes options={'verify_signature': False}). The method then enforces iss/aud/nonce/exp on these unverified claims (lines 334-369) and returns them as 'validated claims dictionary' (line 369). No call to decode_and_verify, no JWKS/public-key lookup, no signature verification exists anywhere in the method — the metadata cache holds jwks_uri but it is never used to fetch a key. An attacker can mint an ID token with any iss/aud/sub and forged signature; all claim pre-filters pass because they are read from the attacker-controlled payload. This is a complete OIDC ID-token forgery / authentication bypass, directly violating the catalog rule 'peek_claims must NEVER feed a trust decision'.

findings.title: SAML signature check is a presence-only stub — any forged assertion is treated as signed
findings.detail: _check_signature() (lines 267-277) discards the signing certificate (`_ = signing_certificate_pem`, line 276) and returns True whenever a <ds:Signature> element is merely PRESENT in the XML (`assertion_doc.select_ns('.//ds:Signature', _NAMESPACES) is not None`). It performs no XML-DSig cryptographic verification. The resulting signature_valid=True flows onto the parsed ISAMLAssertion (line 86/111). SAMLAuthenticationProvider.handle_authentication_response() sets is_valid=assertion.signature_valid (saml_authentication_provider.py:202) and validate_assertion() returns assertion_data.signature_valid (saml_authentication_provider.py:271). An attacker can submit a fully self-authored SAML Response containing any unsigned/garbage <ds:Signature> element and it is accepted as a valid, signed assertion — a complete SAML authentication bypass. The catalog requires the adapter to actually validate XML-DSig when a signing cert is supplied.

findings.title: SAML provider disables signature verification when no config is set
findings.detail: parse_saml_response() computes signing_cert = self._configuration.signing_certificate if self._configuration is not None else '' (lines 243-247) and passes it to the adapter. Per the catalog, signing_certificate_pem == '' disables signature verification and is permitted ONLY in test/dev. A SAMLAuthenticationProvider constructed with config=None (the default, __init__ line 76) silently runs with signature verification disabled while still passing validate_signature=True (line 160), so callers believe assertions are signature-checked. Fail-open default: no IdP cert => no verification, yet the flow still produces an authenticated session.

findings.title: SAML assertion validity window silently widened by default-on-missing conditions
findings.detail: _parse_conditions() returns default_not_before=now / default_not_on_or_after=now+1h when the <saml:Conditions> element is absent (line 201-202) and individually defaults missing NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter attributes to now / now+1h (lines 207-216). SAMLAuthenticationProvider.validate_assertion() then enforces only `now < not_before` / `now >= not_on_or_after` (saml_authentication_provider.py:266-269) against these defaulted values. An assertion with no Conditions (or missing time bounds) is treated as valid for a fresh one-hour window instead of being rejected, silently widening the trust window — exactly the default-on-missing hazard the catalog warns about. Combined with the absence of any replay/InResponseTo/one-time-use dedupe in the provider, a captured or forged assertion remains broadly replayable.

findings.title: OIDC nonce binding not enforced; only checked when caller supplies it
findings.detail: validate_id_token() only verifies nonce when the caller passes one: `if nonce and payload.get('nonce') != nonce` (line 350). If the caller omits nonce (it is Optional, default None), the ID-token nonce that binds the token to the original authn request is never checked, defeating the OIDC replay/binding protection the catalog requires ('the calling service validates nonce on the ID token'). The method also never validates iat / issued-at sanity. Because the provider generates a nonce when building the auth URL (line 154-155) but stores it only in _pkce_sessions keyed by a throwaway session id, there is no path that feeds the issued nonce back into validate_id_token, so in practice the binding is unenforced.

findings.title: PKCE verifier lookup returns first stored verifier regardless of authorization code
findings.detail: _get_stored_pkce_verifier() ignores the authorization_code argument and returns the FIRST value in _pkce_sessions: `for session_data in self._pkce_sessions.values(): return session_data['code_verifier']` (lines 540-543, comment 'Simplified implementation'). With concurrent flows this returns the wrong verifier (breaking the PKCE binding between this code and its challenge) and _cleanup_pkce_session() (lines 545-555) never deletes the session for the code just exchanged — it only garbage-collects entries older than 10 minutes, so PKCE verifiers are reusable within that window. The PKCE code-to-verifier binding the catalog relies on is effectively not maintained.

findings.title: JWKS endpoint generator is a permanent empty stub (dead JWKS)
findings.detail: _generate_jwks() always returns {'keys': []} (lines 359-399). The real key iteration is entirely commented out with TODOs ('JWTTokenManager needs get_public_keys() method'). get_jwks() therefore publishes an empty key set even when a jwt_manager is injected, so any relying party that fetches this provider's JWKS to verify tokens gets no keys — the advertised 'JWT signature verification' security feature (get_provider_info lines 462-468) is non-functional. Dead/stub security primitive.

findings.title: SAML provider hardcodes ACS/SP URLs and placeholder metadata
findings.detail: initiate_authentication() builds the AuthnRequest with a hardcoded ACS URL `URL.from_string('https://your-sp.example.com/saml/acs')` (line 123), and get_sp_metadata() emits placeholder entityID 'urn:example:sp' and your-sp.example.com ACS/SLO URLs (lines 430-441). create_authn_request_xml (line 469) and create_logout_request (line 380) emit minimal XML that ignores destination, RelayState, ForceAuthn, requested_auth_context and does not sign the request despite SP metadata advertising AuthnRequestsSigned='true'. These are non-production stubs that, if deployed, point the SAML flow at an example domain — a misconfiguration / dead-wire hazard rather than a direct bypass.

findings.title: SAML assurance level and MFA-usage are hardcoded constants
findings.detail: _determine_assurance_level() always returns SSOAssuranceLevel.FAL2 (line 548) and _check_mfa_usage() always returns False (line 551), ignoring the assertion's AuthnContextClassRef entirely. handle_authentication_response() propagates these into SSOAuthenticationResult.assurance_level / mfa_used (lines 200-201). Downstream policy that trusts assurance_level=FAL2 is being fed a fabricated constant unrelated to what the IdP actually asserted, and mfa_used=False under-reports MFA. No-op security classification.

findings.title: Enterprise SSO default IP allowlist is 0.0.0.0/0 and _is_ip_allowed short-circuits to allow-all
findings.detail: The default policy sets allowed_ip_ranges={'0.0.0.0/0'} (line 81) and _is_ip_allowed() returns True if allowed_ranges is None (line 705-706) or contains '0.0.0.0/0' (line 708-709); otherwise it does a plain string membership test (`ip_address in allowed_ranges`, line 711) rather than CIDR matching, so any non-trivial CIDR range silently never matches a real client IP. The combination means IP restriction is either fully open (default) or, once a real range is configured, broken — the comment even says 'in production use proper IP network verification'. Weak/fail-open network guard.

findings.title: Session security-context drift only produces warnings; session stays valid
findings.detail: validate_session() collects warnings from _validate_security_context() for trust-level, device-trust, location-trust and authentication-strength CHANGES during a session (lines 507-527) but returns (True, warnings) regardless (line 239) — only expiry, non-ACTIVE status and idle timeout cause a False. A session whose device/location trust or auth strength downgrades mid-session (a classic hijack indicator) remains valid; the warnings are advisory only and risk is re-baselined into the session (line 233-234 updates last_accessed and overwrites security_context). Weakened continuous-verification guard.

findings.title: OIDC validate_id_token never verifies the ID-token signature (peek_claims is UNVERIFIED)
findings.detail: validate_id_token() (implementing IOIDCProvider.validate_id_token, whose docstring promises 'Validates ID token signature, claims, and security requirements') extracts claims via self._codec.peek_claims(id_token). jwt_codec.peek_claims runs with options={"verify_signature": False} (jwt_codec.py:115), so the JWS signature is NEVER checked. The method only re-reads iss/aud/exp/nonce from the unverified payload (lines 334-366) and returns them as 'validated claims'. The catalog's OIDC invariant requires the calling service to validate signature on the ID token; this is THE calling service and it feeds an unverified peek directly into a trust decision. An attacker can forge an ID token with any iss/aud/sub (matching the configured client_id/issuer, which are public) and self-sign or leave it unsigned; it will be accepted. This is a full OIDC authentication bypass.

findings.title: OIDC PKCE verifier is not bound to the authorization code (returns first stored verifier)
findings.detail: _get_stored_pkce_verifier(authorization_code) ignores its argument and returns session_data['code_verifier'] for the FIRST entry in self._pkce_sessions (lines 540-543, comment 'Simplified implementation'). exchange_code_for_tokens relies on this when no verifier is passed (line 217). With concurrent flows the verifier of an unrelated session is sent to the token endpoint, defeating the per-request PKCE binding the create_authorization_url path sets up (S256 challenge stored per session_id at line 145). The class docstring and create_authorization_url advertise 'mandatory PKCE'; the binding it provides is broken.

findings.title: SAML handle_authentication_response stores session and returns result without rejecting invalid signature or expired conditions
findings.detail: handle_authentication_response() parses the assertion (line 159) then unconditionally builds SSOAuthenticationResult and stores it in self._active_sessions (lines 180-205). It sets is_valid=assertion.signature_valid (line 202) but never gates on it: a session with signature_valid=False is still stored and returned, and the conditions window (not_before/not_on_or_after) is never checked here. The catalog states the calling SSO service MUST gate on signature_valid and enforce the validity window before trusting the assertion; this consumer does neither, so a forged/expired assertion yields a live session whose only signal is an is_valid flag callers may ignore.

findings.title: SAML signature 'validation' is a stub: signature_valid is True whenever a ds:Signature element is present
findings.detail: _check_signature() (saml_assertion_adapter.py:266-277) discards signing_certificate_pem ('_ = signing_certificate_pem') and returns assertion_doc.select_ns('.//ds:Signature') is not None — i.e. signature_valid is True merely because a <ds:Signature> XML element exists, with NO cryptographic XML-DSig verification against the IdP cert. The catalog asserts the adapter 'parses & validates XML-DSig when a signing cert is supplied'; it does not. SAMLAuthenticationProvider.parse_saml_response (line 248) consumes this and propagates the bogus signature_valid into the SSO trust path. An attacker submits an unsigned-but-Signature-element-bearing Response and it is treated as signed. (Adapter lives outside the target dirs but is the integrity root for the in-scope SAML provider.)

findings.title: SAML conditions default to a valid now..now+1h window when Conditions/NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter are missing
findings.detail: _parse_conditions() sets default_not_before=now and default_not_on_or_after=now+1h (lines 197-198) and returns those defaults when the Conditions element is absent (line 202) or when individual NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter attributes are missing (lines 207-215). SAMLAuthenticationProvider.validate_assertion (saml_authentication_provider.py:264-269) then checks now against these defaulted bounds and passes. The catalog explicitly warns a missing condition 'must not silently widen the window in a trust decision'; here a missing-condition assertion is granted a fresh 1-hour validity window instead of failing closed.

findings.title: OIDCDiscoveryService builds endpoints with urljoin and an absolute leading-slash path, dropping any issuer base path
findings.detail: get_openid_configuration composes endpoints via urljoin(str(self._base_url), "/auth") etc. (lines 82-97, 180, 267-296). Because the second arg starts with '/', urljoin discards the base path: urljoin('https://idp.example.com/tenant1/','/auth') -> 'https://idp.example.com/auth'. For any issuer hosted under a path prefix the published authorization/token/jwks/userinfo URLs point at the wrong (host-root) location, and the advertised 'issuer' (self._issuer, line 81) will not match the prefix of these endpoints — an OIDC discovery-document consistency defect.

findings.title: OIDCDiscoveryService advertises insecure response types, alg 'none', and token_endpoint_auth 'none' in the discovery document
findings.detail: The static discovery document advertises implicit/hybrid response_types ('id_token','id_token token','code token', etc.) (lines 85-92) contrary to the OAuth 2.1 / mandatory-PKCE posture the same file claims (code_challenge_methods_supported=['S256'], line 141), and lists userinfo_signing_alg_values_supported including 'none' (line 156). Publishing 'none' as an acceptable signing alg and enabling implicit-flow response types in the provider metadata invites algorithm-downgrade and token-in-fragment exposure for any client that trusts this metadata. (Generation-side; no signature on the doc, so clients rely on these advertised values.)

findings.title: OIDCDiscoveryService.get_jwks always returns an empty key set (TODO stub) — verifiers get no keys
findings.detail: _generate_jwks() returns {"keys": []} unconditionally: the real key-iteration body is commented out with 'TODO: JWTTokenManager needs get_public_keys()' (lines 359-399), and even when a jwt_manager is injected no keys are emitted. get_jwks (line 209) caches and serves this empty set. A relying party performing JWKS-based verification against this provider obtains zero keys and cannot verify any token — the discovery surface claims 'JWT signature verification' as a security feature (get_provider_info line 466) that the JWKS endpoint cannot back.

findings.title: OIDC provider metadata cache uses a 1-hour TTL despite catalog's MUST-NOT-cache discovery invariant; cache key is raw str
findings.detail: discover_provider_metadata caches typed metadata in self._provider_metadata_cache keyed by raw issuer_url.url (str) with a 3600s freshness check (lines 431-450), and validate_id_token reads issuer/JWKS expectations from this cache (line 305). The OIDC discovery invariant in the catalog says the discovery adapter MUST NOT cache; caching the issuer->metadata binding for an hour at the provider layer means IdP key/issuer rotation is not observed for up to an hour. The cache also keys on a bare URL string and the cached branch (line 438) returns only {'issuer', 'from_cache'}, an inconsistent shape versus the full-metadata branch (lines 453-466).

findings.title: OIDC ID token validated via UNVERIFIED peek_claims — signature never checked (auth bypass)
findings.detail: validate_id_token() builds its entire trust payload from self._codec.peek_claims(id_token) (line 319), then derives iss/aud/nonce/exp checks and RETURNS the claims dict (line 369) as if validated. peek_claims is defined in jwt_codec.py:112-117 as jwt.decode(..., options={'verify_signature': False}) — it explicitly does NOT verify the JWS signature, and the catalog states it 'must NEVER feed a trust decision'. The provider has metadata.jwks_uri available (discover_provider_metadata returns it) and the codec exposes decode_and_verify(token, verification_key, algorithm, ...), but neither the JWKS key nor decode_and_verify is ever invoked here. An attacker can mint an ID token with arbitrary iss/aud/sub/nonce (and even alg=none) and have validate_id_token accept it, since only the unsigned payload fields are compared. This is a full OIDC authentication bypass.

findings.title: SAML provider trusts assertions whose signature_valid only means a <ds:Signature> element is present
findings.detail: handle_authentication_response() calls parse_saml_response(..., validate_signature=True) and then constructs an SSOAuthenticationResult with is_valid=assertion.signature_valid (line 202) and a live session — but never raises or rejects when signature_valid is False. The signature_valid attribute is produced by SAMLAssertionAdapter._check_signature (saml_assertion_adapter.py:266-277), which is a stub: it returns True merely when a .//ds:Signature element EXISTS and ignores signing_certificate_pem entirely (the cert arg is discarded with `_ = signing_certificate_pem`). No XML-DSig cryptographic verification occurs. An attacker submitting a forged SAML Response that contains any (self-signed/garbage) <ds:Signature> element gets signature_valid=True and a fully authenticated session bound to the attacker-chosen NameID/subject. SAML authentication bypass.

findings.title: PKCE verifier lookup returns an arbitrary stored verifier, ignoring the authorization code
findings.detail: _get_stored_pkce_verifier(authorization_code) ignores authorization_code and returns the FIRST verifier in self._pkce_sessions ('for session_data in self._pkce_sessions.values(): return session_data["code_verifier"]', lines 540-543). exchange_code_for_tokens (line 217) falls back to this when no code_verifier is passed. Because verifiers are never keyed to the authorization code (or to state) and any caller's code reuses whatever verifier happens to be first, PKCE provides no per-flow binding: a code obtained in one flow can be redeemed with a verifier minted for a different flow. This defeats the CSRF/code-injection protection PKCE is meant to provide. Compounded by _cleanup_pkce_session (line 545) only deleting >600s-old entries, so used verifiers linger.

findings.title: OIDC state/nonce generated but never persisted for callback verification (no CSRF check)
findings.detail: create_authorization_url auto-generates state and nonce when the caller omits them (lines 154-159), but the generated nonce is discarded (only state is stored in the PKCE session at line 145) and the provider exposes NO callback method that compares the state returned by the IdP against the issued state. The only nonce check is validate_id_token line 350: 'if nonce and payload.get("nonce") != nonce' — it (a) is skipped entirely when the caller passes nonce=None, (b) compares against a caller-supplied value rather than the server-issued generated nonce (which was thrown away), and (c) uses plain != on raw strings rather than a constant-time compare. Net effect: when the provider auto-generates these values there is no enforced CSRF (state) or token-replay (nonce) binding, contrary to OAuth 2.1 / OIDC requirements.

findings.title: validate_id_token audience check rejects RFC 7519 array audiences and trusts unsigned aud
findings.detail: Audience validation (lines 342-347) compares payload.get('aud') (a single string drawn from the UNVERIFIED peek_claims payload) by equality to the configured client_id. Two problems: (1) since the source is peek_claims, the aud is attacker-controllable (see the crit finding); (2) the check does not handle the RFC 7519 case where aud is a JSON array, and does not enforce the azp/single-audience rules — a token whose aud is a list will be coerced/compared incorrectly. Because this runs on unverified claims, it is defense-in-theatre rather than a real audience binding.

findings.title: SAML assertion default validity window silently set to [now, now+1h] when Conditions absent
findings.detail: _parse_conditions (lines 196-216) substitutes default_not_before=now and default_not_on_or_after=now+1h when the SAML <Conditions> element or its NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter attributes are missing. The in-scope SAMLAuthenticationProvider.validate_assertion (saml_authentication_provider.py:264-269) then enforces the window using these defaulted values, so an assertion with NO Conditions passes the time-validity gate for a full hour. A missing condition must not silently widen the trust window; absence of NotOnOrAfter should fail closed, not default to a 1-hour-valid assertion. (Adapter is the source of the defaulted value; the provider is the in-scope consumer that makes the trust decision on it.)

findings.title: SAML response handling does not enforce Conditions window, audience, or replay before creating a session
findings.detail: handle_authentication_response (lines 154-205) parses the assertion and immediately builds a session + SSOAuthenticationResult. It never calls validate_assertion and never checks NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter, the Audience restriction, or assertion ID / InResponseTo one-time-use replay protection. The catalog explicitly requires the SSO service to enforce the validity window, audience, and replay because the adapter makes no trust decision. As written, an expired, wrong-audience, or replayed SAML assertion (that merely contains a <ds:Signature> element) yields a valid authenticated session. _create_session_info (line 506) even sets the session expiry to assertion.not_on_or_after without first validating it is in the future.

findings.title: Enterprise SSO manager creates sessions without checking auth_result.is_valid
findings.detail: create_session() -> _validate_session_creation (lines 404-457) checks IP allow-list, concurrent-session count, and authentication strength, but NEVER inspects auth_result.is_valid (the field SAMLAuthenticationProvider sets from signature_valid) nor any signature/assertion-validity flag. It unconditionally builds and stores an EnterpriseSession from the supplied SSOAuthenticationResult (lines 135-167). Combined with the SAML signature stub, an unverified/forged authentication result is promoted to a trusted enterprise session. The trust gate that should reject is_valid==False is absent.

findings.title: OIDC discovery metadata cached without verifying issuer matches requested issuer URL
findings.detail: discover_provider_metadata (lines 444-450) fetches provider metadata via the adapter and caches it keyed by the raw issuer URL, but never asserts that metadata.issuer equals the requested issuer_url (OIDC Discovery requires the returned 'issuer' to byte-match the issuer used for discovery to prevent issuer-spoofing / mix-up attacks). validate_id_token later trusts metadata.issuer (line 335) as the expected issuer. If a malicious/mis-redirected discovery document returns a different issuer, that attacker-chosen issuer becomes the value every subsequent ID-token issuer check is measured against, neutralizing the issuer binding.

findings.title: Nonce comparison in validate_id_token uses non-constant-time equality
findings.detail: The nonce check (line 350: payload.get('nonce') != nonce) compares the security-sensitive nonce with Python's '!=' on raw strings rather than a constant-time comparison (e.g. hmac.compare_digest). The catalog requires constant-time matching for CSRF/replay tokens (IOAuthState.matches). Low severity given the dominating crit (claims are unverified anyway), but the comparison should be constant-time once real verification is in place.

findings.title: OIDCSessionManager.validate_token_session uses unsalted SHA-256(token) as the lookup key
findings.detail: _hash_token (lines 533-537) stores/looks up tokens as bare hashlib.sha256(token).hexdigest() with no per-session salt/HMAC key. _session_tokens maps these digests to session IDs and validate_token_session (line 347) trusts a digest match to resolve a session. While SHA-256 of a high-entropy bearer token is not trivially reversible, an unkeyed digest allows offline confirmation/correlation if the in-memory map ever leaks and provides no domain separation between access and refresh tokens beyond the explicit type check at lines 358-367. Prefer an HMAC with a process secret. Informational/low given tokens are high-entropy.

findings.title: OIDC ID-token "validation" never verifies the signature — peek_claims (unverified) feeds a trust decision
findings.detail: validate_id_token() peeks claims via self._codec.peek_claims(id_token) (line 319), which the codec implements as jwt.decode(..., options={'verify_signature': False}) (jwt_codec.py:113-116). It then validates iss/aud/nonce/exp from those UNVERIFIED claims and returns payload as 'validated claims dictionary' (line 369). No decode_and_verify / JWKS signature check is ever performed against the provider's signing key, despite the cached metadata.jwks_uri being available. The catalog says peek_claims must NEVER feed a trust decision. An attacker can mint an ID token with any iss/aud/sub/nonce and it passes validate_id_token unchanged — complete OIDC authentication bypass.

findings.title: JWTTokenManager.validate_refresh_token falls back to self-generated key when JWKS lookup fails (fail-open key resolution)
findings.detail: In validate_refresh_token (lines 438-447), when a JWKS client is configured but peek_key_id / get_public_key throws, the except branch logs a warning and sets public_key = self._public_key, then proceeds to decode_and_verify against the LOCAL self-generated key. The catalog mandates fail-closed key resolution: JWKS unreachable / unknown kid must become UNKNOWN_KID and never proceed. introspect_token (lines 930-934) deliberately does NOT do this fallback and documents why; validate_refresh_token contradicts that and lets a token signed by the wrong (local) key be treated as valid when the real issuer key is momentarily unavailable.

findings.title: JWTTokenManagerValidated.validate_token_binding is a no-op that always returns True
findings.detail: validate_token_binding() (lines 672-689) ignores token, client_proof, and binding_method and unconditionally `return True`. This is a security-relevant verification (sender-constrained / DPoP-style proof) that always passes. Any caller relying on it to confirm proof-of-possession gets an unconditional ALLOW. Combined with bind_token_to_client (lines 653-670) which returns the original token unchanged, the entire token-binding feature is a noop that silently advertises success rather than fail-closed or raise not-implemented.

findings.title: OIDC _get_stored_pkce_verifier returns an arbitrary stored verifier, defeating PKCE binding
findings.detail: _get_stored_pkce_verifier(authorization_code) ignores the authorization_code and returns the FIRST verifier in self._pkce_sessions (lines 540-543: 'for session_data in self._pkce_sessions.values(): return session_data["code_verifier"]'). When exchange_code_for_tokens is called without an explicit code_verifier, it resolves whatever verifier happens to be stored, not the one bound to this flow's code/state. With concurrent flows this binds the wrong verifier (or a verifier the attacker can influence) — PKCE no longer proves the code-exchanging client initiated the authorization, defeating the PKCE authorization-code-injection protection. The inline comment even flags 'Simplified implementation - in production, properly map codes to verifiers'.

findings.title: SAML handle_authentication_response builds a full success result regardless of signature validity
findings.detail: handle_authentication_response parses the response and constructs SSOAuthenticationResult populated with subject, attributes, roles, groups, email, and only reflects signature validity as data: is_valid=assertion.signature_valid (line 202). It does NOT raise or deny when signature_valid is False; the result object otherwise looks fully authenticated (user_id=assertion.subject, etc.). Per the catalog the calling SSO service must gate on signature_valid, but this provider returns an authenticated-looking result that a careless consumer (e.g. EnterpriseSSOManager.create_session, which does not check auth_result.is_valid) will trust. Default-on-missing in the adapter's condition parse plus no in-provider gate makes this a fail-open path.

findings.title: EnterpriseSSOManager.create_session never checks auth_result.is_valid before creating a trusted session
findings.detail: create_session() (lines 87-180) accepts an SSOAuthenticationResult and calls _validate_session_creation (IP range, concurrent-session, MFA-strength checks) but never inspects auth_result.is_valid / signature validity. Given SAMLAuthenticationProvider returns results with is_valid=False when the assertion signature is invalid (see saml_authentication_provider.py:202), this consumer creates a fully active EnterpriseSession (status ACTIVE, user_id=auth_result.user_id) from an unverified/invalid authentication. The two components together form an end-to-end fail-open: invalid SAML assertion -> result object -> active enterprise session.

findings.title: OAuth21Provider redirect-URI wildcard match allows arbitrary host via suffix matching
findings.detail: _matches_wildcard_redirect_uri (lines 453-476) splits a registered pattern on a single '*' and matches with redirect_uri_str.startswith(prefix) and .endswith(suffix). The '*' is meant to wildcard only the port (e.g. http://localhost:*/callback), but startswith/endswith places no constraint on what the '*' spans: 'http://localhost:' is a prefix and '/callback' is a suffix, so 'http://localhost:@evil.com/callback' or 'http://localhost:anything.evil/callback' also match. There is no parse of the URL authority, so a registered localhost port-wildcard pattern can be satisfied by attacker-controlled hosts, enabling redirect-URI/open-redirect bypass and code exfiltration.

findings.title: OAuth21Provider.start_authorization_flow hardcodes the authorization base URL (TODO, not configurable)
findings.detail: The authorization URL is built from a hardcoded 'https://auth.example.com/oauth/authorize' (line 189, '# TODO: Make configurable'); get_authorization_server_metadata() (lines 872-901) likewise hardcodes issuer/authorization_endpoint/token_endpoint/jwks_uri to auth.example.com. In production this either points users at a non-existent/wrong authorization server or, if example.com is registrable in some environment, sends the state/PKCE-bearing redirect to an unintended host. The provider is effectively a stub for real deployment yet exposes no signal that the endpoints are placeholders.

findings.title: PKCEVerificationBusinessRule accepts PLAIN method (records advisory violation but still returns True)
findings.detail: _validate_pkce_method (lines 157-168): for PKCEMethod.PLAIN it appends 'PLAIN method is discouraged ... use S256' to violations but then returns True, and the comment states 'We log warning but don't fail for compatibility'. Because the appended string lands in the violations list, the overall evaluate() will actually fail the rule for PLAIN — but the method's own boolean return is inconsistent with its side effect, and the design intent ('don't fail for compatibility') would, if violations were not shared, permit PLAIN. OAuth 2.1 (per catalog) requires S256 and PLAIN should be rejected in production verification, not merely annotated.

findings.title: OAuth21Provider.revoke_token returns True (success) for access tokens and on invalid refresh IDs without actually revoking
findings.detail: revoke_token (lines 586-632): for access tokens it does no revocation and returns True ('we rely on short expiry rather than revocation', line 629-630); for refresh tokens whose wire value is not a valid TokenID it returns True ('already effectively revoked', line 619). Reporting success without performing or recording revocation means a relying party that calls revoke_token to neutralize a leaked access token gets a false confirmation; the access token remains usable until natural expiry. This is a noop masquerading as success rather than an explicit unsupported-operation signal.

findings.title: PKCEHandlerValidated.generate_code_challenge has dead unreachable PKCEChallenge return after raise_error
findings.detail: In the unsupported-method branch (lines 74-80) error.raise_error() raises, then `return PKCEChallenge(challenge="", method=cast(PKCEMethod, method))` is dead code. Harmless but it constructs an empty-challenge object that, were raise_error ever to not raise, would yield a PKCEChallenge with empty challenge string — a latent fail-open shape. Worth removing so the fail-closed contract is unambiguous (PKCEHandler in pkce_handler.py:79 uses a bare `raise` instead, which is the correct pattern).

findings.title: JWTTokenManager.create_access_token / create_refresh_token are hard RuntimeError stubs
findings.detail: create_access_token (lines 228-232) and create_refresh_token (lines 281-285) unconditionally raise RuntimeError('... requires EntityIdentity repository ...'). These are non-functional stubs in a concrete ITokenManager. While they fail closed (raise rather than mint), OAuth21Provider.exchange_authorization_code and get_client_credentials_token call self._token_manager.create_access_token expecting a real token; if wired to this manager the whole token-exchange path is dead. The sibling JWTTokenManagerValidated implements these for real, so this is an unwired/half-implemented class that callers must avoid — a dead-wire hazard rather than an exploit.

findings.title: validate_id_token trusts UNVERIFIED peek_claims as the validated claim set (signature never checked)
findings.detail: validate_id_token() calls self._codec.peek_claims(id_token) (line 319) — which the normative catalog defines as UNVERIFIED, diagnostic-read-only and 'must NEVER feed a trust decision' — then builds `payload` from those claims and validates only iss/aud/nonce/exp against them (lines 334-369), returning `payload` as the validated result. The ID-token signature is never verified against the provider's JWKS/metadata key. An attacker who forges an unsigned (or wrongly-signed) ID token with the correct iss/aud/nonce/exp passes validate_id_token() and is treated as authenticated. This is the OIDC analogue of alg:none / signature bypass. decode_and_verify with a JWKS-resolved key is required here, not peek_claims.

findings.title: exchange_authorization_code issues tokens without authenticating the client (client_secret never verified)
findings.detail: OAuth21Provider.exchange_authorization_code() (lines 196-295) takes ClientCredentials but only uses client_credentials.client_id (for code/redirect match at line 230 and audience at line 255). It never verifies client_credentials.client_secret / client_assertion against the registered ClientInfo. The same omission exists in get_client_credentials_token() (lines 677-743), which only checks `client_id in self._client_registry` (line 702) before minting a token. For confidential clients this means anyone who knows a client_id and can present a (PKCE-matched) code, or just a known client_id for the client-credentials grant, obtains tokens. Client authentication is mandated by RFC 6749 §2.3 / §4.1.3.

findings.title: _get_stored_pkce_verifier returns the first verifier of ANY session, not the one bound to the authorization code
findings.detail: _get_stored_pkce_verifier(authorization_code) ignores its authorization_code argument and returns the code_verifier of whatever session iterates first (`for session_data in self._pkce_sessions.values(): return session_data['code_verifier']`, lines 540-543). PKCE's binding of a flow's verifier to its own authorization code is destroyed: a concurrent/attacker session's verifier can be substituted, defeating the PKCE protection that exchange_code_for_tokens relies on (line 217). The code comment even admits 'Simplified implementation'. PKCE for OAuth 2.1 must bind verifier↔code per the catalog.

findings.title: Authorization-code wildcard redirect_uri matching is substring-based and bypassable
findings.detail: _matches_wildcard_redirect_uri() (lines 453-476) splits a pattern like 'http://localhost:*/callback' on '*' and accepts any redirect_uri where `startswith(prefix) and endswith(suffix)`. The wildcard is not constrained to a port: 'http://localhost:evil.com/x/callback' satisfies prefix 'http://localhost:' and suffix '/callback', as does 'http://localhost:1/callback?next=//evil/callback' style values. Loose redirect-URI matching enables authorization-code / token redirection (open-redirect → code theft). Redirect URIs must be matched exactly per OAuth 2.1, with a tightly scoped (numeric-port-only) exception at most.

findings.title: JWTTokenManagerValidated.validate_access_token verifies only against the self-generated public key (JWKS never consulted)
findings.detail: This ITokenManager impl hard-codes verification against self._public_key (line 318), the in-process RSA key generated in __init__ (lines 97-98), with no JWKS client at all. It can therefore only validate tokens it itself minted; any real OAuth-server-issued access token fails, and conversely the only tokens it accepts are self-signed by an ephemeral per-instance key. Combined with create_access_token also signing with that ephemeral key, the 'validated' manager cannot participate in a real trust boundary. The sibling JWTTokenManager wires a JWKSClient (_get_verification_key); this impl silently lacks the equivalent path.

findings.title: JWTTokenManagerValidated drops additional_claims — MCP capability claims silently discarded
findings.detail: create_access_token builds ValidatedTokenClaims (lines 142-151) with no use of the `additional_claims` parameter beyond a protected-name collision check (lines 154-174); the claims are never passed to encode_access_token (line 178). create_mcp_token (lines 620-651) relies on additional_claims to carry mcp_capabilities, so the resulting MCP token contains NO capability restriction claims — a capability-confinement noop. Either the codec must receive additional_claims or create_mcp_token is silently broken.

findings.title: validate_access_token / refresh_token classify failures by stringifying ANY exception (catch-all Exception)
findings.detail: JWTTokenManager.validate_access_token wraps the whole body in `except Exception as e:` and re-emits a generic security error with str(e) (lines 409-415); validate_refresh_token (line 510) and introspect_token (line 986) likewise collapse every exception into a generic invalid/active=False. The catalog requires failure classification by PyJWT exception TYPE (_classify_pyjwt_error), specific subclasses before generic DecodeError, never by string-sniffing. This blanket catch defeats that discipline and risks masking distinct failure modes (e.g. key-resolution/authority errors indistinguishable from a bad signature).

findings.title: validate_refresh_token falls back to self-generated key when JWKS lookup fails (fail-open key resolution)
findings.detail: When a JWKS client is configured, validate_refresh_token tries peek_key_id + jwks.get_public_key, and on ANY exception logs a warning and sets `public_key = self._public_key` (the in-process self-generated key) at lines 442-447, then proceeds to decode_and_verify with it. This is exactly the fail-open the sibling introspect_token explicitly warns against (lines 922-934): an unknown-kid / JWKS-unreachable condition must NOT be rescued by the self key, or a token forged with the self key (or wrong key) is accepted. Key resolution must be fail-closed (UNKNOWN_KID), never fall back.

findings.title: PKCE challenge↔verifier match uses non-constant-time `!=` comparison
findings.detail: _validate_challenge_verifier_match compares the recomputed S256/plain challenge to the supplied code_challenge with `if expected_challenge != code_challenge` (line 192). The codebase's own PKCEHandler.verify_code_challenge uses secrets.compare_digest for this exact comparison (pkce_handler.py line 107); this business-rule path regresses to a timing-variable `!=`. Use hmac/secrets.compare_digest for the PKCE challenge equality check.

findings.title: ClientCredentials carries client_secret/assertion as bare Optional[str] instead of typed IClientSecret
findings.detail: ClientCredentials declares client_secret/client_certificate/client_assertion as Optional[str] (lines 17-20), bypassing the typed-credential discipline. The catalog requires credentials to be typed primitives (IClientSecret etc.) with redaction and wire str only at from_wire/reveal boundaries; a sibling ClientSecret value object already exists (client_secret.py) with __repr__/__str__ redaction. As bare str these secrets are loggable via the dataclass repr and lose type-confusion protection. Note this is also the credential that exchange_authorization_code never verifies (separate finding).

findings.title: OAuth21Provider start_authorization_flow uses a hard-coded 'https://auth.example.com' base URL
findings.detail: start_authorization_flow builds the authorization URL against `base_url = 'https://auth.example.com/oauth/authorize'  # TODO: Make configurable` (line 189), and get_authorization_server_metadata likewise hard-codes issuer/endpoints to auth.example.com (lines 872-901). These are non-functional placeholders that would mis-issue authorization URLs / metadata in production; flagged as unfinished conformance rather than a direct bypass.

findings.title: OIDC validate_id_token verifies claims on UNVERIFIED token (no signature check) — ID-token forgery / auth bypass
findings.detail: validate_id_token() (the production IOIDCProvider impl, wired by SSOProviderFactory.create line 102/147) obtains claims via self._codec.peek_claims(id_token). peek_claims decodes with options={"verify_signature": False} (jwt_codec.py:115). The method then checks iss (line 336), aud (line 344), nonce (line 350) and exp (line 354) against these UNVERIFIED claims and returns payload as 'validated claims' (line 369). No call to decode_and_verify and no JWKS/key resolution anywhere in the method. An attacker can mint an id_token with arbitrary sub/iss/aud/nonce (using alg=none or any unsigned/wrong-signed token) and it passes validation, yielding full identity forgery. Catalog: 'peek_claims ... must NEVER feed a trust decision — diagnostic reads only.'

findings.title: MCPAuthenticator.authenticate_mcp_request accepts any non-empty bearer token as successful auth
findings.detail: authenticate_mcp_request() splits the Bearer header (line 41), checks only that the token is non-empty (line 43), then unconditionally returns SecurityAuthenticationResult(is_successful=True, authentication_method='mcp_bearer') (line 50). There is no JWT signature verification, no expiry/audience/scope check, no introspection. Any caller presenting 'Authorization: Bearer x' authenticates successfully. This is a complete authentication bypass for MCP requests.
