findings.title: OIDC PKCE verifier lookup returns an arbitrary session's verifier — defeats PKCE binding
findings.detail: _get_stored_pkce_verifier() ignores the authorization_code argument entirely and returns the code_verifier of the FIRST entry in self._pkce_sessions (for session_data in self._pkce_sessions.values(): return session_data['code_verifier']). The verifier is therefore not bound to the specific authorization request/code, so PKCE no longer ties the token exchange to the client that initiated the flow — an attacker who intercepts an auth code can complete the exchange using whatever verifier happens to be stored. Comment even says 'Simplified implementation - in production, properly map codes to verifiers'.

findings.title: JWTTokenManager.validate_refresh_token falls back to self-generated key when JWKS lookup fails
findings.detail: In validate_refresh_token, when self._jwks_client is set, a failure to fetch the issuer key by kid is caught and silently replaced with public_key = self._public_key (lines 442-447), then decode_and_verify proceeds against the manager's OWN self-generated key. This conflates 'trusted-issuer key unavailable / unknown kid' with 'verify against a local key', allowing a token signed by the local key (or enabling key-confusion) to validate when the real issuer key cannot be resolved. Contrast with introspect_token (lines 930-934) and _get_verification_key, which deliberately do NOT fall back in JWKS mode. Catalog: 'JWKS unreachable (any exception) -> UNKNOWN_KID; never proceed without a key.'

findings.title: ClientCredentialsVerificationBusinessRule.evaluate() is a no-op that always passes
findings.detail: evaluate() — the BusinessRule ABC entry point invoked by the verification orchestrator — sets self._violations = [] with a comment 'actual verification logic would go here' and returns BusinessRuleResult(passed=len(self._violations)==0 => always True). All real checks (client_id presence, redirect-URI exact-match/HTTPS, confidential-client auth, secret strength) live in a SEPARATE method evaluate_credentials() that the orchestrator never calls. Any flow relying on this rule's evaluate() for client-credentials enforcement passes unconditionally.

findings.title: OIDC discovery document advertises insecure flows: implicit/hybrid response_types, none signing, token_endpoint_auth none
findings.detail: get_openid_configuration advertises response_types_supported including 'id_token', 'id_token token', 'code token', 'code id_token token' (lines 85-92) — i.e. the implicit and hybrid flows that OAuth 2.1 removes — and userinfo_signing_alg_values_supported includes 'none' (line 156). For a server claiming 'OAuth 2.1' and 'Mandatory PKCE', advertising implicit/hybrid and alg=none weakens client behavior and invites downgrade. response_modes_supported also includes 'fragment'/'form_post' tied to those flows.

findings.title: JWTTokenManagerValidated.validate_token_binding / bind_token_to_client are accept-all stubs
findings.detail: validate_token_binding() returns True unconditionally ('For now, return True') (line 689) and bind_token_to_client() returns the original token unchanged (line 670). A caller using this manager to enforce sender-constrained (DPoP/mTLS-bound) tokens gets a silent always-pass: any client_proof validates, so token theft is not mitigated despite the binding API appearing to be honored. Unlike the non-validated JWTTokenManager which raises 'not yet implemented', this variant fails OPEN.

findings.title: JWTTokenManagerValidated uses random per-process kid unrelated to the verification key
findings.detail: _get_key_id() returns secrets.token_hex(8) — a fresh random value each call ('Simplified for demo'). The kid stamped in the JWT header thus does not identify the signing key and changes per token; any consumer doing kid-based JWKS key selection cannot resolve it. validate_access_token here ignores kid and always verifies with self._public_key, so it 'works' only in the single-key self-signing case, masking the problem for multi-key/rotation deployments.

findings.title: SAML create_logout_request interpolates name_id_value into XML without escaping
findings.detail: create_logout_request builds the LogoutRequest via an f-string embedding name_id_value directly between <NameID> tags with no XML escaping (line 380). Same pattern for AuthnRequest ACS URL (line 469). The value currently derives from a stored session user_id (originating from a verified assertion), bounding exploitability, but unescaped interpolation of identity-derived strings into outbound SAML XML is an XML-injection footgun if the source ever broadens; use proper XML serialization/escaping.

findings.title: OIDCSessionManager idle-timeout and discovery-cache use .seconds, ignoring multi-day deltas
findings.detail: _is_session_valid computes idle_minutes = (now - last_accessed).seconds // 60 (line 552); timedelta.seconds is the sub-day remainder (0-86399), so a session idle for e.g. 1 day + 1 minute yields idle_minutes=1 and is treated as fresh, defeating the 30-minute idle timeout for gaps that are whole-day multiples. Same .seconds bug in cleanup_expired_sessions (line 447) and in OIDCAuthenticationProvider discovery cache TTL (oidc_authentication_provider.py:435). Use .total_seconds().

findings.title: _encode_saml_request returns a hardcoded placeholder instead of real AuthnRequest XML
findings.detail: _encode_saml_request() (lines 54-58) ignores all request fields (request_id, issuer, destination, force_authn, NameIDPolicy, requested auth context, signature requirements) and unconditionally returns the constant string "encoded_saml_request". to_query_params() (line 46-52) builds the HTTP-Redirect 'SAMLRequest' parameter from this placeholder, so any caller that serialises a SAMLAuthenticationRequest for transport emits a constant, non-functional, unsigned request. This is a stub masquerading as a working serializer; if it were ever wired into an SSO flow it would silently produce a broken/forgeable authn request rather than failing closed.

findings.title: SAMLAuthenticationRequest is dead code — never constructed and its serializer never called
findings.detail: Grep across the repo (excluding the defining file and tests) finds no construction of SAMLAuthenticationRequest( and no call to to_query_params or _encode_saml_request. The class — including its placeholder encoder — is never instantiated or invoked in production code, so the AuthnRequest-building path advertised by this module does not actually exist.

findings.title: SAMLResponseData is never constructed; its validity helpers ignore signature_valid (fail-open surface)
findings.detail: No production code constructs SAMLResponseData( (grep finds zero non-test instantiations). The class carries signature_valid/is_signed (lines 51-52) as plain bool data, but none of its trust-relevant helpers consult them: is_valid_at_time (54), is_success_status (75), is_audience_valid (79) and is_session_active (83) all return positive results regardless of signature_valid. A future caller wiring this in could call is_valid_at_time()+is_success_status() and treat the response as trustworthy while signature_valid is False — a fail-open helper surface. The catalog requires consumers to gate on signature_valid; this DTO provides no helper that does so.

findings.title: is_audience_valid / is_valid_for_session use `or {}` fallback on List fields (semantics-masking)
findings.detail: audience_restrictions is declared List[str] but is_audience_valid does `expected_audience in (self.audience_restrictions or {})` (line 81); likewise SAMLLogoutRequest.is_valid_for_session does `session_index in (self.session_indexes or {})` (saml_logout_request.py line 37) where session_indexes is List[str]. Membership in an empty dict still works, so behaviour is correct, but the `or {}` is the wrong empty default for a list and signals copy-paste; it masks intent and would silently break if logic ever depended on .get on these fields.

findings.title: SAML SP/IdP credentials stored as bare str in config dataclasses
findings.detail: SAMLServiceProvider stores certificate: str and private_key: str (lines 33-34) directly in a config-style dataclass; SAMLProviderConfiguration stores signing_certificate: str / encryption_certificate (saml_provider_configuration.py lines 33-34) the same way, and create_basic (line 67) takes signing_certificate as a bare str. This contradicts the credential-injection discipline (typed credential primitives like IPublicKeyPEM, never stored as plain strings in config models). A bare-string private key in a config object is also more likely to be logged/serialized than a typed credential primitive.

findings.title: SAMLProviderConfiguration / SAMLConfigurationStorage / SAMLLogoutRequest / SAMLServiceProvider have no production construction path
findings.detail: These types appear only as field type-annotations in sso_configuration.py / multi_provider_configuration.py / sso_metadata_manager.py; grep finds no SAMLProviderConfiguration(, SAMLConfigurationStorage(, SAMLLogoutRequest(, or SAMLServiceProvider( call sites in non-test production source (create_basic and create_basic matches elsewhere are unrelated classes). The configuration/storage scaffolding is declared and wired as types but never instantiated, so the SAML config-loading flow it implies is not actually realised in code.

findings.title: SAML AuthnRequest encoding is a hardcoded placeholder stub
findings.detail: `_encode_saml_request()` (lines 54-58) ignores every field on the request and returns the literal string `"encoded_saml_request"`. `to_query_params()` (line 49) feeds this constant directly into the `SAMLRequest` query parameter. Any HTTP-Redirect SP authentication flow built on this produces a non-functional / forged AuthnRequest containing no request_id, issuer, destination, ACS URL, NameIDPolicy, or signature. This is exactly the forbidden 'method that only returns a placeholder' stub pattern; it must either fully serialize the SAML AuthnRequest XML (deflate+base64 per SAML 2.0 binding) or the type must not advertise `to_query_params`.

findings.title: SP decryption private key carried as bare str in a config-shaped dataclass
findings.detail: `SAMLServiceProvider.private_key: str` (line 34) and `certificate: str` (line 33) hold the SP's private decryption key and cert as raw `str` inside a plain `@dataclass` that models provider/configuration state. Per the credential-injection discipline in the normative catalog, signing/decryption keys must be typed credential primitives (e.g. IPublicKeyPEM / a key value object validated at construction) and constructor-injected, never bare strings co-located with config fields. A bare `str` performs no header/footer/format validation and structurally permits key/cert/secret type confusion.

findings.title: IdP signing_certificate stored as unvalidated bare str
findings.detail: `SAMLProviderConfiguration.signing_certificate: str` (line 33, also `encryption_certificate: Optional[str]` line 34) is the IdP X.509 cert used downstream to gate `signature_valid` on assertions, yet it is an unvalidated bare `str` with no PEM header/footer validation at construction. The catalog requires PEM credential primitives (IPublicKeyPEM validates header/footer) so a malformed or empty cert cannot silently flow into signature verification. `create_basic` (lines 67-96) accepts any string with no format check.

findings.title: audience_restrictions (List[str]) given a dict `or {}` fallback in audience check
findings.detail: `is_audience_valid` (line 79-81) evaluates `expected_audience in (self.audience_restrictions or {})`. `audience_restrictions` is declared `List[str]` (line 48), but the fallback substitutes an empty `dict`. When the list is non-empty the membership test works, but the wrong-type fallback is a type-confusion latent bug in a security-relevant audience-restriction check (SAML 2.0 audience confinement), and signals the validation was written without regard to the declared type. Should be `or []`.

findings.title: session_indexes (List[str]) given a dict `or {}` fallback in SLO session match
findings.detail: `is_valid_for_session` (lines 35-37) computes `session_index in (self.session_indexes or {})` where `session_indexes` is declared `List[str]` (line 29). Same wrong-type `or {}` fallback as saml_response_data; an empty-list session list falls back to a dict membership test. Single-logout session matching is security-relevant (controls which sessions a LogoutRequest may terminate); the fallback should be `or []`.

findings.title: Unused IRelativeReference / timezone imports across SAML domain modules
findings.detail: `IRelativeReference` is imported but unused in saml_service_provider.py (line 16), saml_authentication_request.py (line 12), saml_endpoint_configuration.py (line 11), and saml_provider_configuration.py (line 13); `timezone` is imported but unused in saml_service_provider.py (line 12), saml_response_data.py (line 9), saml_assertion.py (line 12), and saml_logout_request.py (line 9). Hygiene only — no behavioral impact — but indicates copy-paste of the module header without pruning.

findings.title: SAML response time-window check fails OPEN when Conditions absent
findings.detail: is_valid_at_time (lines 54-60) returns True when both not_before and not_on_or_after are None (both declared Optional at lines 46-47). A SAML response/assertion that carries NO Conditions time bounds is therefore treated as valid at every point in time. This is exactly the 'default-on-missing must not silently widen the window' anti-pattern called out in the SAML normative catalog: a missing NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter must NOT pass a trust gate. A forged or replayed assertion that simply omits the Conditions element passes this validity check unconditionally. The method must require the bounds to be present (treat missing as invalid) rather than skipping the comparison.

findings.title: SAML SP private_key stored as plain str field on dataclass (violates credential-injection discipline)
findings.detail: SAMLServiceProvider.private_key: str (line 34) and certificate: str (line 33) store the SP decryption/signing private key as a bare string on a configuration/domain dataclass that is exported from the package __init__ (security/__init__.py:258) and embedded in SSOConfiguration (sso_configuration.py:38). The catalog's credential-injection invariant requires signing/private keys to be constructor/DI params via typed credential primitives (e.g. IPublicKeyPEM-style value objects), never carried as raw str inside config models — a raw private-key str is trivially serialized into logs/config dumps and bypasses format validation at construction.

findings.title: SAML AuthnRequest encoding is a hardcoded placeholder stub emitted on the wire
findings.detail: _encode_saml_request (lines 54-58) returns the constant string 'encoded_saml_request' instead of serializing the actual AuthnRequest XML, and to_query_params (lines 46-52) places this placeholder into the SAMLRequest query parameter that is sent to the IdP. Every security-relevant request attribute (force_authn, require_signed_response/require_signed_assertion at lines 25/43-44, name_id_policy_format, requested_auth_context_class_refs) is silently dropped — the IdP receives a garbage/empty request, so the SP's signing and authn-context requirements are never actually asserted on the wire. A stub on a trust-bearing wire path; either implement real encoding or do not expose to_query_params.

findings.title: is_audience_valid uses dict fallback for a List audience-restriction set (type confusion)
findings.detail: is_audience_valid (lines 79-81) evaluates `expected_audience in (self.audience_restrictions or {})` but audience_restrictions is typed List[str] (line 48); the empty-fallback is a dict literal `{}` rather than `[]`. Membership still computes the correct boolean here, but mixing a dict fallback on a list-typed audience-restriction check is type-confused and brittle — a future refactor that depends on the fallback's type (iteration, indexing) would silently misbehave on this security-relevant audience-restriction gate. Same dict-fallback-on-a-List pattern recurs in saml_logout_request.py:37 (`session_index in (self.session_indexes or {})`).

findings.title: JWT protocol domain types are non-constructible (missing @dataclass) and entirely dead
findings.detail: JWTStandardClaims (jwt_standard_claims.py:12), JWTHeader (jwt_header.py:13, subclass of plain ABC IJWTHeader), JWKSKey (jwks_key.py:12), JWKSConfiguration (jwks_configuration.py:16) and JWTHeaderVerificationData (jwt_header_verification_data.py:13) declare class-level annotated fields with defaults (e.g. jwt_standard_claims.py:16 `issuer: str`, jwt_header.py:16 `algorithm: JWTAlgorithm`) but carry NO @dataclass decorator and inherit from no Pydantic/dataclass base. They therefore have no generated __init__: `JWTStandardClaims(issuer=...)` raises TypeError, and bare fields like `issuer` are class attributes shared across non-existent instances. A repo-wide grep finds ZERO constructions of any of these classes and zero importers outside the package's own generated __init__.py re-export. This is a whole cluster of dead, non-functional code masquerading as the JWT claim/header/JWKS domain model. JWKSConfiguration.find_key_by_id (jwks_configuration.py:25) would also never match (compares str key.key_id to opaque IKeyID), and find_signing_keys/get_key_ids iterate `(self.keys or {})` (List defaulted to dict) — latent bugs that are masked only because nothing constructs the type.

findings.title: JWKSKey.can_verify_signatures defaults to True when key 'use'/'key_ops' absent (fail-open key selection)
findings.detail: jwks_key.py:80-86 can_verify_signatures() returns True ('Default assumption') when neither key_use nor key_operations is set, so a JWK with no usage restriction is treated as a valid signature-verification key. If this type were ever wired into key selection it would let an encryption-only (or unspecified) key be used to verify signatures. Severity is held at low because the class is currently dead (no constructions repo-wide), but the default is fail-open rather than fail-closed and should be flagged before any future wiring.

findings.title: Token lifecycle/verification domain classes are non-constructible (missing @dataclass) with dead __post_init__ defaults
findings.detail: Eight classes under common/tokens carry annotated fields but no @dataclass decorator, so they have no __init__ and (for those with one) their __post_init__ never runs: TokenVerificationContext (token_verification_context.py:13, plus security flags verify_signature/verify_audition default True and a validate_claims() method that can never be exercised), TokenVerificationContextData (token_verification_context_data.py:13), RefreshTokenVerificationContextData (refresh_token_verification_context_data.py:13), TokenVerificationMetadata (token_verification_metadata.py:15, __post_init__ at :40 is dead), TokenRevocationInfo (token_revocation_info.py:15), TokenRevocationMetadata (token_revocation_metadata.py:17, __post_init__ at :39 dead), TokenGenerationRequest (token_generation_request.py:16), TokenGenerationContext (token_generation_context.py:16, __post_init__ at :43 dead). All eight have ZERO constructions repo-wide and are only re-exported through __init__.py. The security-flag defaults (verify_signature=True etc.) give a false impression of an enforced verification policy that no code path actually uses.

findings.title: TokenIntrospectionResponse.is_expired returns False (not expired) when exp claim is missing
findings.detail: token_introspection_response.py:93-95 is_expired() returns `self.exp is not None and now >= self.exp`, i.e. a token whose exp is None is reported as NOT expired. The ITokenIntrospectionResponse ABC (itoken_introspection_response.py:78-80) does not specify the missing-exp behavior. In practice the `active` flag (RFC 7662) is the real trust gate and is checked separately in is_valid_for_audience, so this is not an auth bypass on its own; but a caller that consults is_expired() in isolation as a lifetime check would treat an exp-less token as perpetually valid. Worth a fail-closed default (treat missing exp as expired) or an explicit documented contract.

findings.title: JWTStandardClaims is not a dataclass and has no __init__ — instances have no claim attributes, to_dict() raises AttributeError
findings.detail: class JWTStandardClaims (line 12) declares only class-body annotations (issuer: str, subject: str, audience: List[str], issued_at, expires_at at lines 16-19) with NO @dataclass decorator and NO __init__. Non-defaulted annotations like `issuer: str` are PEP 526 variable annotations, not real attributes — an instance carries none of them. to_dict() (line 28) reads self.issuer/self.subject/self.issued_at, which raises AttributeError at runtime. is_valid_at_time() (line 50) reads self.issued_at/self.expires_at and likewise breaks. The class is publicly exported (security/__init__.py) but never constructed in production; it is a non-functional time-validity claim object masquerading as usable. Compare RefreshTokenFamily in the same tree which correctly uses @dataclass.

findings.title: JWTHeader concrete is not a dataclass / has no __init__ — cannot be instantiated with its declared fields
findings.detail: class JWTHeader(IJWTHeader) (line 13) binds to ABC IJWTHeader and implements to_dict(), but declares its fields only as class-body annotations (algorithm: JWTAlgorithm, token_type, key_id, etc. lines 15-27) with no @dataclass and no __init__. `algorithm: JWTAlgorithm` is non-defaulted so it is not an instance attribute; to_dict() line 31 reads self.algorithm.value and would raise AttributeError. The ABC contract (security_interfaces/common/protocols/jwt/ijwt_header.py) expects a header carrying real cryptographic metadata; this concrete cannot hold any. Fields with mutable-looking defaults that ARE set (token_type=TokenType.JWT at line 17) become shared class attributes rather than per-instance state.

findings.title: JWTHeader.to_dict emits the TokenType enum object instead of its wire string for the `typ` header
findings.detail: Line 31: `header: Dict[str, Any] = {"alg": self.algorithm.value, "typ": self.token_type}`. `alg` is correctly serialized via `.value`, but `typ` is set to the raw TokenType enum member (TokenType.JWT, value "jwt" per security_interfaces/layer_0_primitives/authentication/token_type.py:40), not `self.token_type.value`. A JOSE header `typ` must be a string; serializing an enum object produces a non-spec value (and even with .value it would be lowercase "jwt" rather than the RFC 7519 canonical "JWT"). Diagnostic-only impact since the class is unconstructable, but the serialization is wrong on its face.

findings.title: JWKSKey is not a dataclass / has no __init__ — key_type and all JWK params are class annotations only
findings.detail: class JWKSKey (line 12) declares key_type: str (line 16, non-defaulted) plus all RSA/EC/symmetric params as class-body annotations with no @dataclass and no __init__. to_jwk() (line 39) reads self.key_type — AttributeError on a bare instance. can_verify_signatures() (line 80) reads self.key_use/self.key_operations. This is the value object that is supposed to represent a verification key inside a JWKS; in its current shape it cannot hold an actual key, so any code that did construct and rely on it for signature-key selection would silently mis-resolve. Publicly exported via security/__init__.py.

findings.title: JWKSConfiguration is not a dataclass and find_signing_keys/get_key_ids iterate `self.keys or {}` (a dict fallback for a List field)
findings.detail: class JWKSConfiguration (line 16) declares `keys: List[JWKSKey]` (line 19) as a class annotation with no @dataclass/__init__, so an instance has no `keys` attribute and to_jwks()/find_key_by_id() (lines 21,25) raise AttributeError. Additionally find_signing_keys() (line 34) and get_key_ids() (line 38) use `self.keys or {}` — an empty-dict fallback for what is typed as a List — which is a copy/paste type confusion (find_key_by_id at line 27 correctly uses `self.keys or []`). Combined with the missing constructor this signing-key-selection helper is non-functional.

findings.title: JWTHeaderVerificationData security policy fields are shared mutable class attributes, not per-instance, due to missing @dataclass
findings.detail: class JWTHeaderVerificationData (line 13) is a plain class with class-body annotations and no @dataclass/__init__. The security-relevant policy fields allowed_algorithms: FrozenSet[str] = frozenset({"RS256","ES256","PS256"}) (line 22) and require_key_id: bool = True (line 23) become class-level attributes SHARED across all references, not per-verification settings — there is no constructor to override them per instance, so callers cannot tighten/loosen the allow-list per verification context; any attempt to set them per-instance would silently have no effect or, if assigned, mutate process-wide state. Algorithm allow-listing is a fail-closed control; binding it to shared class state rather than an injected, per-call value defeats the intent. Contrast JWTClaimsVerificationData in the same tree which is correctly @dataclass with field(default_factory=...).

findings.title: Authentication protocol ABCs (IOAuth21Provider, IOIDCProvider, ITokenManager, etc.) defined outside an *_interfaces/ package
findings.detail: Eight ABCs (IEnterpriseSSOManager, IOAuth21Provider, IOIDCProvider, IPKCEHandler, ISAMLProvider, ISSOAuthenticationProvider, ISSOConfigurationManager, ITokenManager) are correctly declared as abc.ABC with @abstractmethod, but live under the concrete `security/common/authentication/interfaces/` directory rather than in security_interfaces, contrary to the codebase's interface-location rule (interfaces belong in *_interfaces/ packages; the misplaced-interface hook flags exactly this). No security weakness — they are real ABCs with no stub bodies — but it is a conformance/placement defect for the auth-jwt-protocol unit.

findings.title: Bearer-token equality uses non-constant-time `==` on raw secret value
findings.detail: AccessToken.matches() returns `self._value == other._value` (line 36), a byte-by-byte short-circuiting comparison of two high-entropy bearer secrets. The class docstring (lines 24-26) explicitly justifies plain `==` ("bcrypt hashing is not required"), but that reasoning omits timing-side-channel resistance. A bearer token presented by an attacker is compared against the stored value with early-exit semantics, leaking a timing oracle. The codec/PDP catalog already mandates constant-time comparison for OAuth state (IOAuthState.matches); the same discipline is absent here. Use hmac.compare_digest(self._value, other._value). Identical defect in refresh_token.py:32, session_token.py:33, id_token.py:32.

findings.title: RefreshToken/SessionToken/IDToken matches() are non-constant-time secret comparisons
findings.detail: RefreshToken.matches() (line 32) compares the long-lived refresh secret with `self._value == other._value`. Refresh tokens are the highest-value credential at the token endpoint; a timing oracle on this comparison is more damaging than on a short-lived access token. SessionToken.matches() (session_token.py:33) and IDToken.matches() (id_token.py:32) share the same pattern. All four token classes should use hmac.compare_digest.

findings.title: Verification-context classes declare fields but are not dataclasses — no __init__, shared mutable class-level state
findings.detail: TokenVerificationContextData (line 13) carries security-control attributes (allow_insecure_algorithms: bool = False line 25, verify_signature: bool = True line 33, require_secure_transport, maximum_token_size_bytes) but the class has NO @dataclass decorator and NO __init__ (confirmed by grep: only `class TokenVerificationContextData:` declares it). Consequences: (a) it cannot be constructed with these fields — TokenVerificationContextData(token=..., verify_signature=False) raises TypeError, so the type is unconstructable as designed; (b) every annotated default is a single class-level object shared across all (would-be) instances, so any code that mutates instance.verify_signature would silently flip the security policy process-wide. The same non-dataclass defect affects TokenVerificationContext (token_verification_context.py:13), RefreshTokenVerificationContextData (refresh_token_verification_context_data.py:13), JWKSKey, JWKSConfiguration, JWTStandardClaims, and JWTHeaderVerificationData.

findings.title: verify_signature / allow_insecure_algorithms knobs are dead-wired; JWT rule never verifies the signature
findings.detail: TokenVerificationContextData advertises verify_signature (line 33) and allow_insecure_algorithms (line 25) as if they gate verification. The only consumer, TokenVerificationBusinessRule.evaluate (layer_0_primitives/authentication/verification/token_verification_business_rule.py:54-143), decodes the JWT via self._codec.peek_claims(...) (line 112) — which the normative catalog defines as UNVERIFIED, diagnostic-read-only — plus a manual base64 header decode (lines 105-110), then validates exp/iat/aud/scope and returns passed=True (line 138) WITHOUT ever verifying the cryptographic signature against any key. Neither verify_signature nor allow_insecure_algorithms is ever read. A forged token with valid structure, correct claims and a strong alg header passes this rule. The flags create a false impression of fail-closed signature enforcement that does not exist.

findings.title: JWKSConfiguration.find_signing_keys / get_key_ids iterate `self.keys or {}` — inconsistent type-confused fallback, no warm-cache guard
findings.detail: keys is typed List[JWKSKey] (line 19), but find_signing_keys (line 34) and get_key_ids (line 38) fall back to (self.keys or {}) — an empty dict — while find_key_by_id (line 27) falls back to (self.keys or []). The inconsistency signals the fallback was never reasoned about. More importantly there is no warm-cache / non-empty guard: a JWKS document that produced an empty key list silently yields zero signing keys with no error, and the helper provides no signal distinguishing 'no keys loaded' from 'kid absent', so a key-resolution layer cannot fail closed with a precise reason (catalog requires UNKNOWN_KID on a kid the JWKS cannot supply).

findings.title: JWKSConfiguration.find_key_by_id compares wire str key_id against opaque IKeyID
findings.detail: find_key_by_id (line 25) takes key_id: IKeyID and compares key.key_id == key_id (line 28), where key.key_id is Optional[str] (jwks_key.py:18). Comparing an opaque IKeyID value object against a raw str will not match unless IKeyID.__eq__ is overloaded to compare to str; if it is not, the lookup always returns None and key resolution silently fails (every kid becomes UNKNOWN). This is exactly the type-confused boundary the opaque-ID discipline is meant to prevent; comparison should be against a typed key_id with str only at the wire boundary.

findings.title: JWKSKey.can_verify_signatures fails open (returns True) when use and key_ops are both absent
findings.detail: can_verify_signatures (line 80) returns True as a "Default assumption" (line 86) when a JWK omits both use and key_ops. An attacker-influenced or misconfigured JWKS entry with no usage constraints is therefore treated as signature-capable. RFC 7517 makes use/key_ops optional, so a benign-but-unconstrained key is plausible, but in a security context the safe default is to NOT assume verification capability when the publisher did not assert use:"sig" or verify in key_ops. Combined with find_signing_keys consuming this predicate, an encryption-only or ambiguous key could be selected for signature verification.

findings.title: JWTHeaderVerificationData allowlist/require_key_id are shared class-level defaults no code reads
findings.detail: JWTHeaderVerificationData declares allowed_algorithms (frozenset {RS256,ES256,PS256}, line 22), require_key_id=True (line 23) and require_type_header (line 24) as if they constrain header acceptance, but the class is not a dataclass (no __init__) and grep finds no production consumer of the type or these attributes under security/src. The allowlist and require_key_id policy are inert; nothing enforces that the verified algorithm is in allowed_algorithms or that a kid is present. The hard-coded allowlist in the actually-used rule (token_verification_business_rule.py:227) is a separate duplicated set, so this object is a misleading dead security control.

findings.title: JWTTokenVerificationRule security branch is unreachable (TokenVerificationContextData never constructed)
findings.detail: JWTTokenVerificationRule.evaluate (line 55) gates its extra security checks behind isinstance(context_data, TokenVerificationContextData) (line 81). Because TokenVerificationContextData has no constructor (see token_verification_context_data.py finding) and grep finds zero TokenVerificationContextData(...) construction sites in security/src, this branch can never be true in production — the only thing it would add (token-size bound and XSS-pattern sniff at lines 107-116) never runs. Even if reached it performs string-pattern sniffing rather than signature verification, reinforcing that this rule chain validates structure/claims of an UNVERIFIED token (peek_claims) and returns passed=True without crypto verification.

findings.title: Runtime policy evaluation ignores all rules — uses only policy.default_effect
findings.detail: PolicyDecisionPoint.evaluate_single_policy() comments '# Evaluate policy rules (simplified - production would parse rule conditions)' and then sets `effect = policy.default_effect`, ignoring every rule/condition in the policy. The PDP is the pure decision core for the whole ABAC/Zero-Trust flow (reached via PEP.authorize_and_enforce -> _pdp.evaluate -> evaluate_single_policy). Any policy whose default_effect is PERMIT grants access regardless of its rules/conditions; conversely conditional DENY rules are never applied. Authorization decisions are not actually computed from policy logic.

findings.title: PDP target matching is naive substring containment (over-broad applicability)
findings.detail: _matches_target() lowercases policy.target and returns True if `request.action.lower() in target` (substring). A policy targeting 'noread' would match action 'read'; a policy with target containing 'read' matches every read request across all resources/subjects because resource/subject are not considered at all. Combined with default_effect-only evaluation, a single broad PERMIT policy applies far more widely than intended. Same naive logic duplicated in ABACEngine._matches_target (abac_engine.py line 909).

findings.title: PEP obligation enforcement is a hardcoded success stub (always returns True)
findings.detail: _execute_obligation() body is '# For now, assume all obligations succeed (mock) / return True'. The PEP's stated CRITICAL INVARIANT is 'PERMIT + required obligation failure -> DENY' (line 222-228, enforced via _has_required_obligation_failures). Because every obligation is reported fulfilled, security obligations attached to PERMIT decisions (STEP_UP_AUTH, ENCRYPT_DATA, REDACT_FIELDS, LOG_ACCESS) are never actually performed yet treated as satisfied — the invariant can never trigger a deny. Obligated access is granted without the obligation.

findings.title: LDAP attribute source returns hardcoded mock identity for ANY subject with HIGH assurance
findings.detail: _get_mock_value() returns a fixed dict {department:'engineering', roles:['developer','team_lead'], email, manager:'manager-456'} for every subject_id, and fetch_attributes() wraps it as AttributeWithProvenance with assurance_level=TrustLevel.HIGH, revocation_status=ACTIVE, confidence 0.95, plus a _create_mock_evidence() whose has_cryptographic_backing() returns True and a _get_trust_anchor() reporting HIGH. This is a real IAttributeSource registered via the PIP; any authorization that consults subject roles/department gets forged authoritative attributes. The catalog claims 'engine never fabricates values' — this source fabricates them with maximal trust.

findings.title: Database attribute source returns hardcoded mock resource attributes with HIGH assurance
findings.detail: _get_mock_value() returns fixed {owner:'user-123', classification:'confidential', status:'active', created_at} for any resource_id, wrapped with assurance_level=TrustLevel.HIGH and revocation_status=ACTIVE. Ownership/classification-based policies (e.g. owner-equals-subject) evaluate against forged resource facts: every resource appears owned by 'user-123' and classified 'confidential', so decisions keyed on resource ownership/classification are meaningless and exploitable.

findings.title: Policy bundle signature verification is a stub that always returns True
findings.detail: PolicyBundle.verify_signature() body is '# For now, assume signature is always valid (mock) / return True', and create_signed_bundle() (line 219) sets signature = b'mock-signature-' + version_hash. The bundle is documented as 'signed by PAP for integrity'. Any attacker-supplied or tampered bundle passes signature verification, so PDP would evaluate forged policies. Integrity of the policy set rests on nothing.

findings.title: PRP never verifies bundle signature before serving it to the PDP (fail-open)
findings.detail: get_active_bundle() has the only integrity check commented out: lines 124-127 '# if not active_bundle.verify_signature(pap_public_key): raise RuntimeError(...)' with 'we assume bundles are trusted if they're in the store'. Even the stub verify_signature is not called. The docstring promises 'RuntimeError: If bundle signature verification fails' but no verification occurs — any bundle cached (incl. via the mock-signed PAP path) is served as authoritative to the decision engine.

findings.title: ABACEngine publishes policy bundles signed with a hardcoded mock key
findings.detail: _publish_policy_bundle_to_prp() calls PolicyBundle.create_signed_bundle(..., signing_key=b'mock-signing-key', # Production would use real key). Combined with the stub verify_signature and disabled PRP check, the entire PAP->PRP->PDP integrity chain is cryptographically inert; there is no real signing identity on policy bundles consumed at runtime.

findings.title: UnifiedPolicyEngine.evaluate_action always returns permit
findings.detail: evaluate_action() returns {'decision': 'permit', 'reason': 'Action evaluation completed'} unconditionally — it never consults policies, attributes, or trust. It is described as the IPolicyEngine compatibility method 'Evaluate if action complies with policy'. Any caller routing an action through this method receives an unconditional PERMIT, a fail-open authorization decision.

findings.title: UnifiedPolicyEngine.verify_policy / get_policy_status / compare_policies are constant stubs
findings.detail: verify_policy() returns {'valid': True, ...} regardless of policy (line 499); load_policy_from_yaml() returns {'loaded': True} without reading anything (line 540); get_policy_status() always reports status 'active' (line 558); compare_policies() always returns identical=True, differences=[] (line 607). These IPolicyEngine methods give callers false positive assurances (e.g. a malformed policy passes verify_policy; an invalidated policy still reads 'active').

findings.title: PDP runtime verifier object is constructed but never invoked (dead wire)
findings.detail: __init__ sets self._runtime_verifier = ABACEvaluationVerificationRule(), but evaluate() calls the private _verify_runtime_invariants() (line 105) and self._runtime_verifier.validate() is never called anywhere. The ABACEvaluationVerificationRule (which checks ID/action format validity) is therefore dead in the PDP. The actual invariant check is a minimal presence/strategy/count check; the format validation the rule provides never runs.

findings.title: OAuth authorize_resource_access can proceed on unbound auth_result after token-validation failure
findings.detail: In authorize_resource_access(), validate_token() is wrapped in try/except; on exception the handler builds an error and calls error.raise_error() (line 118) but the except block has no `raise`/`return` of its own. If raise_error() ever fails to raise (returns), execution falls through to line 142 using `auth_result`, which is unbound -> NameError, or worse if auth_result were partially set. The control-flow safety of denying on token failure depends entirely on raise_error() always raising; the method does not itself guarantee it. Same pattern in authorize_client_credentials_access (line 209).

findings.title: OAuth server derives resource/service IDs via MD5
findings.detail: authorize_resource_access/validate_and_authorize hash resource_uri with hashlib.md5(...).digest() to derive ResourceID, and authorize_client_credentials_access hashes the client_id with MD5 to derive the service SubjectID (lines 151, 236, 243, 337). MD5 is collision-prone; two distinct resource URIs or client IDs could collide to the same opaque ID used as the authorization subject/resource key, conflating distinct principals/resources in cache and policy targeting. Use a collision-resistant hash.

findings.title: Constraint validator fails open when constraint attributes are absent (hasattr gates)
findings.detail: Every sub-validator returns (True, '') when the constraints object lacks the relevant attribute: validate_temporal returns valid if no start_time/end_time (line 103-106); validate_financial valid if no max_amount (139); validate_resource_access valid if no allowed_resources (169); validate_operational valid if no allowed_operations (200). Since `constraints` is typed Any, a malformed/empty constraints object (or wrong type) silently satisfies all constraints rather than failing closed. Also validate_constraints returns (True, []) immediately when `not constraints` (line 47).

findings.title: In-process PolicyEnforcementPoint allows access to unprotected resources in non-strict modes
findings.detail: enforce_authorization() in 'lenient'/'monitor_only' modes returns access for resources never registered for protection: when resource_id not in self._protected_resources and mode != strict it returns `decision.decision == PERMIT` (line 143), and monitor_only returns True unconditionally (line 155) — bypassing the access_controller entirely. An unregistered (i.e. unknown) resource should fail closed; here enforcement is skipped. Caller-supplied enforcement_mode thus weakens the guard.

findings.title: UnifiedPolicyEngine._calculate_trust_score is time-of-day heuristic, not a real trust signal
findings.detail: _calculate_trust_score() computes trust from base 0.5 plus request.urgency, wall-clock business-hours bonus (+0.1 if 9-17), and len(session_history)*0.01. With base 0.5 the score lands in MEDIUM/HIGH for typical requests and never reaches UNTRUSTED unless urgency is 'critical' off-hours. The only place trust gates a decision is authorize() line 315 (UNTRUSTED + PERMIT -> DENY); because UNTRUSTED is essentially unreachable, the trust-based override is effectively inert, and trust 'verification' is a fabricated heuristic rather than an authority-resolved signal as the Zero-Trust catalog requires.

findings.title: ABACEngine.batch_evaluate_requests appends unawaited coroutines as results
findings.detail: batch_evaluate_requests() is a sync method that calls the async make_authorization_decision() without await (line 1166) and appends the returned coroutine object to results; the success/deny counting at line 1189 then reads r.decision on a coroutine. The batch path never actually evaluates authorization (coroutines are never run) and the audit 'successful_count' is computed from objects that have no .decision — a broken, never-executed decision path masquerading as a batch evaluator.

findings.title: ABAC PAP-time verification rules are constructed but never enforced in any decision/admin path
findings.detail: _setup_verification_rules() instantiates _policy_rule, _abac_evaluation_rule, _least_privilege_rule, _compliance_rule, _policy_conflict_rule, but _validate_policy_operation() (line 316-325) does only a 'policy.policy_id and policy.rules present' check and explicitly comments the real validation is 'currently simplified'. None of the stored rule objects are invoked. Least-privilege / compliance / conflict rules (which in any case only emit warnings, never errors — see least_privilege_rule.py) have no effect on policy admission or evaluation.

findings.title: ABACEngine.make_authorization_decision swallows all exceptions into a generic DENY without structured reason codes
findings.detail: The broad `except Exception as e` (line 1096) fails closed to DENY (correct direction) but flattens every internal error — including programming bugs like the unawaited-coroutine batch path or attribute resolution failures — into reason=f'Authorization failed: {e}' with no PDPReasonCode. The catalog requires denials carry a STRUCTURED set of reason codes; here operational failures are indistinguishable from policy denials, hampering detection of a degraded/failing PDP that is silently denying-all (or, where default_effect=PERMIT dominates upstream, masking real faults).

findings.title: ObligationFulfiller reports unwired obligations (ENCRYPT_DATA/REDACT_FIELDS/RATE_LIMIT/TOKEN_EXCHANGE) as fulfilled, defeating the PERMIT+obligation-failure→DENY invariant
findings.detail: fulfill() at lines 74-81 returns ObligationFulfillment(fulfilled=True) for every ObligationType other than LOG_ACCESS and STEP_UP_AUTH, with a comment that this is deliberate to 'keep the global obligation must succeed or treat decision as DENY invariant from triggering.' A PERMIT decision carrying a mandatory ENCRYPT_DATA or REDACT_FIELDS obligation is therefore reported fulfilled while the data is never encrypted/redacted. The documented security invariant (PEP must DENY when a required obligation fails) is silently bypassed for these obligation types.

findings.title: UnifiedPolicyEngine.evaluate_action() hardcodes a PERMIT decision regardless of action/context
findings.detail: evaluate_action() (lines 501-517) is described as an IPolicyEngine compatibility shim but unconditionally returns {"decision": "permit", "reason": "Action evaluation completed"} for any action and any context. This directly contradicts the class docstring guarantee that the engine 'fails closed (evaluation errors and no-applicable-policy both yield DENY).' Any caller binding to this method gets PERMIT with no policy evaluation. It is not part of the IUnifiedPolicyEngine ABC (which has only authorize/continuous_authorization/session/stats methods), so it is an undeclared extra surface that fails open.

findings.title: UnifiedPolicyEngine decision cache key omits trust/attribute context, so a cached PERMIT bypasses the untrusted-trust-score override
findings.detail: _generate_cache_key() keys only on subject_id:resource_id:action:urgency. authorize() (lines 268-286) returns a cached result BEFORE computing the trust score and BEFORE the UNTRUSTED→DENY override at lines 315-331. A PERMIT cached for one request is served verbatim for a later request with the same subject/resource/action/urgency but a different (e.g. UNTRUSTED) trust context, so the trust-based deny is never applied on cache hits. Trust-relevant environment/attributes are not in the cache key.

findings.title: ABACEngine publishes the runtime policy bundle signed with a hardcoded mock key on the live create/update path
findings.detail: _publish_policy_bundle_to_prp() calls PolicyBundle.create_signed_bundle(..., signing_key=b"mock-signing-key") with the comment 'Production would use real key.' This method is reached from create_policy() (line 746) and update_policy() (line 815) on every policy change. The PDP/PEP treat the PRP bundle as a 'signed, immutable, versioned' trusted artifact, but the signature is produced with a hardcoded constant, so bundle-signature integrity is meaningless. The signing credential is also a literal in code rather than a constructor/DI-injected credential.

findings.title: common/authorization PolicyEnforcementPoint declares IPolicyEnforcementPoint but implements neither abstract method
findings.detail: The class inherits IPolicyEnforcementPoint (imported from security_interfaces at lines 18-23). That ABC defines exactly two abstractmethods: authorize_and_enforce() and evaluate_only() (security_interfaces/.../enforcement/ipolicy_enforcement_point.py lines 59-120). This concrete provides neither; instead it exposes enforce_authorization()/register_protected_resource()/session methods. As written it cannot be instantiated (TypeError: abstract methods not implemented) — it claims an ABC it does not satisfy, violating the family→ABC binding.

findings.title: ContextHandler returns empty attribute maps and passes trust validation when no PIP / no verification_context, instead of failing closed
findings.detail: _fetch_attributes_from_pip() returns all-empty attribute collections when self._pip is None or verification_context is None (lines 258-265). validate_attribute_trust() (lines 184-189) then iterates over an empty attribute set and returns True. The PDP is handed a context with zero subject/resource attributes that 'passed' Checkpoints B & C. Checkpoint B (attribute authority) is an unimplemented placeholder (lines 176-181), so the catalog invariant 'required-attribute resolution failure raises and the PEP fails closed' is not enforced — missing attributes are silently treated as a valid empty context.

findings.title: LeastPrivilegeRule.validate() can never fail — all checks append warnings only, is_valid is always True
findings.detail: Every branch in validate() appends to warnings, never to errors (lines 48-104), and is_valid = len(errors) == 0 (line 107) is therefore always True. Global-scope permissions, SYSTEM roles, bulk privilege assignments, and privileged admin/delete/execute actions all yield only warnings, so this least-privilege verification rule never blocks any assignment. The file also begins with '# mypy: ignore-errors' (line 1) suppressing type checking on a security rule.

findings.title: OAuthAuthorizationServer fabricates a fresh SubjectID when the token's user_id is missing/invalid, then proceeds to authorize
findings.detail: In authorize_resource_access (lines 142-148) and validate_and_authorize (lines 328-334), user_id_str = getattr(auth_result, 'user_id', '') then UUID(user_id_str); on ValueError/AttributeError the code does subject_id = SubjectID() — generating a brand-new random subject — and still builds an AuthorizationRequest and calls the policy engine. A token with no/invalid subject thus receives an authorization decision under a fabricated identity rather than failing closed.

findings.title: Resource identity derived via MD5 of the resource URI risks collision-conflation of distinct resources
findings.detail: ResourceID is derived as UUID(bytes=hashlib.md5(f"resource:{resource_uri}".encode()).digest()) at lines 151-153, 243-245, and 337-339. MD5 is collision-prone and truncates the URI to 128 bits; two distinct resource URIs can map to the same ResourceID, conflating separate protected resources in the policy decision and cache keys. Use a collision-resistant hash or a real resource registry mapping.

findings.title: DelegationAuthorizer permits any action for delegation when allowed_actions is empty (the default)
findings.detail: check() guards the action only via 'if limits.allowed_actions and action_wire not in limits.allowed_actions' (line 54). DelegationCheckLimits.allowed_actions defaults to an empty frozenset (delegation_check_limits.py line 12), so with default-constructed limits the guard is skipped and every action passes the action check. The empty allow-list is treated as allow-all rather than deny-all for the action dimension.

findings.title: PDP and ABACEngine target matching uses substring containment, mis-scoping policies to unintended actions/attributes
findings.detail: _matches_target() returns True when 'request.action.lower() in target' (substring), so a policy with target text containing 'read' matches action 'read' but also a target 'readwrite' is matched by action 'read', and conversely. The mirror implementation in abac_engine.py _matches_target (lines 909-943) additionally matches if any subject/resource attribute value substring appears in the target. Substring containment can both over-match and under-match policy scope; XACML-style structured target matching is needed.

findings.title: PDP/ABACEngine single-policy evaluation returns policy.default_effect without evaluating rules
findings.detail: evaluate_single_policy() (lines 254-277) sets effect = policy.default_effect and ignores the policy's rules/conditions entirely ('simplified'). abac_engine._evaluate_single_policy (lines 1009-1022) does the same. A policy whose default_effect is PERMIT therefore permits unconditionally regardless of its rules. Combined with substring target matching, an over-matched PERMIT-default policy can grant access without any rule actually being satisfied.

findings.title: OAuth authorization server falls back to a fresh random SubjectID when the token's user_id is invalid, instead of denying
findings.detail: In authorize_resource_access (and identically in validate_and_authorize at line 333), the code does `UUID(user_id_str); subject_id = SubjectID.from_string(...)` and on `except (ValueError, AttributeError): subject_id = SubjectID()` mints a brand-new random subject identity. A token whose user_id claim is missing/malformed therefore does not fail closed: it proceeds into policy evaluation under a fabricated subject. Per the catalog's fail-closed invariant, a missing/unresolvable subject must DENY, not synthesize an identity. This breaks tenant/entity binding and lets a token with no usable subject still reach the policy engine.

findings.title: Resource and service-account identities derived with MD5
findings.detail: resource_id and service-account subject_id are derived via `hashlib.md5(f"resource:{resource_uri}".encode()).digest()` (lines 151, 236, 243, 337). MD5 is collision-prone, so two distinct resource_uris / client_ids can collide to the same ResourceID/SubjectID, letting a policy decision for one resource apply to another. Identity derivation feeding an authorization decision must use a collision-resistant hash (SHA-256) or, better, a proper opaque-ID mapping rather than a derived UUID.

findings.title: UnifiedPolicyEngine.evaluate_action is a hardcoded permit stub
findings.detail: evaluate_action() unconditionally returns `{"decision": "permit", "reason": "Action evaluation completed"}` regardless of action or context. It is presented as the IPolicyEngine action-evaluation entry point. Any caller that routes an authorization check through evaluate_action receives PERMIT for everything — a complete authorization bypass. Either remove it or make it delegate to the real authorize() path.

findings.title: Authorization trust score is fabricated from request body, clock, and session count rather than verified authority
findings.detail: _calculate_trust_score derives the Zero-Trust trust level from `request.urgency` (caller-controlled body field, line 157), local wall-clock hour (line 161), and `len(session_history)` (line 170). This is the value used at line 315 to gate PERMIT→DENY. A caller can raise its own trust tier purely by setting urgency='normal' and issuing requests during business hours; trust never comes from an authority-resolved signal. The catalog requires trust inputs to be authority-resolved (VerifiedTrustContext), never fabricated from forgeable/request-derived data.

findings.title: Untrusted-override applied only when require_trust_score=True; client_credentials path disables it
findings.detail: The UNTRUSTED→DENY override (lines 314-331) runs only inside `if require_trust_score:`. OAuthAuthorizationServer.authorize_client_credentials_access calls the engine with `require_trust_score=False` (o_auth_authorization_server.py:259), so service-account authorizations skip the trust-floor check entirely and a PERMIT from the ABAC engine is returned unmodified. Combined with the stubbed policy evaluation below, service-to-service calls get the weakest path.

findings.title: PDP single-policy evaluation ignores rules and returns the policy default effect
findings.detail: evaluate_single_policy() sets `effect = policy.default_effect` and never evaluates the policy's rules/conditions (comment line 255: 'simplified - production would parse rule conditions'). Every applicable policy therefore resolves to its default effect irrespective of subject/resource/action conditions. A policy whose default_effect is PERMIT will permit any request its loose target matches, defeating per-rule access constraints. This is the core decision function of the XACML PDP.

findings.title: PDP policy target matching uses substring containment, causing over-broad policy applicability
findings.detail: _matches_target does `if request.action.lower() in target` — a substring test against the lowercased target string. Action 'read' matches a target containing 'readonly', 'thread', 'already', etc.; a PERMIT policy targeted at one action can be selected for unrelated actions/resources. The same pattern appears in abac_engine.py _matches_target (line 921) which additionally matches if any subject/resource attribute value is a substring of the target (lines 928-941). Targets must be matched by structured equality, not `in`.

findings.title: Global singleton policy-decision cache is not tenant- or trust-scoped; cache key omits tenant_id
findings.detail: CacheKey.from_request builds the key from subject_id, resource_id, action, a context hash, and optional policy_version — there is NO tenant_id. UnifiedPolicyEngine uses a process-global singleton cache (policy_decision_cache.py:335 get_policy_cache, used at unified_policy_engine.py:115). Two tenants that happen to share subject/resource opaque-ID values (e.g. derived deterministically as in the OAuth server's MD5 derivation) collide in the same cache, so a PERMIT computed for tenant A can be served to tenant B. ResourceID here is derived purely from resource_uri (no tenant), making cross-tenant collision realistic. Cache key must include tenant_id and the verified trust tier.

findings.title: Cached authorization result is reused without re-validating freshness/revocation of the underlying attributes
findings.detail: authorize() returns the cached result (lines 268-286) before any attribute resolution or the trust-score override, keyed only on subject/resource/action/context-hash with a default 300s TTL (policy_decision_cache.py:39). Within the TTL window a revoked role or downgraded trust is not reflected — a previously-granted PERMIT keeps being served. For a Zero-Trust PDP that claims continuous verification, decisions should not be cached across a trust/attribute change without invalidation tied to those inputs.

findings.title: common/authorization PEP fails OPEN in lenient/monitor modes and monitor_only always returns True
findings.detail: enforce_authorization: in 'monitor_only' mode it returns True unconditionally regardless of the decision (lines 148-155); for unregistered resources in non-strict modes it returns `decision.decision == PERMIT` (line 143) bypassing the access_controller; and on access_controller exception in lenient mode it returns False but in strict raises. A deployment configured (or misconfigured) to 'monitor_only' grants every access. The enforcement default must be deny/strict; a mode that turns the PEP into a no-op while reporting success is a latent auth-bypass.

findings.title: Delegation trust scoring and the gate threshold are computed from caller-supplied party trust_scores
findings.detail: DelegationAuthorizer.check gates on `delegatee.trust_score < limits.min_delegatee_trust` (line 71) and computes delegated trust from `delegator.trust_score` (line 83), where delegator/delegatee are IDelegationParty inputs passed into the method. If these trust_score values originate from the request rather than an authority (DAS/VC verifier), a caller can self-assert a passing trust score. The catalog requires delegation scope/trust to come from the authority, never the request body; this file should document/enforce that delegator_can_delegate and party trust_scores are authority-resolved, not client-provided.

findings.title: ABAC policy bundles are signed with a hardcoded mock signing key
findings.detail: _publish_policy_bundle_to_prp calls `PolicyBundle.create_signed_bundle(..., signing_key=b"mock-signing-key")` (line 688, comment 'Production would use real key'). The PRP/PDP treat bundles as 'signed, immutable, versioned' (policy_decision_point.py docstring). A constant well-known key provides no integrity guarantee — anyone able to construct a bundle can forge a valid signature, and the signature check (if any consumer relies on it) is meaningless. Signing key must be injected by DI, never a literal.

findings.title: PEP authentication 'Checkpoint A' only checks subject_id presence; no token signature/issuer/integrity validation
findings.detail: authorize_and_enforce Step 1 (lines 149-160) documents that production should validate token signature, assurance level, request integrity, and issuer allowlist, but the implementation only does `if not request.subject_id: deny`. Mere presence of a subject_id is accepted as proof of authentication. Since subject_id arrives on the request (forgeable channel), this checkpoint provides no real authentication gate — the PEP relies entirely on an upstream caller having validated the token, which is not enforced here.

findings.title: Authorization audit log entries are fire-and-forget asyncio tasks whose loss never affects the decision and is silently swallowed
findings.detail: _audit_authorization wraps the audit call in `asyncio.create_task(...)` (line 488) with no awaited result and a broad try/except that only logs (lines 514-522). If no event loop is running, or the task fails, the authorization audit record for a PERMIT is silently lost while access is granted. For a security PDP, missing audit on grant is a tamper/accountability gap; the immediate (phase-1) authorization audit should be durable, not best-effort detached.

findings.title: evaluate_only enforcement-bypass method is part of the production class with only a docstring warning
findings.detail: evaluate_only() returns a raw IPolicyDecision skipping obligation enforcement and the PERMIT+obligation-failure→DENY invariant, guarded only by a docstring 'MUST NOT be used in production' (lines 286-289). Nothing structurally prevents a caller from using it as the decision source, which would drop the obligation-enforcement security invariant. A test-only bypass on the production interface should be removed or gated behind an explicit test-only construction flag.

findings.title: SAML signature verification is a noop stub returning signature_valid=True for any assertion containing a <ds:Signature> element
findings.detail: _check_signature (lines 266-277) discards signing_certificate_pem (`_ = signing_certificate_pem`) and returns `assertion_doc.select_ns('.//ds:Signature', _NAMESPACES) is not None` — i.e. True for ANY assertion that merely contains a <ds:Signature> element, with no XML-DSig cryptographic verification against the supplied IdP cert. The ABC contract (security_interfaces/.../isaml_assertion_adapter.py:29-33) requires 'Validating XML signature per SAML Core §5, when a signing certificate is supplied' and setting signature_valid accordingly. The result flows to SAMLAssertion(_signature_valid=signature_valid) at line 111, and the consuming SSO provider (security/layer_0_primitives/authentication/sso/saml_authentication_provider.py:202 `is_valid=assertion.signature_valid` and :271 `return assertion_data.signature_valid`) treats it as the authentication trust decision. An attacker can forge any assertion, paste an arbitrary (even invalid) <ds:Signature> element, and it is accepted as signature_valid=True — a SAML authentication bypass. The docstring openly labels it a stub deferred to 'a separate ticket'.

findings.title: SAML assertion _encryption_valid is hardcoded True, never computed
findings.detail: parse_response constructs SAMLAssertion(..., _encryption_valid=True) at line 112 with no decryption or encryption-validity check anywhere in the adapter. Any consumer that gates on _encryption_valid (a sibling of the signature_valid trust attribute) gets an unconditional pass, so encrypted-assertion integrity is asserted without ever being verified — fail-open by constant.

findings.title: SAML _parse_conditions defaults missing NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter to a now..now+1h window, silently widening validity
findings.detail: _parse_conditions (lines 193-221) sets default_not_before=now (line 197) and default_not_on_or_after=now+1h (line 198), and when the <saml:Conditions> element or its attributes are absent (lines 201-202, 207-216) returns this fabricated window instead of signalling 'no condition present'. A caller enforcing the validity window cannot distinguish an IdP-asserted window from a defaulted-on-missing one; an assertion with no Conditions silently becomes valid for the next hour. The catalog explicitly flags this default-on-missing as a trust hazard: a missing condition must not silently widen the window.

findings.title: AuthorizationRequestBuilder fabricates random IDs for missing or malformed principal/resource instead of failing closed
findings.detail: build() uses `principal.get('principal_id', str(uuid4()))` / `resource.get('resource_id', str(uuid4()))` (lines 35-36) and then, on a parse failure, swallows the ValueError/RuntimeError and substitutes a fresh `SubjectID()` / `ResourceID()` (lines 41-42, 46-47). A request whose principal_id is missing or malformed does not raise — it produces an AuthorizationRequest bound to an arbitrary random subject/resource that no authority resolved. This is the one place wire dicts become opaque IDs (per the catalog), so silently inventing identity here defeats fail-closed boundary discipline; a garbage/forged principal should be rejected, not coerced into a valid-shaped random ID.

findings.title: OAuthAuthorizationUriBuilder ships a hardcoded placeholder authorization endpoint used when none is configured
findings.detail: _DEFAULT_AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT = 'https://auth.example.com/authorize' (line 31) is the default for the constructor param (line 45) and build_authorization_uri never accepts an endpoint argument — it unconditionally emits `f'{self._default_authorization_endpoint}?{query}'` (line 101) when no oauth2_provider is injected. A caller that constructs the builder without overriding the default silently produces an authorization URI pointing at an external placeholder domain (example.com), carrying client_id/redirect_uri/state/code_challenge to a non-owned host. A missing required endpoint should fail closed rather than default to a wire-resolvable third-party domain.

findings.title: SPIFFE provider-path decoder substitutes now / now+1day for missing not_before / not_after, manufacturing a validity window
findings.detail: _decode_via_provider defaults not_before to `now` (line 64) and not_after to `now + timedelta(days=1)` (lines 65-69) when the provider's validation_result omits them, then surfaces these on SPIFFEDecodedSVID as authoritative not_before/not_after (lines 74-79). The catalog notes consumers enforce the cert window from these fields; a gateway checking the window cannot tell a provider-asserted expiry from a fabricated now+1day, so a provider that fails to report not_after yields an SVID that appears valid for a day. The window should be surfaced as unknown/absent, not defaulted to a permissive range.

findings.title: SAMLAssertionAdapter._check_signature is a stub that never verifies XML-DSig — signature_valid is forgeable
findings.detail: The ABC ISAMLAssertionAdapter (isaml_assertion_adapter.py:30-33) mandates "Validating XML signature per SAML Core §5, when a signing certificate is supplied" and setting signature_valid accordingly. The concrete _check_signature (line 266-277) explicitly discards the cert (`_ = signing_certificate_pem`, line 276) and returns True for the mere PRESENCE of any `<ds:Signature>` element (line 277), performing zero cryptographic verification. Per the normative catalog, the SSO service trusts an assertion solely on `signature_valid`; thus any attacker-crafted assertion containing an unverified (or attacker-signed, or empty) `<ds:Signature>` element yields signature_valid=True and is trusted — a complete SAML authentication bypass. The class docstring (lines 50-54) admits this is a stub. This concrete therefore does NOT satisfy its ABC contract.

findings.title: SAMLAssertionAdapter hardcodes _encryption_valid=True without any decryption/validation
findings.detail: parse_response constructs SAMLAssertion with `_encryption_valid=True` literally (line 112). Nothing in the adapter inspects or validates EncryptedAssertion/EncryptedID. A consumer gating on encryption_valid (parallel to the signature_valid pattern) would treat every assertion as encryption-verified regardless of reality — an unconditional pass for a security data attribute surfaced on the typed result.

findings.title: SAML parse fabricates valid subject/issuer/audience identities ('unknown') on missing fields instead of failing closed
findings.detail: When NameID/Issuer/Audience are absent the adapter substitutes the literal string 'unknown' into typed identities: issuer_id=SAMLIssuer.from_wire('unknown') (line 92), subject_id/name_id=UserID/NameID.from_string('unknown') (lines 93-94), audience_id=AudienceID.from_string('unknown') (line 95). Combined with the missing-NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter defaulting in _parse_conditions (lines 197-198, 207-216) which manufactures a now..now+1h validity window, a structurally minimal/forged assertion parses into a fully-populated, currently-valid typed assertion. The normative fail-closed invariant requires no silent fallback-to-defaults on missing required fields; here a missing subject must not become a usable identity.

findings.title: SPIFFE provider-path decoder defaults not_before/not_after to now..now+1day when provider omits them
findings.detail: _decode_via_provider (lines 61-69) substitutes `now` for a missing not_before and `now + timedelta(days=1)` for a missing not_after, then surfaces these on ISPIFFEDecodedSVID. The normative catalog explicitly WATCHes this: 'a defaulted window must not be treated as authoritative validity.' A consumer enforcing the certificate validity window via not_before/not_after (the documented gateway responsibility) cannot distinguish a real provider-asserted window from this fabricated 24h window, so a provider response lacking validity metadata yields a freshly-valid SVID.

findings.title: SPIFFE x509 decode path performs no chain/signature validation — only SAN parsing
findings.detail: When no spiffe_provider is injected, _decode_via_x509 (lines 81-91) builds the typed SVID via X509SVID.from_certificate, which (x509_svid.py:43-110) only loads the cert and extracts the SPIFFE SAN URI — it does NOT validate the certificate signature, trust chain, or validity window against any trust bundle (verify_certificate_chain at x509_svid.py:216 is never called and itself only checks the chain is non-empty). Unlike the provider path, this path has no `valid` gate; it returns an ISPIFFEDecodedSVID for any parseable cert bearing a spiffe:// SAN, including an attacker self-signed one. Identity binding then rests entirely on the (unverified-here) bundle, contradicting fail-closed SVID-decode expectations for an adapter that is the sole decode boundary.

findings.title: AuthorizationRequestBuilder fabricates a random subject/resource ID on missing or unparseable wire input
findings.detail: build() defaults a missing principal_id/resource_id to `str(uuid4())` (lines 35-36) and, on a from_string failure, swallows the exception and mints a brand-new random SubjectID()/ResourceID() (lines 41-47). The normative opaque-ID/fail-closed discipline forbids fabricating identity fields and requires no silent fallback on malformed input. A request body with an absent or malformed principal therefore yields a well-formed RANDOM subject rather than raising — masking malformed input and manufacturing an authorization subject from nothing instead of failing closed (the catalog's 'never fabricate a field' rule for trust inputs).

findings.title: OIDC discovery/token/userinfo adapters do not require or surface a caller-injected TLS policy
findings.detail: The normative OIDC property states discovery integrity 'rests on TLS' and the adapter 'applies caller-injected TLS policy (pinning/CA/mTLS).' The adapter only accepts an Optional[aiohttp.ClientSession] (line 35) and, when omitted, creates a bare `aiohttp.ClientSession()` (line 55) with default system TLS — no pinning/CA/mTLS hook. Same pattern in oidc_token_exchange_adapter.py:76 and oidc_userinfo_adapter.py:46. TLS policy can only be carried implicitly via an injected session's SSL context; the adapter neither requires it nor exposes a TLS-policy parameter, so a caller that omits the session silently gets default TLS for a security-critical metadata/token fetch.

findings.title: SPIFFE provider-path trust-domain extraction can IndexError on a malformed spiffe_id string
findings.detail: _decode_via_provider derives trust_domain via `spiffe_id_str.split('://')[1].split('/')[0]` (line 59) after only a truthiness check (line 55). A provider returning a non-empty spiffe_id lacking '://' raises IndexError rather than a typed/fail-closed ValueError, and the value is taken before SPIFFEID.from_string validation (line 72). Minor robustness/typing gap on the trusted-provider path.

findings.title: Unused IRelativeReference import in OIDC and OAuth adapters
findings.detail: `from network_interfaces import IURL, IRelativeReference` imports IRelativeReference which is never referenced (also oidc_userinfo_adapter.py:8 and oauth_authorization_uri_builder.py:19). Hygiene only — dead import, no behavioral impact.

findings.title: SAML signature verification is a no-op stub that returns valid on mere presence of a <ds:Signature> element
findings.detail: _check_signature (lines 266-277) explicitly discards the signing_certificate_pem argument (`_ = signing_certificate_pem`) and returns `assertion_doc.select_ns('.//ds:Signature', _NAMESPACES) is not None` — i.e. it reports signature_valid=True whenever ANY <ds:Signature> element is present, performing zero cryptographic XML-DSig verification against the IdP certificate. The normative catalog asserts the adapter 'parses & validates XML-DSig (SAML Core §5) when a signing cert is supplied'; it does not. Worse, this fake result is consumed as the authentication verdict: saml_authentication_provider.py line 202 sets `is_valid=assertion.signature_valid` and line 271 returns `assertion_data.signature_valid` as the signature-validation gate. An attacker can POST a forged SAML Response carrying an arbitrary subject NameID/attributes and a structurally-present-but-unverified <ds:Signature> element and authenticate as any user — full SSO auth bypass.

findings.title: SAMLAssertion._encryption_valid hardcoded True regardless of any encryption check
findings.detail: parse_response constructs the assertion with `_encryption_valid=True` unconditionally (line 112). No EncryptedAssertion/EncryptedID decryption or validation is performed anywhere in the adapter, yet the field reports the assertion's encryption state as valid. Any consumer gating on encryption_valid (the field is part of the typed assertion contract) is told encryption succeeded when nothing was checked, a fail-open on the confidentiality/integrity property for encrypted SAML assertions.

findings.title: AuthorizationRequestBuilder fails open to a fabricated random SubjectID on missing/malformed principal_id
findings.detail: build() defaults a missing `principal_id` to `str(uuid4())` (line 35) and, when `SubjectID.from_string` raises on malformed input, swallows the error and substitutes a brand-new random `SubjectID()` (lines 41-42; same pattern for resource_id at 44-47). Rather than rejecting an unparseable/absent subject, the builder mints a fresh opaque subject identity, so a request with a garbage or absent principal silently proceeds bound to an attacker-uninfluenceable but fabricated subject instead of failing closed. The catalog names this builder 'the ONE place wire dicts are converted to opaque IDs' and the cross-cutting invariant is DENY/raise on malformed input; here malformed input is normalised to a synthetic identity.

findings.title: SPIFFE provider-path decoder substitutes now / now+1day when provider omits not_before/not_after
findings.detail: _decode_via_provider defaults a missing not_before to `now` and a missing not_after to `now + timedelta(days=1)` (lines 61-69), then surfaces these as the SVID's authoritative validity window on ISPIFFEDecodedSVID. A consumer that enforces the certificate validity window (as the catalog requires) is handed a fabricated 24h-valid window for a cert whose real expiry is unknown/absent, so an expired or never-valid SVID can be treated as currently valid. The trust_domain is also derived by naive string-splitting of the provider-returned spiffe_id (line 59: `split('://')[1].split('/')[0]`) with no validation that it matches the cryptographically-verified SAN.

findings.title: OIDC discovery and token-exchange adapters apply no caller-injectable TLS policy (no pinning/CA/mTLS hook)
findings.detail: The catalog states discovery integrity 'rests on TLS' and the adapter 'applies caller-injected TLS policy (pinning/CA/mTLS)'. In practice _fetch_json (line 55) creates a bare `aiohttp.ClientSession()` with no ssl/connector argument and no parameter through which a caller could inject a pinned CA or mTLS context; the only injection point is an optional pre-built session. The same gap exists in oidc_token_exchange_adapter._post_form (line 76) and oidc_userinfo_adapter._get_authenticated_json (line 46). Default TLS validation still applies, but the documented pinning/mTLS control surface is absent, so discovery/token endpoints cannot be pinned at this layer — they rely entirely on ambient system trust.

findings.title: OAuthAuthorizationUriBuilder ships a default authorization endpoint constant
findings.detail: `_DEFAULT_AUTHORIZATION_ENDPOINT = 'https://auth.example.com/authorize'` (line 31) is used as the fallback base URL when no oauth2_provider is configured (line 101). The build_authorization_uri signature takes no authorization endpoint, so a misconfigured deployment that forgets to inject a provider will silently emit authorization URIs pointing at the placeholder host rather than failing closed. The fail-closed/no-silent-default invariant argues this default should be a required injected value, not a baked-in constant.

findings.title: DelegationPolicy is not a @dataclass — field defaults become class attributes, __post_init__ never runs
findings.detail: class DelegationPolicy (line 17) declares typed fields with defaults (policy_id, allowed_delegation_types, blacklisted_delegatees, enabled, etc.) and a __post_init__ (line 44) that uses object.__setattr__ to initialize collections, but the class has NO @dataclass decorator (contrast delegation_constraints.py:14 which does).
