Consequently: (1) all annotations are bare class-level attributes, the ones with values become shared class attributes; (2) there is no generated __init__, so DelegationPolicy(policy_id=..., ...) raises TypeError; (3) __post_init__ never fires, so blacklisted_delegatees/applicable_resources stay None. is_delegatee_allowed (line 67) and applies_to_resource (line 80) would operate on None/shared state. The policy object governing delegation depth/blacklists is structurally non-constructible and its guard methods are unreliable. It is exported from security/__init__.py:1013 as public API.

findings.title: DelegationRequest is not a @dataclass — non-constructible, __post_init__ dead
findings.detail: class DelegationRequest (line 19) has typed fields with defaults (urgency_level='normal', requires_approval=False, etc.) and a __post_init__ (line 50) defaulting delegation_scope/constraints, but no @dataclass decorator. No generated __init__ exists, so constructing a request fails; __post_init__ never runs leaving collections None; has_time_constraints (line 61) and get_constraint_by_type (line 68) iterate self.constraints which is the class-level None default. Exported as public API from security/__init__.py:1014 but never constructed anywhere in the repo.

findings.title: DelegationConstraint is not a @dataclass — non-constructible, defaults are shared class state
findings.detail: class DelegationConstraint (line 16) declares fields with defaults (mandatory=True, violation_action='deny', constraint_parameters=None) and a __post_init__ (line 36), but lacks @dataclass. No __init__ is generated; constraint_parameters never gets initialized to {} because __post_init__ never runs; get_parameter (line 47) reads a class-level None. This type is referenced by DelegationGrant/DelegationRequest/DelegationPolicy as their constraint container, so the whole common/delegation aggregate cannot be instantiated as designed.

findings.title: DelegationManager.create_delegation forges a fake cryptographic_proof from a plaintext token id
findings.detail: create_delegation builds cryptographic_proof = CryptographicSignature.from_wire(base64(b'proof_<token_id>')) (lines 71-77, comment 'Placeholder' at line 89); create_transitive_delegation does the same (lines 130-140, 152). This is NOT a signature — it is base64 of a public, predictable plaintext string derived solely from the (non-secret) token id, with no signing key involved. Any DelegationToken minted by this manager carries an unforgeable-looking-but-worthless proof; validate_delegation (line 162) never checks the proof at all, so the field is purely decorative. If anything downstream trusts cryptographic_proof as authority evidence, it is trivially forgeable.

findings.title: DelegationManager.validate_delegation never verifies cryptographic_proof, signer, or delegation chain
findings.detail: validate_delegation (lines 162-193) checks only is_revoked, expiry, and permission/resource subset. It does not verify cryptographic_proof, does not confirm the token came from this manager's store, and does not validate the parent chain or that the subject/actor entities are still valid (is_valid_at exists on the token at delegation_token.py:53 but is unused here). A caller passing any IDelegationToken-shaped object with is_revoked=False and a future expires_at is accepted as valid, with no authenticity check.

findings.title: DelegationManager.can_delegate authorizes delegation purely on entity_type equality
findings.detail: can_delegate (lines 355-372) returns subject_entity.entity_type == actor_entity.entity_type — i.e. ANY two entities of the same type may delegate to each other, with no policy, trust, or authority check. get_max_delegation_depth (line 374) likewise hardcodes return 3 regardless of delegation_type. Both are stub-grade policy. Neither is called by create_delegation's own path except get_max_delegation_depth, so can_delegate is effectively a never-enforced no-op gate.

findings.title: Entire DelegationManager implementation is dead code (never constructed; in-memory only)
findings.detail: DelegationManager is exported (security/__init__.py:1009) but grep across components+applications finds zero construction sites outside tests. It stores delegations in a process-local dict self._delegations (line 38), so revocation/cleanup/chain lookups are non-durable. Additionally most of its methods (create_transitive_delegation, refresh_delegation, revoke_all_delegations_for_entity, get_delegations_by_*, get_delegation_chain, cleanup_expired_delegations, can_delegate, get_max_delegation_depth) are NOT on the IDelegationManager ABC (which declares only create_delegation/validate_delegation/revoke_delegation), so they are off-contract methods on an unwired class.

findings.title: MandateService.revoke_mandate fails OPEN when no repository is injected
findings.detail: revoke_mandate (lines 266-291): if self._mandate_repository is None it returns True ('treat as success', line 290-291) without recording any revocation. mandate_repository is an optional constructor arg (line 44). A caller that revokes a mandate gets a True success signal but nothing is persisted or tracked — and verify_mandate (line 157) never consults any revocation list, only mandate.status/expiry/signature. Result: a 'revoked' mandate whose status field is still ACTIVE continues to verify and authorize. Revocation is silently a no-op in the default (no-repo) configuration.

findings.title: verify_mandate returns valid=True for an unsigned mandate
findings.detail: verify_mandate (lines 178-179): 'if not mandate.user_signature: return True, "Mandate is valid (no signature to verify)"'. A mandate with no signature passes verification on expiry+status alone. Combined with create_mandate only signing when signing_key is provided (lines 99,125 — validity_hours/expiry also only set when signing_key given, so unsigned mandates additionally never expire, expires_at=None), an unsigned, never-expiring mandate is accepted as cryptographically valid. The authority of a delegation mandate should not be grantable without a verified signature.

findings.title: ConstraintValidator.validate_resource_access raises RuntimeError (unfinished — fail-closed but a live landmine)
findings.detail: validate_resource_access (lines 263-291) unconditionally raises RuntimeError citing a TODO(opaque-id-architecture) — an unimplemented ABC method. It is not a NotImplementedError stub but functionally identical: any caller invoking this method crashes. validate_operational (lines 293-313) is a different shape of stub: it ignores the 'operation' argument entirely and returns (True,'') for any non-None operational constraint, so operational authorization via this entrypoint is a no-op pass (note validate_constraints at line 65 does check max_compute_tokens, but the per-method validate_operational does not).

findings.title: ConstraintValidator.validate_constraints computes 'action' then discards it
findings.detail: Line 42 'context.get("action", "")' fetches the action but assigns it nowhere — the result is discarded. No constraint in validate_constraints ever evaluates the action, so per-action restriction is silently unenforced at this layer (action allow-listing happens only in MandateService.check_authorization line 246). A reader expecting the constraint validator to gate on action gets nothing.

findings.title: RoleAuditEntry.get_retention_until uses //365, drifting retention short by days
findings.detail: get_retention_until (lines 64-68) computes retention as performed_at.replace(year = year + retention_period_days//365). With the 2555-day (7-year) default this is 2555//365 = 7 years (ok), but for any non-multiple-of-365 retention the day/month are ignored entirely (replace only bumps year), so the retention date can be off by up to a year and ignores leap years. A Feb-29 performed_at would also raise ValueError on replace. Compliance retention math should add a timedelta, not mutate the year field.

findings.title: RoleAuditEntry.involves_subject compares an Optional[str] field against an ISubjectID
findings.detail: involves_subject(subject_id: ISubjectID) does 'self.subject_id == subject_id or self.performed_by == subject_id' (line 73), but self.subject_id and self.performed_by are plain Optional[str]/str (lines 31,28) while subject_id is an opaque ISubjectID. A str == opaque-ID comparison will be False unless the opaque ID's __eq__ accepts raw str, so this audit-search predicate likely never matches — a silently-broken 'does this entry involve subject X' query. involves_role (line 78) has the same str-vs-IRoleID mismatch.

findings.title: DelegationGrant.can_be_used has a dead context-matching branch (pass)
findings.detail: can_be_used (lines 95-109): the branch 'if context and self.business_context:' (line 105) contains only a comment 'Would implement context matching logic' and 'pass' (line 107) — business-context constraint matching is unimplemented, so can_be_used ignores context entirely and a context that should be rejected is silently allowed. DelegationGrant correctly uses @dataclass (line 20) unlike its sibling types, but this guard is a stub.

findings.title: security.delegation.ConstraintEvaluator swallows all exceptions to ConstraintOutcome.ERROR with no logging
findings.detail: evaluate_constraint (lines 50-59) catches bare 'except Exception' and returns ConstraintOutcome.ERROR; the comment claims 'Log error' (line 57) but nothing is actually logged — self._error_factory (line 30) is constructed and never used. Failures in constraint.validate are silently classified as ERROR with no diagnostic trail. Note this ConstraintEvaluator (in security/delegation/) is distinct from the actively-used verification.ConstraintEvaluator; grep shows the security/delegation one is exported (security/__init__.py:328) but never constructed outside its own package, so it is also effectively dead code.

findings.title: DelegationRequest is not a @dataclass — __post_init__ never runs, defaults are shared class attributes
findings.detail: class DelegationRequest declares class-level annotated fields with defaults (request_id, delegator_id, delegatee_id, delegation_scope=None, constraints=None, etc.) and defines __post_init__ (line 50) which calls object.__setattr__ to materialize empty set/list defaults — but the class has NO @dataclass decorator (confirmed: line 19 is a bare `class DelegationRequest:`). Consequence: there is no generated __init__, so the opaque-ID fields (request_id/delegator_id/delegatee_id) cannot be supplied positionally; __post_init__ is dead code (never invoked by Python without @dataclass); and the annotated 'defaults' like delegation_scope=None become a single shared mutable class attribute. applies_to_resource() (line 80) then reads self.delegation_scope which is the class-level None unless callers set it manually. This domain object is structurally non-constructable as intended.

findings.title: DelegationConstraint is not a @dataclass — __post_init__ default-population never executes
findings.detail: class DelegationConstraint (line 16, bare class, no @dataclass) declares constraint_id: IConstraintID, constraint_type, constraint_parameters=None, mandatory=True, violation_action='deny', and a __post_init__ (line 36) that sets constraint_parameters={} and created_at=now. Without @dataclass there is no __init__ and __post_init__ is never called, so get_parameter()/is_mandatory_constraint() read shared class-level attributes. is_time_based() (line 54) and constraint_type are unusable per-instance. Same structural defect as delegation_request.py.

findings.title: DelegationPolicy is not a @dataclass — policy fields are shared class state, __post_init__ dead
findings.detail: class DelegationPolicy (line 17, bare class, no @dataclass) declares policy_id, blacklisted_delegatees, maximum_delegation_depth, etc. with defaults and a __post_init__ (line 44). is_delegatee_allowed() (line 67) returns True when self.blacklisted_delegatees is falsy — since blacklisted_delegatees is a class-level None/empty-set never populated per instance (no @dataclass init runs), every delegatee is allowed by default and the blacklist is structurally bypassed. This is an authorization-relevant fail-open: a populated blacklist passed at construction would have no constructor to accept it.

findings.title: MandateService.revoke_mandate fails open — returns True when no repository is injected
findings.detail: revoke_mandate (line 266) returns True at line 291 with comment 'No repository available — treat as success (in-memory / test mode)' when self._mandate_repository is None. The interface contract (imandate_service.py line 69) says 'Returns True if revoked'. A caller wiring the service without a repository (the repository is Optional, default None per __init__ line 43) gets a successful-revocation signal while nothing is persisted/revoked. A revoked mandate therefore remains ACTIVE and continues to authorize via verify_mandate/check_authorization. Revocation must fail closed (raise or return False) when it cannot actually revoke.

findings.title: verify_mandate trusts the public key carried on the mandate it is verifying — signature is self-certifying
findings.detail: verify_mandate reconstructs the payload and verifies the signature using mandate.user_public_key_hex (line 209-214), which is a field stored ON the mandate at creation (create_mandate line 132). The verifier never binds that public key to a trusted user identity (user_id/user_did) via any registry or key-resolution authority. Any party can generate an ECDSA keypair, sign a forged mandate granting arbitrary actions, and attach the matching public key; verify_mandate returns (True, 'verified cryptographically'). This is signature-without-trust-anchor: the catalog's credential-injection discipline requires the verifying key to come from a trusted authority, not from the artifact being verified.

findings.title: ConstraintValidator.validate_resource_access is an always-raising stub of an abstract method
findings.detail: validate_resource_access (line 263) overrides the IConstraintValidator abstractmethod (iconstraint_validator.py line 51) but unconditionally `raise RuntimeError(...)` at line 287 with a TODO(opaque-id-architecture). This is a stub implementation: any caller invoking the resource-access validation path crashes with RuntimeError rather than returning a fail-closed (False, reason). Per project rules a concrete must fully implement its ABC with no NotImplementedError-only/always-raising methods. Note the parallel validate_constraints() path DOES check resource access via _check_resource_access, so the two resource-validation entry points are inconsistent.

findings.title: prove_monotonicity omits prohibited_resources, data_classifications, currencies, providers, and temporal window — sub-delegation can widen scope
findings.detail: prove_monotonicity (line 74) only checks allowed_resources subset, three financial maxima, three operational maxima, and the two approval/human-review booleans. It never validates that the child does not shrink prohibited_resources (child could drop a parent-prohibited resource — line 48 shows prohibited_resources is enforced elsewhere), nor data_classifications, allowed_currencies, allowed_providers, nor the temporal window (child start/end could be wider than parent). A child mandate can therefore relax these dimensions while passing the monotonicity proof, contradicting the docstring guarantee that 'sub-delegation cannot grant additional permissions beyond what the parent allows.'

findings.title: RoleAuditEntry compares opaque ISubjectID/IRoleID against raw str fields and stores _id: str outside adapters
findings.detail: RoleAuditEntry stores role_id/subject_id/permission_id as Optional[str] (lines 32-34) — raw _id: str in a non-adapter/non-generated domain object. involves_subject(self, subject_id: ISubjectID) (line 71) evaluates `self.subject_id == subject_id or self.performed_by == subject_id`, comparing a str (and performed_by:str) to an opaque ISubjectID; involves_role (line 78) compares str self.role_id to IRoleID. Opaque IDs do not equate to bare strings, so these predicates return False for legitimately-matching subjects/roles (or rely on accidental __eq__ behavior). This breaks the involves_* contract and violates opaque-ID discipline at an internal boundary.

findings.title: DelegationManager.create_delegation cryptographic_proof is a fake base64-of-log-string placeholder
findings.detail: create_delegation builds crypto_proof = CryptographicSignature.from_wire(base64(f'proof_{token_id.to_log_string()}')) (lines 70-77, explicitly commented '# Placeholder' at line 89); create_transitive_delegation does the same (lines 130-140). The DelegationToken.cryptographic_proof field is typed ICryptographicSignature and is meant to prove provenance, but here it is a deterministic, non-cryptographic, unkeyed encoding of the public token id — forgeable by anyone who knows the token id. validate_delegation (line 162) never checks cryptographic_proof at all, so the unforgeable-proof invariant of a delegation token is absent. Combined with the in-memory-only _delegations dict (line 38), tokens carry no real cryptographic binding.

findings.title: DelegationToken declared immutable but is a non-frozen dataclass mutated by manager (refresh/revoke)
findings.detail: DelegationToken (line 21) is `@dataclass` without frozen=True yet its docstring (line 23) and the IDelegationToken contract describe it as 'Immutable delegation token'. DelegationManager.refresh_delegation mutates delegation_token.expires_at in place (delegation_manager.py line 213) and revoke_all_delegations_for_entity sets token.is_revoked = True (line 264). A token claimed immutable but mutated in place defeats audit/trust-chain integrity assumptions and means a leaked reference can have its expiry extended after issuance.

findings.title: DelegationManager.can_delegate authorizes delegation purely on matching entity_type
findings.detail: can_delegate (line 355) returns `subject_entity.entity_type == actor_entity.entity_type` (line 372) as the sole gate on whether delegation is permitted. This grants delegation between any two entities of the same type with no policy, no authority check, and no relationship verification, and create_delegation never even calls can_delegate. Effectively an authorization no-op for delegation issuance.

findings.title: validate_constraints discards the parsed 'action' value (dead read)
findings.detail: Line 41 `context.get("action", "")` retrieves the action from context but assigns it to nothing and is never used; only 'resource' (line 42) is consumed. The action dimension of the execution context is therefore never validated against any constraint in validate_constraints, despite check_authorization/check_action_permitted building full_context specifically to carry the action (mandate_service.py lines 251, 317).

findings.title: verify_mandate trusts the public key carried inside the mandate it is verifying (key self-binding)
findings.detail: verify_mandate reconstructs the payload and verifies the ECDSA signature using public_key = VerifyingKey.from_string(bytes.fromhex(mandate.user_public_key_hex), ...) (lines 209-214). The verifying key is read from mandate.user_public_key_hex — a field on the SAME mandate object that also carries user_signature (line 213) — and is never bound to or checked against the claimed user_id / user_did. An attacker forging a mandate generates a fresh keypair, signs their forged payload, and stores their own pubkey in user_public_key_hex; verify_mandate then reports 'Mandate signature verified cryptographically' (line 215). The signature proves only internal consistency, not that the claimed user authorized anything — a full authenticity bypass for the delegation mandate.

findings.title: verify_mandate fails OPEN for an unsigned mandate (returns valid)
findings.detail: After expiry/status checks, verify_mandate hits `if not mandate.user_signature: return True, 'Mandate is valid (no signature to verify)'` (lines 178-179). A mandate with user_signature=None is accepted as valid with no cryptographic proof of authorization whatsoever. Combined with check_authorization (line 219) and check_action_permitted (line 293) — which only check agent_did match, action membership and constraints, never the signature — a forged unsigned mandate authorizes actions. Verification must fail closed when proof is absent.

findings.title: Unsigned mandates are created with no expiry, so they never expire
findings.detail: create_mandate sets validity_hours = 24 if signing_key else None (line 99) and expires_at = now + timedelta(hours=validity_hours) if validity_hours else None (line 100). When no signing_key is supplied the mandate has expires_at=None. verify_mandate only rejects on expiry when `mandate.expires_at` is truthy (line 170), so such a mandate is valid forever. Token expiry is an invariant in the catalog; an unsigned, non-expiring, fail-open-accepted mandate is a durable bypass primitive.

findings.title: prove_monotonicity skips any constraint the child omits, letting sub-delegation escape parent limits
findings.detail: Every monotonicity check is gated on BOTH sides being present, e.g. `if parent_constraints.financial and child_constraints.financial:` (line 104), same for resource_access (line 94) and operational (line 124). If the parent imposes a financial cap (e.g. max_transaction_amount=100) but the child's financial constraint is None, the entire block is skipped and prove_monotonicity returns is_monotonic=True. Because DelegationConstraints fields default to None (delegation_constraints.py lines 18-21), a child that simply omits a constraint dimension is treated as monotonic while actually being UNCONSTRAINED in that dimension — the opposite of the intended 'child must be at least as restrictive' property. Privilege escalation via sub-delegation.

findings.title: _check_resource_access returns allow when allowed_resources is empty
findings.detail: _check_resource_access returns True ('No restrictions') when constraints.allowed_resources is empty (lines 180-181). ResourceAccessConstraints.allowed_resources defaults to an empty list (resource_access_constraints.py line 12), so a resource_access constraint object that was constructed to PROHIBIT access (prohibited_resources populated, allowed_resources left empty) grants access to every non-prohibited resource by default. An empty allow-list in a security constraint should fail closed (deny), not open.

findings.title: DelegationManager cryptographic_proof is a base64 label string, not a signature; validate_delegation never checks it
findings.detail: create_delegation builds cryptographic_proof = CryptographicSignature.from_wire(base64.b64encode(f'proof_{token_id...}'.encode())...) (lines 70-77, 89) — base64 of a plaintext label derived only from the token id, with no key and no signing operation; the comment even says '# Placeholder'. The same in create_transitive_delegation (lines 130-140, 152). validate_delegation (lines 161-193) checks is_revoked, expiry, and permission/resource subset but NEVER verifies cryptographic_proof. The token's authenticity field is forgeable by anyone who knows the token id and is never validated, so DelegationToken provides no cryptographic provenance despite the IDelegationToken contract.

findings.title: DelegationManager.can_delegate authorizes solely on entity_type equality
findings.detail: can_delegate returns `subject_entity.entity_type == actor_entity.entity_type` (line 372) — any entity may delegate to any other entity of the same type, with no policy, trust-chain, or authority check. Moreover create_delegation (line 42) and create_transitive_delegation (line 97) never call can_delegate at all, so even this weak gate is dead: a delegation token is minted for any subject/actor pair the caller supplies, with no verification that the actor is entitled to act for the subject.

findings.title: validate_resource_access is a runtime stub that raises RuntimeError instead of validating
findings.detail: validate_resource_access (the IConstraintValidator contract method, iconstraint_validator.py line 51) unconditionally raises RuntimeError('Resource access validation requires architectural clarity ...') after an early-return-True when constraints.resource_access is None (lines 279-291). Any caller that relies on this dedicated resource-access check to gate a delegation either crashes (DoS) or, if the constraint object happens to have resource_access=None, gets a silent allow (line 280). The contract method does not perform the validation it promises; resource-scope enforcement here cannot be trusted.

findings.title: validate_operational always returns valid regardless of operation or limits
findings.detail: validate_operational returns (True, '') whenever constraints.operational is None (lines 309-310) and otherwise falls through to `return (True, '')` (line 313) with only the comment 'Operation verification can be extended as needed' — it never checks the supplied `operation` string against allowed_providers or any operational limit. The dedicated operational-constraint gate is a no-op; only the batch validate_constraints path checks compute_tokens, so any caller invoking validate_operational directly receives an unconditional allow.

findings.title: Domain constraint/policy/request classes are not dataclasses, so __post_init__ never runs and defaults stay None
findings.detail: DelegationPolicy (delegation_policy.py line 17), DelegationRequest (delegation_request.py line 19) and DelegationConstraint (delegation_constraint.py line 16) declare class-level annotated fields and a __post_init__ that calls object.__setattr__ to replace None defaults with empty sets/lists, but none of the three carry a @dataclass decorator (contrast DelegationGrant line 20 which does). Without @dataclass there is no generated __init__ and __post_init__ is never invoked, so the None-to-empty-collection normalization never happens. Policy predicates such as is_delegatee_allowed (delegation_policy.py line 67) and allows_delegation_type (line 61) treat None/empty as 'allow all' — a blacklist that was meant to be populated but left at its class default of None silently permits every delegatee. These types appear non-instantiable as written and, if instantiated via an alternate path, fail open on their allow/deny predicates.

findings.title: LTL tool-access integration fails OPEN when the security layer raises
findings.detail: In check_unified_tool_access, security_allowed is initialized to True (line 184) and only set False when check_tool_access() returns a deny (line 203/207). If self.security_controller.check_tool_access() RAISES, the except block (lines 210-214) merely calls verification_context.add_warning('...proceeding with LTL only') and leaves security_allowed == True. overall_allowed = security_allowed and ltl_allowed (line 257) then depends solely on the LTL layer; if the LTL layer is disabled, also raises (lines 250-254 likewise swallow and leave ltl_allowed True), or returns allow, the request is GRANTED despite the security layer never having produced a decision. The module docstring (lines 54-56) explicitly promises 'Fail-closed: Deny if either layer denies' — a raised exception in a security check must be treated as a denial, not silently dropped to allow.

findings.title: MCP threat-detection / tool-access family is dead code (never constructed by any consumer)
findings.detail: MCPThreatDetector, PromptInjectionProtector, PromptSanitizer, ToolAccessController and LTLToolAccessIntegration are referenced ONLY by the security package's own re-export __init__.py chain. grep across components/ and applications/ finds zero production caller of analyze_mcp_request / analyze_prompt_content / protect_prompt / check_tool_access / check_unified_tool_access outside the security tree (the single hit is a docstring in security_interfaces iaccess_decision.py). The entire XPIA/prompt-injection/tool-poisoning detection subsystem is wired to nothing, so none of its protections actually run in any request path — a security control that exists in code but is never invoked provides no protection while creating a false impression of coverage.

findings.title: RiskScore.__post_init__ never runs — range validation is dead (class is not a dataclass)
findings.detail: RiskScore declares class-level annotated fields and a __post_init__ that validates 0.0<=overall_risk_score<=1.0 and 0.0<=confidence_level<=1.0 and substitutes mutable defaults (lines 39-49), but the class has NO @dataclass decorator (only `class RiskScore(IRiskScore):` at line 16). __post_init__ is invoked only by dataclass-generated __init__, so it never executes; the class also has no usable __init__ to set the annotated fields. The bounds checks that would reject an out-of-range risk score are therefore inert. (No production constructor was found, so impact is latent, but as written the validation invariant is silently non-functional.)

findings.title: TrustVerificationContext.__post_init__ never runs and class is uninstantiable as designed (not a dataclass)
findings.detail: Like RiskScore, TrustVerificationContext (line 18 `class TrustVerificationContext:` with no @dataclass) defines annotated fields plus a __post_init__ (lines 41-48) that defaults environment_context/security_context/required_evidence_types and uses object.__setattr__. With no dataclass decorator there is no generated __init__, so the fields are only class-level annotations and __post_init__ is never called; required_evidence_types would default to None and has_required_evidence()/get_evidence_by_type() would operate on unset attributes. The type is dead (no constructor found) but the default-population logic that guards has_required_evidence() is non-functional as written.

findings.title: LegalWrapper does not enforce that the NPE owner is a party WITH legal capacity
findings.detail: The class docstring states a Machine Actor 'MUST be owned by a Party with legal capacity (Natural Person or Organization)' and MachineActor (machine_actor.py) relies on the LegalWrapper to carry that ownership guarantee. LegalWrapper itself accepts any IEntityIdentity as owner_entity (line 59) with no validation that the owner actually has FULL/non-NONE legal capacity. Nothing prevents constructing a LegalWrapper whose owner is itself another machine (capacity NONE), defeating the stated invariant that liability ultimately rests on a legally-capable party. The structural guarantee the design claims is not enforced anywhere.

findings.title: ToolAccessController grants admin path on caller-supplied user_role string with no binding to verified identity
findings.detail: ADMIN_REQUIRED tools are gated solely on context.user_role.lower() in ['admin','administrator','system'] (lines 124-125), where user_role is a free-form string carried on AccessContext (access_context.py line 20) and, in the LTL integration, derived from a caller-passed UserRole enum value (ltl_tool_access_integration.py line 196). There is no cross-check that the asserted role is bound to the verified user_id / a non-body proof channel — the role is self-asserted request data. If this controller were ever wired (see dead-code finding), an admin role claim would bypass the REQUIRE_APPROVAL path purely on an unverified string. Defect is latent because the subsystem is currently unwired.

findings.title: score_to_level has an unreachable hard-coded fallback ladder that can silently disagree with configured thresholds
findings.detail: score_to_level iterates the provided thresholds (lines 58-60) and, if no band matches, falls back to a hard-coded ladder (lines 63-71: >=80 VERIFIED, >=60 HIGH, ...). For any non-contiguous custom threshold map (e.g. a high-security context that deliberately leaves a gap so mid scores are UNTRUSTED), a score landing in the gap is silently re-bucketed by the default ladder rather than failing closed to UNTRUSTED, quietly overriding the caller's stricter context. The branch is dead for the default contiguous map but is a fail-open hazard for any sparse/strict custom threshold configuration.

findings.title: ToolAccessController.reset path in LTL integration swallows controller re-init failures
findings.detail: reset() re-creates the security controller inside try/except that silently `pass`es on failure (lines 355-359), and __init__ does the same (lines 117-123, setting enable_security_layer=False on failure). A failed re-init leaves self.security_controller potentially stale/None while enable_security_layer may still read True in the get_statistics branch, and combined with the fail-open behavior above means a degraded security layer is not surfaced to callers as a hard error. Hygiene/observability concern compounding the fail-open finding.

findings.title: LTLToolAccessIntegration fails OPEN when a verification layer raises (contradicts documented fail-closed)
findings.detail: check_unified_tool_access initializes security_allowed=True (line 185) and ltl_allowed=True (line 218). The security-layer try/except (lines 188-214) and LTL-layer try/except (lines 221-254) catch ALL exceptions and only call verification_context.add_warning, never flipping the *_allowed flag to False. So if the security layer raises (e.g. ToolAccessController bug, AccessContext construction error) the code 'proceeds with LTL only' with security_allowed still True; if BOTH layers raise, overall_allowed = True and True = ALLOW. The class docstring explicitly promises 'Fail-closed: Deny if either layer denies' (lines 53-56), but an errored layer is treated as approval. A crash in the gate becomes an access grant.

findings.title: LTL layer enabled but ltl_engine None still grants access (silent verification skip)
findings.detail: ltl_allowed defaults to True and the LTL block runs only `if self.enable_ltl_layer and self.ltl_engine`. The only hard guard against running with zero active layers is at lines 170-176 (both disabled). If the security layer silently degrades to disabled in __init__ (lines 117-123 swallow any ToolAccessController() exception and set enable_security_layer=False) while the LTL engine is also unavailable for any non-construction reason, the request is approved with no check actually performed. Combined with the exception fail-open above, a single component fault converts the gate into allow-all rather than deny.

findings.title: allow_html=True sanitization escapes only <script> and javascript:, leaving XSS vectors un-neutralized
findings.detail: _sanitize_content with allow_html=True strips only and the literal 'javascript:' substring (lines 502-508) and then does NOT html.escape the remainder (the escape is in the else branch, line 511). Event-handler payloads (e.g. ,

findings.title: Injection/role/bypass detection runs on raw input before Unicode normalization, enabling homoglyph/zero-width bypass
findings.detail: protect_prompt (and detect_role_manipulation/detect_system_bypass/detect_tool_hijacking) apply ASCII regex patterns directly to the raw `content` with no prior Unicode NFC normalization or zero-width-char stripping (normalization only happens later, and only inside the separate PromptSanitizer class). An attacker can insert zero-width characters or use confusable homoglyphs (e.g. 'ad​min', Cyrillic 'а') so phrases like 'ignore all instructions' / 'i am admin' evade every regex while still being interpreted downstream. These are heuristic detectors, but the gap means safety_score can report 1.0 for an evasive injection.

findings.title: TrustVerificationContext declares __post_init__ but is not a @dataclass — instances are broken/uninstantiable
findings.detail: The class has only class-level attribute annotations (verification_id, subject_id, required_evidence_types, etc.) and a __post_init__, but no @dataclass decorator and no __init__. __post_init__ is invoked only by a dataclass-generated __init__, so it never runs; the documented constructor fields cannot be set, and methods like has_required_evidence() reference self.required_evidence_types which would raise AttributeError (the annotation has no value). grep finds no non-test construction. A trust-verification context object that silently cannot be built (or whose required-evidence defaulting never executes) is a latent fail-unsafe if ever wired in.

findings.title: CrossDomainTrustConfiguration declares __post_init__ and security defaults but is not a @dataclass
findings.detail: Same defect class as TrustVerificationContext: no @dataclass decorator, so __post_init__ (lines 45-61) never fires and no __init__ exists. The security-relevant policy defaults (allow_transitive_trust=False, require_mutual_authentication=True, default_trust_level=TrustLevel.LOW) are bare class attributes; is_domain_trusted()/supports_protocol() read self.trusted_domains / self.supported_protocols which are annotation-only (no value) and would raise AttributeError. The class is unusable as written and unused outside tests; if adopted, the defaulting of trusted_domains/required_evidence_types would not occur.

findings.title: score_to_level fallback cutoffs diverge from configured threshold ranges, mismapping gap scores
findings.detail: All threshold dicts (DEFAULT_TRUST_THRESHOLDS etc.) have 1.0-wide gaps between bands (UNTRUSTED ends 20.0, LOW starts 21.0; MEDIUM ends 60.0, HIGH starts 61.0). A fractional score landing in a gap (e.g. 60.5) matches no band in the loop (lines 58-60) and falls through to a hard-coded fallback (lines 63-71) whose cutoffs (>=80 VERIFIED, >=60 HIGH, >=40 MEDIUM...) do NOT match the active threshold profile. For the security-hardened profiles (e.g. HIGH_SECURITY where MEDIUM is 76-85) a gap score is classified by the generic fallback, which can over-promote trust relative to the intended profile. Tighten bands to be contiguous or remove the divergent fallback.

findings.title: SecurityOrchestrator: all 7 evaluation stages are hardcoded stubs that allow-by-default
findings.detail: evaluate_complete_security_context orchestrates _authenticate/_verify_identity/_resolve_attributes/_evaluate_trust/_evaluate_authorization/_handle_obligations/_audit_decision, but every one of those private stages is a stub returning a hardcoded success value: _authenticate returns {'authenticated': True} (l.245), _evaluate_trust returns 1.0 (l.270), _evaluate_authorization returns {'authorized': True, 'decision_id': 'default'} (l.281), _audit_decision is a bare `pass` (l.298). The public method therefore reports authenticated=True/authorized=True for ANY input with NO authentication, identity, trust, or policy check, and never emits an audit record. This is a fail-open authorization engine. It implements ISecurityOrchestrator so it can be DI-substituted as the real PDP. evaluate_authorization_only (l.212) likewise hardcodes trust_score=1.0 and returns PERMIT.

findings.title: SecurityCoordinator.authenticate_user reads non-existent 'success' key — gateway contract is dead-wired (always fails closed)
findings.detail: authenticate_user calls self._authentication_gateway.authenticate_user_with_presentation(...) then branches on `if not auth_result.get('success')`. But AuthenticationGatewayImpl.authenticate_user_with_presentation returns AuthenticatedUserContext.to_dict(), whose keys (authenticated_user_context.py l.50-64) are user_did/authentication_method/verified_claims/trust_score/session_id/... — there is no 'success' key and no 'failure_reason'. So auth_result.get('success') is always None, the coordinator always takes the failure branch and returns CoordinatorAuthenticationResult(success=False) for every caller, even after the gateway successfully authenticated. The two layers were never wired to a shared response contract; the success path (trust scoring, method mapping at l.217-253) is unreachable dead code. Fail-closed here, but the contract break means the entire coordinator-over-gateway authentication path is non-functional.

findings.title: ContextPropagator signs/verifies security context with a hardcoded HMAC key 'default-key'
findings.detail: __init__ sets self._signature_key = 'default-key'  (comment 'Should be from config') and _create_signature (l.272) computes HMAC-SHA256 over the context payload with that constant key. decode_context_from_transport (l.103) verifies the inbound signature against the same hardcoded key. Because the key is a compile-time constant shipped in source, any attacker can forge a valid X-Security-Signature for an arbitrary principal/trust_score/scopes payload, defeating the entire tamper-protection the propagator claims to provide. The class violates the credential-injection invariant (signing keys must be DI/constructor params, never literals).

findings.title: ContextPropagator accepts unsigned context — signature check skipped when signature header absent
findings.detail: In decode_context_from_transport the signature is only verified inside `if signature:` (l.158). If an attacker supplies X-Security-Context but omits the X-Security-Signature header, `signature` is None, the verification block is skipped, and the base64 JSON payload is decoded and returned as trusted context (l.174-178) with no integrity check at all. A forged principal/trust context is accepted simply by not sending a signature. Verification must fail closed when the signature is missing.

findings.title: ContextCarrierImpl fabricates a fully-trusted placeholder security context when no propagator configured
findings.detail: decode_from_transport (l.63), when self._context_propagator is None, returns a hardcoded placeholder {'principal_id':'user123','trust_score':0.85,'scopes':['read','write'],'signature_valid':True,...} (l.88-98). extract_context_from_request similarly returns a hardcoded service principal with trust_score 0.95 and signature_valid:True (l.236-243), and in its base64 fallback sets context['signature_valid']=True with the comment 'Would verify in production' (l.228) — i.e. it stamps any base64 blob as signature-valid without verifying anything. A deployment that forgets to inject a propagator silently authenticates every request as a trusted principal. encode_for_transport also emits dummy header values 'eyJ...'/'sha256:abc123...' (l.57-60).

findings.title: VerifyIdentityImpl returns verified=True / trusted placeholders when no provider configured
findings.detail: Across IIdentityGateway methods, when the injected provider/verifier is None the impl returns hardcoded positive results instead of failing closed: verify_identity returns {'verified':True,'trust_level':'high'} (l.77-83); verify_credential returns {'verified':True,...} (l.241-247); check_credential_trust returns {'is_trusted':True,'trust_level':'high','reason':'Issuer is in trusted root chain'} (l.345-350); get_trusted_issuers returns a static set of example issuer DIDs (l.306-309). Any consumer that gates on these results grants trust to unverified identities/credentials whenever the gateway is partially configured. Trust decisions must default to deny/unverified, not allow.

findings.title: CredentialIssuerImpl returns valid:true credential-verification placeholder when issuer not configured
findings.detail: verify_credential (l.72) returns {'valid':True,'verified_claims':{},'issuer_did':'did:example:issuer'} (l.92-96) when self._credential_issuer is None, and get_trusted_issuers returns a hardcoded high/medium-trust issuer list (l.230-244). A caller verifying a VC against an unconfigured issuer receives 'valid' for arbitrary input. Verification of a credential must not default to valid.

findings.title: AuthenticationGatewayImpl swallows token-parse failures and emits placeholder identity (no signature verification on VP/OAuth path)
findings.detail: authenticate_user_with_presentation uses self._codec.peek_claims_from_wire(vp_token) (l.117) — an UNVERIFIED diagnostic read per the codec contract — and feeds the resulting issuer/subject straight into the returned AuthenticatedUserContext with trust_score 0.85-0.90 (l.132-176). On any exception it logs 'using placeholder data' and proceeds to build an authenticated context anyway (l.153-179), defaulting user_did to 'did:example:user123' and verified_claims to {sub:user123,...}. authenticate_external_oauth_token similarly peeks unverified claims (l.234) and on parse failure fabricates verified_claims for user123@provider (l.253-261); OIDC verify_token failure is also swallowed (l.276-279) and the unverified peeked claims are returned. No signature is ever verified on these authentication paths, so a forged/unsigned token still yields an 'authenticated' context.

findings.title: SecurityGatewayFacade health check treats unknown/unconfigured components as healthy and swallows check errors
findings.detail: _check_component_health returns 'healthy' for any component whose health_check returns an unknown type (l.210) or that lacks a health_check method (l.216), and maps exceptions to 'degraded' (l.211-213). Combined with health_check() treating 'not_configured' as acceptable for overall_healthy (l.177-179), a facade with all security gateways missing reports overall_status 'healthy'. Operational signal is misleading rather than fail-closed.

findings.title: AuditEventReporterImpl reports success ('recorded':True) when no audit aggregator is configured
findings.detail: Every report_* method, when self._audit_aggregator is None, logs the event locally and returns the module-level _LOCAL_LOGGER_FALLBACK = {'recorded':True,'backend':'local_logger'} (l.14, l.52-57, repeated for authorization/identity/token/credential/trust/anomaly). Callers that check the returned 'recorded' flag believe the security event was durably audited when it was only emitted to a logger and not persisted. For an audit/compliance sink this is an integrity gap: dropped audit records reported as recorded.

findings.title: AuditEventAggregator is in-memory only and never correlates events (request_id helper hardwired to None)
findings.detail: This is the concrete IAuditEventAggregator used by SecurityCoordinator/SecurityEventBus/TrustEvaluator. _request_id_for is a static method that unconditionally returns None (l.40-46), so _record never indexes any event by request id; correlate_events_for_request (l.93) therefore always returns an empty CorrelatedEventLog regardless of how many events were recorded. Events are also held only in a Python list with no durable sink, so all audit state is lost on process restart. Audit correlation — a stated purpose of the aggregator — is a no-op.

findings.title: SecurityEventBus drops subscriber handler exceptions to print() (fire-and-forget, no audit of failure)
findings.detail: _invoke_handler catches all exceptions from security event handlers and merely `print(f'Error in event handler: {e}')` (l.388-390) — not via the logging/error_handling framework — and swallows them. Subscribers are dispatched via asyncio.create_task with no reference retained (l.370), so failures in audit/monitoring/compliance subscribers are silently lost and the publisher never learns delivery failed. Security-event delivery failures should be surfaced through error_handling, not a bare print.

findings.title: ComplianceMonitor control validation passes on trivial 10-char evidence presence check
findings.detail: _validate_control (l.271) determines whether a SOC2/ISO27001/HIPAA/PCI/GDPR control passes purely by checking that the required evidence keys are present and each value has len(strip()) >= 10 (l.294-302). Any string of 10+ characters marks a regulatory control as compliant, and compliance_score/get_compliance_status are derived from these booleans. This produces attestable-looking but meaningless compliance scores; the docstrings themselves admit 'simplified verification'. Treat as a stub masquerading as a compliance check.

findings.title: SPIFFE authenticate_service_with_svid assigns fixed trust_score 0.95 and empty signing_key without validating SVID validity window
findings.detail: authenticate_service_with_svid decodes the SVID then builds AuthenticatedServiceContext with a hardcoded trust_score=0.95 and signing_key='' (l.200-210), using decoded.not_after as expires_at but performing no check that not_before <= now <= not_after before treating the service as authenticated. Per the SPIFFE invariant the certificate validity window is surfaced precisely so the gateway can enforce it; here the window is copied into the context but never enforced, so an expired or not-yet-valid SVID still yields a 0.95-trust authenticated service context.

findings.title: authenticate_user_with_presentation swallows all token-parse failures and returns a trusted placeholder identity (fail-open)
findings.detail: The whole VP-token handling is wrapped in `try/except Exception` (lines 116-154). On ANY exception (malformed/forged/unsigned token, missing claims, provider error) it logs a warning and falls through to build an AuthenticatedUserContext with user_did="did:example:user123", verified_claims defaulted to {"sub":"user123",...}, and trust_score=0.85 (lines 156-179), then returns context.to_dict(). Worse, the token is only ever read via self._codec.peek_claims_from_wire (line 117) which the catalog states is UNVERIFIED and must NEVER feed a trust decision — there is no signature verification anywhere on this path. An attacker supplies any bytes and receives a successful authenticated context with trust 0.85. This violates the cross-cutting fail-closed invariant (default must be raise/deny) and the JWT peek-vs-verify discipline.

findings.title: External OAuth token authentication trusts unverified peek_claims and fails open to placeholder user on parse error
findings.detail: authenticate_external_oauth_token derives user_did and verified_claims from self._codec.peek_claims(oauth_token) (lines 234-247), which is the UNVERIFIED diagnostic read per the catalog. On exception it builds verified_claims={"sub":f"user123@{provider}",...} and proceeds (lines 253-261). OIDC verification is only attempted `if self._oidc_provider:` and its own failure is caught and ignored (lines 263-279, "using decoded claims"). So when no oidc_provider is injected, or its verify_token raises, the method returns an authenticated context (trust_score 0.60) built entirely from unverified token claims. No signature is ever enforced — auth bypass.

findings.title: SecurityOrchestrator.evaluate_complete_security_context is a stub that always returns authorized/authenticated true
findings.detail: Every private stage is a hardcoded stub: _authenticate returns {"authenticated":True} (245), _verify_identity echoes it (253), _resolve_attributes returns {} (261), _evaluate_trust returns 1.0 (270), _evaluate_authorization returns {"authorized":True,"decision_id":"default"} (281), _handle_obligations returns [] (289), _audit_decision is pass (298). The public evaluate_complete_security_context therefore reports authenticated=True and authorized=<decision>.get('authorized',False)=True for ANY input. evaluate_authorization_only additionally hardcodes trust_score=1.0 (line 212). This concrete satisfies the ISecurityOrchestrator ABC by signature but performs no real authentication/authorization — a complete auth bypass if any consumer wires this orchestrator.

findings.title: ContextCarrierImpl.decode_from_transport returns a forged trusted context (signature_valid:True) when no propagator is configured
findings.detail: When self._context_propagator is None, decode_from_transport ignores the inbound headers entirely and returns a hardcoded dict with principal_id="user123", trust_score=0.85, scopes=["read","write"], device_posture compliant, and crucially "signature_valid": True (lines 89-98). The docstring promises signature verification and tamper protection; instead the unconfigured path fabricates a fully-trusted, signature-validated context regardless of what the caller sent. Any consumer that gates on signature_valid is bypassed. encode_for_transport similarly emits placeholder "X-Context-Signature":"sha256:abc123..." (line 59) and attach_context_to_request emits "sha256:placeholder" (line 134) — unsigned context presented as signed.

findings.title: VerifyIdentityImpl returns verified:True / is_trusted:True placeholders when no verifier is injected
findings.detail: Across nearly every method, when self._identity_verifier / self._identity_provider is None the impl returns an affirmative placeholder rather than failing closed: verify_identity -> {"verified":True,"trust_level":"high"} (78-83); verify_credential -> {"verified":True,...} (241-247); check_credential_trust -> {"is_trusted":True,"trust_level":"high","reason":"Issuer is in trusted root chain"} (345-350); get_trusted_issuers -> a fixed set of did:example issuers (306-309). This violates the fail-closed invariant: an unconfigured identity gateway must deny, not assert trust.

findings.title: Coordinator/verifier read 'is_valid' but the gateway emits 'verified' — verification result silently lost
findings.detail: IdentityVerifier.verify_did consumes the gateway result via verification_result.get("is_valid", False) (line 94) and SecurityCoordinator.verify_identity does identity_result.get("is_valid", False) (security_coordinator.py:335). But VerifyIdentityImpl.verify_identity returns the key "verified" (verify_identity_impl.py:80), and when an external _identity_verifier is wired its return shape is whatever that service yields — neither path is guaranteed to contain "is_valid". The real verification verdict is dropped on the floor and the consumers default to False (here fail-closed) — but combined with VerifyIdentityImpl's verified:True placeholder this is a fragile contract mismatch: the data attribute carrying the trust verdict is not the one read. A reviewer cannot confirm signature/validity ever gates the decision.

findings.title: SecurityCoordinator.authenticate_user keys on auth_result['success'] which the gateway never sets
findings.detail: authenticate_user branches on `if not auth_result.get("success")` (line 192) and later reads auth_result.get("method") and auth_result.get("session_id"). But the gateway returns AuthenticatedUserContext.to_dict() whose keys are user_did/authentication_method/verified_claims/trust_score/session_id/... with NO "success" and NO "method" key (security_interfaces .../authenticated_user_context.py:52-64). So success is always None/falsy and authenticate_user ALWAYS returns CoordinatorAuthenticationResult(success=False). The success path (trust scoring, success audit) is dead code. Fails closed but the contract between coordinator and gateway is broken — the coordinator can never authenticate anyone.

findings.title: authorize_access sets required_threshold = trust_score, so the trust gate can never deny
findings.detail: In authorize_access the result is built with trust_score_used=trust_assessment.score and required_threshold=trust_assessment.score (lines 316-317) — the threshold is set equal to the score it is compared against, so any downstream trust-threshold check (score >= threshold) is trivially satisfied for every request. The trust assessment itself is computed from initial_score=0.5 plus _empty_signals() (lines 279-284), i.e. it never reflects real per-request risk. The trust dimension of this Zero-Trust authorize path is inert; only the PDP decision matters, and the surfaced threshold gives a false impression of a trust gate.

findings.title: evaluate_external_oauth: OIDC verify_token result trusted via hasattr/duck-typing into raw dict claims
findings.detail: After OIDC verification the code does `if hasattr(token_result, "claims"): verified_claims = token_result.claims` (lines 270-271) and rebuilds user_did from verified_claims.get('sub','user123'). It trusts any object exposing a .claims attribute and treats it as a verified-claims dict; there is no typed IValidatedTokenClaims check and no inspection of a verification-success flag. A provider returning a truthy-but-failed result object with a .claims attribute would be accepted. Weakened, structure-sniffing validation in a trust path.

findings.title: Concrete authentication/identity/trust paths operate on Dict[str,Any] wire dicts instead of typed opaque IDs/claims
findings.detail: The orchestration layer is specified as orchestration-only over typed inputs, but these concretes thread untyped Dict[str,Any] trust data throughout: AuthenticationGatewayImpl.authenticate_user_with_presentation takes request_context: Dict[str,Any] and returns Dict[str,Any] (lines 101-108,179); TrustEvaluator.get_current_trust_score/update_trust_score/flag_anomaly take principal/context/decision as Dict[str,Any] and key trust off principal.get('id')/('did') string sniffing (trust_evaluator.py:170-264, 195); AuthorizationContext stores principal/resource/action as Dict[str,Any] and mints PrincipalID()/ResourceID() fresh when the dict lacks an opaque ID (authorization_context.py:28-39). Wire dicts carrying principal/trust data inside the trust core (not at a from_wire boundary) is the forgeable-input pattern the opaque-ID/VerifiedTrustContext discipline exists to prevent; only IAuthorizationRequestBuilder is sanctioned to convert wire dicts.

findings.title: TrustEvaluator default-maps unknown auth methods/anomalies to weaker-but-accepted values
findings.detail: _auth_method falls back to AuthenticationMethod.PASSWORD on any unrecognised string (line 72); _sensitivity falls back to ResourceSensitivity.APPLICATION (line 128); _anomaly_type maps unknown strings to UNUSUAL_REQUEST (line 139). These silently coerce unknown/typo'd inputs into a default classification rather than rejecting, so a caller passing an unrecognised method string is scored as PASSWORD (0.60) and an unrecognised sensitivity is treated as APPLICATION (threshold 0.6) instead of failing closed to the most restrictive value. Defaulting in a trust/risk classifier should bias to most-restrictive, not mid.

findings.title: SecurityEventBus and AuditEventReporterImpl swallow audit failures (print + local-logger fallback)
findings.detail: SecurityEventBus._invoke_handler catches all handler exceptions and merely `print(f"Error in event handler: {e}")` (line 390) — audit/monitoring handler failures are silently dropped to stdout, not surfaced. AuditEventReporterImpl returns _LOCAL_LOGGER_FALLBACK={"recorded":True,...} whenever the aggregator is unconfigured (audit_event_reporter_impl.py:14,57,79,113,...), reporting success while the event only hit a local logger. Audit is a compliance control; reporting recorded:True without durable recording undermines the complete-audit-trail invariant.

findings.title: SPIFFE/credential providers fabricate trust-bearing placeholders when collaborator unconfigured
findings.detail: CredentialIssuerImpl.verify_credential returns {"valid":True,"issuer_did":"did:example:issuer"} when no _credential_issuer is injected (lines 92-96), and get_trusted_issuers/suspend/resume/refresh all return affirmative placeholder dicts (150-244). This is inconsistent with the sibling SPIFFEIdentityProviderImpl which correctly raises RuntimeError('SPIFFEProvider not configured') on every unconfigured path (spiffe_identity_provider_impl.py:63,87,...). The credential verifier asserting valid:True with a placeholder issuer when nothing is wired is fail-open and contradicts the fail-closed invariant; it should raise like the SPIFFE provider does.

findings.title: AuthenticationContext.is_authenticated initialized True and only ever invalidated by clock — no signature/session check
findings.detail: The context sets self._is_authenticated = True in __init__ (line 61) unconditionally, and is_authenticated only flips to False on expiry (lines 73-81). Construction implies authenticated; there is no verification that the supplied principal/verified_claims were actually cryptographically verified, and request_reauthentication/request_mfa both raise RuntimeError (lines 136-139,161-164) so step-up is impossible. A consumer constructing this object from unverified data gets is_authenticated==True until the timer expires.

findings.title: SecurityOrchestrator authorizes and authenticates everything by default (fail-OPEN stub)
findings.detail: Every internal stage of the orchestrator is a hardcoded permissive stub: _authenticate() returns {"authenticated": True} (line 245), _verify_identity() echoes it back (253), _evaluate_trust() returns 1.0 (270), and _evaluate_authorization() returns {"authorized": True, "decision_id": "default"} (281). evaluate_complete_security_context() (line 42) therefore returns authenticated=True / authorized=True / trust_score=1.0 for ANY input, never consulting any credential, gateway, PDP, or trust calculator. evaluate_authorization_only() further hardcodes trust_score=1.0 (line 212). This is a complete authentication+authorization bypass for any caller using ISecurityOrchestrator. Violates the catalog 'Default everywhere is DENY/raise' invariant.

findings.title: authenticate_user_with_presentation trusts UNVERIFIED VP-token claims as the authenticated identity
findings.detail: The VP-token path calls self._codec.peek_claims_from_wire(vp_token) (line 117) — which the catalog marks as UNVERIFIED, diagnostic-read-only, 'must NEVER feed a trust decision'. It then derives user_did from the token's own subject/issuer (lines 132/146/151), builds verified_claims from the raw decoded payload (133/147/152), and assigns trust_score 0.85-0.90. The provider .authenticate() result is only checked for hasattr 'principal_identity' (line 376) and its actual verified identity is discarded — even on the EUID/DID branches the returned identity is taken from the unverified peek, not the provider. There is no signature, nonce, audience, or expiry verification anywhere. An attacker can forge any subject/issuer in an unsigned VP token and be 'authenticated'.

findings.title: authenticate_external_oauth_token falls back to unverified token claims when OIDC verification fails (fail-open)
findings.detail: verified_claims/user_did are first populated from self._codec.peek_claims(oauth_token) (unverified, lines 234-247). The only real verification is the optional self._oidc_provider.verify_token() call (265), but its result is only used if hasattr(token_result,'claims') (270) and any exception is swallowed with a warning that explicitly says 'using decoded claims' (276-279). So if the OIDC provider is absent, or raises (network error, bad signature mapped to an exception, expired), the method still returns a successful AuthenticatedUserContext (trust_score 0.60) built from the forged/unverified token. Signature/issuer/audience/expiry are never enforced on this path. Authentication bypass via an unsigned bearer token.

findings.title: authenticate_service_with_svid does not enforce the SVID validity window
findings.detail: After decode_svid_certificate (line 193) the gateway immediately builds an AuthenticatedServiceContext with trust_score=0.95 and uses decoded.not_after merely as the session expiry (line 204). It never compares now against decoded.not_before/not_after, so an expired or not-yet-valid SVID is accepted as a fully-trusted service. The catalog explicitly requires consumers to 'enforce' the not_before/not_after window surfaced on ISPIFFEDecodedSVID; this consumer does not. Compounded by the decoder defaulting not_before=now / not_after=now+1day when the provider omits them (spiffe_svid_decoder.py lines 63-69), a defaulted window is treated as authoritative validity.

findings.title: VerifyIdentityImpl returns hardcoded 'verified: True / trust_level: high' when no verifier is configured (fail-open default)
findings.detail: verify_identity() (line 55): when self._identity_verifier is None it logs a warning and returns {'verified': True, 'trust_level': 'high', ...} (lines 77-83). Likewise check_credential_trust() returns is_trusted=True/'high' (345-350), verify_credential() returns verified=True (241-247), and get_trusted_issuers() returns a fixed example set (306-309). An unconfigured (or partially-configured) gateway asserts every DID/credential/issuer is trusted instead of failing closed. Violates the deny-by-default invariant for identity/credential trust.

findings.title: IdentityVerifier reads 'is_valid' but the gateway emits 'verified' — verification result is dropped, defaults to invalid AND caches it
findings.detail: verify_did() reads verification_result.get('is_valid', False) (line 94), but the IIdentityGateway implementation (VerifyIdentityImpl.verify_identity) returns the key 'verified', never 'is_valid'. So is_valid silently defaults to False on every call (a key-name contract mismatch) and the wrong/absent value is then cached for cache_ttl (lines 97-109). is_revoked/is_compromised/public_keys are read from keys the gateway never returns either. The verification decision is effectively decoupled from the gateway response — a dead wire that makes the verifier's output independent of the actual identity check.

findings.title: SecurityCoordinator.authenticate_user keys success off a 'success' field the gateway never produces
findings.detail: authenticate_user() branches on auth_result.get('success') (line 192), but AuthenticatedUserContext.to_dict() (authenticated_user_context.py lines 52-64) contains no 'success' key. So the success branch is never taken — the coordinator always records a FAILURE audit event and returns CoordinatorAuthenticationResult(success=False) regardless of the gateway outcome. While this defaults closed (no auth bypass), it is a broken contract that makes the entire authenticate_user flow a dead path, and the downstream trust scoring/audit-success code (lines 217-253) is unreachable. The gateway-result contract and the coordinator are not wired together.

findings.title: refresh_principal_context / authorize_access feed a fixed empty signal bundle so trust deltas are always zero
findings.detail: authorize_access() (line 273) and refresh_principal_context() (line 368) both pass _empty_signals() (NORMAL/UNKNOWN/UNKNOWN, lines 115-128) into evaluate_request_context_signals, and authorize_access hardcodes initial_score=0.5 (line 283). The trust calculator can therefore only ever return the unmodified base score — geographic/time/device/behavior risk adjustments (trust_calculator.py lines 102-123) are never exercised for these flows even when real signals exist on the request. The computed trust_assessment is then reported as both trust_score_used and required_threshold (lines 316-317), so the threshold check is tautological (used == required). Trust-based gating is a no-op here.

findings.title: SecurityGatewayFacade health-check and TrustEvaluator cache key build on forgeable principal dicts
findings.detail: TrustEvaluator caches trust scores under cache_key = f"{principal_key}:{hash(frozenset(context.items()))}" where principal_key is principal.get('id')/('did') or the literal 'unknown' (lines 195-196). Two distinct unauthenticated/identity-less principals both collapse to the 'unknown' bucket and share cached trust scores across requests for cache_ttl. Combined with TrustEvaluator deriving auth method and trust purely from caller-supplied dict fields (principal.get('authentication_method'), _auth_method defaulting to PASSWORD on bad input, lines 64-72), the trust score is computed from forgeable request-body-shaped input rather than an authority-resolved context, contrary to the catalog's 'request body is never proof' principle.

findings.title: Stub credential/SPIFFE adapters return success placeholders when backend provider is absent
findings.detail: CredentialIssuerImpl.verify_credential() returns {'valid': True, 'verified_claims': {}, 'issuer_did': 'did:example:issuer'} when self._credential_issuer is None (lines 92-96); get_trusted_issuers() (230-244), suspend/resume/refresh likewise return hardcoded success placeholders. A caller invoking credential verification against an unconfigured issuer is told the credential is valid. Unlike the SPIFFEIdentityProviderImpl methods (which correctly raise RuntimeError when unconfigured, e.g. line 63), these credential-trust paths fail open. Default must be deny/raise, not 'valid: True'.

findings.title: AuditEvent.event_id default is a single shared UUID evaluated once at class-definition time
findings.detail: `event_id: IEventID = EventID()` on a frozen @dataclass evaluates EventID() exactly once when the class body is defined. EventID() generates a fresh uuid4 per call (core_data_structures/value_objects/base_id.py line 63: object.__setattr__(self, '_BaseID__id', uuid4())), so every AuditEvent constructed without an explicit event_id shares ONE identical event ID for the lifetime of the process. For a security audit record this collapses per-event identity: distinct authentication/authorization/privilege-escalation events become indistinguishable by ID, breaking correlation, dedup and tamper-evidence. The AuditEvent.now() factory happens to pass an explicit EventID(), but direct construction (the dataclass's public constructor, usable by any downstream app) silently inherits the shared default. Should be field(default_factory=EventID).

findings.title: SecurityContext is a mutable (non-frozen) trust-carrying context — trust_level/risk_score can be flipped post-construction
findings.detail: @dataclass (not frozen) on SecurityContext, whose fields include trust_level, risk_score, authentication_level and authentication_methods. is_high_assurance() (line 64) derives a high-assurance grant from authentication_level=='high', the trust assessment, and risk_score<0.3. Because the object is mutable, any code holding a reference after construction can set ctx.authentication_level='high' / ctx.risk_score=0.0 / mutate authentication_methods to elevate a trust decision the authenticating layer never granted. It is constructed in layer_0_primitives/authentication/oauth/o_auth21_provider.py line 828 and flows into downstream checks. Trust/security contexts must be frozen=True so trust inputs cannot be fabricated after the authority resolved them. Also authentication_time default uses datetime.utcnow() (naive, no tz) at line 41.

findings.title: ProvenanceVerificationResult signature/cert/integrity sub-flags default to True (fail-open verification result)
findings.detail: signature_valid: bool = True, certificate_chain_valid: bool = True, content_integrity_valid: bool = True, temporal_consistency_valid: bool = True all default to the trusting value (lines 29-32). is_fully_verified() (line 53) AND-combines these, so a result built with only verification_successful=True is reported as fully verified even though no signature, certificate-chain or content-integrity check ever ran. A verification *result* object's security predicates must default to the deny/unverified value (False) so an incompletely-populated result fails closed, not open.

findings.title: SessionVerificationResult validity sub-flags default True and __post_init__ never runs (not a dataclass)
findings.detail: SessionVerificationResult is a plain class (no @dataclass / not Pydantic) yet declares fields as bare class-body annotations with security-critical defaults: session_valid=True, session_exists=True, session_active=True, session_not_expired=True, permissions_valid=True, concurrent_limits_valid=True, security_context_valid=True (lines 24-32). is_fully_valid() (line 62) returns True purely from these class-level defaults. The defined __post_init__ (line 50) is never invoked because there is no @dataclass, so it provides no protection, and the required fields (request_id, validated_at, verification_successful) have no constructor storage. Net effect: any session-verification result defaults to 'valid' — fail-open — and the class is structurally unconstructable as written.

findings.title: Credential/provenance 'Domain Types' classes are not dataclasses — required fields unconstructable, __post_init__ dead, chain_valid defaults True
findings.detail: A whole family of value objects declared as plain classes implementing ABC interfaces but WITHOUT @dataclass/Pydantic, so fields are bare class annotations with no __init__ and any defined __post_init__ never fires: DigitalCredential (this file), EUIDCredential (credentials/euid_credential.py), ContentAuthenticity (provenance/content_authenticity.py), ProvenanceAssertion + its __post_init__ data-non-empty check (provenance/provenance_assertion.py line 40), ProvenanceActor (provenance/provenance_actor.py line 32), AuditEventMetadata (audit/audit_event_metadata.py line 41), and DelegationChain (layer_0_primitives/common/delegation/delegation_chain.py). Confirmed IDigitalCredential/IEUIDCredential are plain abc.ABC, not dataclasses. None are instantiated anywhere in the security component (dead code today) which is why this is low, but they are latent traps: e.g. DelegationChain.chain_valid: bool = True defaults a broken chain to intact (fail-open), and the credential is_valid_at_time/is_revoked predicates would read non-existent instance attributes. If wired, every one is broken or fail-open.

findings.title: PublicKeyPEM accepts header/footer of mismatched key types and only substring-matches the footer
findings.detail: __post_init__ validates that the content startswith ANY recognised BEGIN header (line 37) and that ANY recognised END footer string appears anywhere in the body via `f in stripped` (line 42). It does not require the BEGIN and END types to correspond, and the footer check is a substring containment rather than an end-anchored match, so e.g. a body starting '-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----' but ending '-----END EC PUBLIC KEY-----', or junk after the footer, passes construction. The class docstring scopes parsing to a downstream crypto decoder, so this is format-hygiene only, but the catalog credits IPublicKeyPEM with validating PEM shape at construction; the validation is weaker than implied.

findings.title: Proof.algorithm and ProofFactory algorithm are unconstrained free strings, not a closed enum
findings.detail: Proof._algorithm is a bare str validated only as non-empty (__post_init__ line 34); ProofFactory.build (common/primitives/proof_factory.py line 35) takes algorithm: str and passes it through unvalidated. The catalog's KMS/signing invariant is that algorithms are a closed enum so callers cannot pass arbitrary algorithm strings. ProofFactory correctly computes its own sha256 digest from payload (good, prevents digest spoofing) and domain code does not inspect the algorithm, so impact is bounded to boundary projectors trusting an arbitrary JOSE-style label; still a typed-enum gap relative to the KMSAlgorithm discipline elsewhere.

findings.title: SSOConfigurationRepository.save_certificate_store is a silent no-op and get_certificate_store always returns None
findings.detail: `save_certificate_store` (line 341-344) is `pass` and `get_certificate_store` (line 347-352) unconditionally `return None`, both overriding abstract methods of ISSOConfigurationRepository (isso_configuration_repository.py:158-166). The SSOCertificateStore holds the IdP signing certificates used downstream to validate SAML assertion signatures. Persisted certs are silently dropped; every retrieval returns None (fail-open: a None store means no current signing cert, which per the catalog disables signature verification when signing_certificate_pem==''). No production caller wires these yet so blast radius is latent, but this is a security-critical interface method implemented as a no-op with a fail-open return rather than raising or persisting.

findings.title: Agent audit-log write swallows all exceptions, silently dropping the security audit trail
findings.detail: `_log_audit_event` (lines 767-782) wraps the audit-row insert + commit in `try/except Exception: pass` with the comment 'Don't fail the main operation if audit logging fails'. Agent register/deactivate/reactivate operations therefore succeed even when their security audit record is never written (e.g. CHECK-constraint violation on valid_operation, or a DB error), leaving no tamper-evident record of an identity-lifecycle change. For a security audit trail, an audit-write failure should be surfaced, not silently discarded.

findings.title: CredentialRepository.is_revoked returns False for a missing/unknown credential (fail-open default)
findings.detail: `is_revoked` (lines 255-271) maps a missing row (scalar_one_or_none() -> None at line 268-270) to `return False` ('not revoked'). A caller using this method as a revocation gate would treat an unknown / mistyped / not-yet-stored credential id as non-revoked rather than failing closed. The interface contract (icredential_repository.py:113-119) only specifies the REVOKED case and is silent on missing rows, so a fail-closed default would be safer for a security gate; no in-scope consumer is shown trusting it today, hence low.

findings.title: Read-path repository methods catch broad Exception and degrade to None/empty/zero
findings.detail: `get_agent_identity` (line 301-302), `resolve_did` (line 335-336), `list_agents` (line 408-409), `count_agents` (line 454-455) and `get_statistics` (line 618-624) each catch bare `except Exception` and return None / [] / 0 / zeroed stats. This masks DB/deserialization errors as 'not found', so an error during identity/DID resolution is indistinguishable from a genuinely absent identity. Not a direct trust-grant bypass (absence denies), but it erases diagnostic signal on a security-relevant resolution path and could hide partial-data corruption in _json_to_did_document/_json_to_credential.
